+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 80.0
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the time Midas found Chewy, the world had already finished falling apart in the practical way, which was to say that the lights were out, the roads were cracked open like old bread, and most of civilization had turned into a collection of useful debris surrounding a few stubborn people. Chewy, however, had survived the Long Collapse with an indignity that suggested either fate or manufacturing pride. He was an animo-tronic stuffed koala of ancient vintage, with matted grey fur, one ear permanently folded, and eyes made of glass that were not so much bright as faintly judgmental. He sat on a crate outside the half-collapsed service station they called home base, paws in front of him, and emitted a soft mechanical wheeze that sounded like a tiny pensioner clearing his throat. Midas sat opposite him on an overturned plastic drum and stared at the map spread between them. It was not a good map. Good maps had roads on them. Good maps had labels. Good maps had not been printed on the back of a cereal box and annotated with charcoal, grease, and the phrase “probably full of rats” written in three different places. “We need,” said Midas, tapping a finger at the map, “a generator.” Chewy’s left eye clicked once. “Hug me, and receive your future.” Midas nodded as though this cleared things up. “Yes. Exactly. A generator. The one in the old clinic. If it still exists. Which is unlikely, but less unlikely than sitting here dying of boredom.” Chewy’s mouth opened in a small stitched smile. “New! Improved! Chewy-brand Family Value!” “That means nothing,” said Midas. Chewy’s ears twitched. “It means everything. Also, you have a smear of something on your cheek.” Midas rubbed at it. “Mud.” “Possibly.” The mission, in principle, was simple. The old clinic had once had a backup generator in the basement. The clinic itself was thirty-odd blocks away, across a district that had become a district only in the sense that all ruined things were eventually given names so people could argue about them more politely. If they found the generator, they would bring it back, restore power to home base, and thereby improve the quality of life for everyone involved by at least one light bulb. Midas folded the map with the care of a man handling a snake. “We go in, we get it, we come back. No heroics.” Chewy’s paws lifted a fraction. “No worries, no fuss, no fluster.” A pause. “Was that a slogan? It feels like a slogan.” “It was probably a lie,” said Midas. “But it’ll do.” They set out after breakfast, which had been boiled grain and a pear of uncertain age. Chewy rode in a shoulder sling made from a seatbelt and a pair of ripped curtains, because the world may have ended but practical tailoring had not. He looked, in profile, like a plush omen. The first problem was the bridge. The second problem was that Midas had forgotten bridges were only half there now. He discovered this while crossing it. There is a special sort of sound a person makes when the universe suddenly remembers gravity. Midas made that sound and disappeared from the remaining span in a flurry of dust and curses. Chewy, being attached by strap and habit, went with him. They landed in a drain full of rainwater, scrap metal, and one very offended bird. Midas groaned. “I hate bridges.” Chewy blinked out rain with a tiny servo-whirr. “At least you landed in a favorable position.” “I’m upside down.” “Exactly.” They clambered out, more or less intact except for Midas’s pride and a small tear in Chewy’s ear, and continued by taking the route no one sensible would take: through the Market of Many Bad Decisions. It had once been a shopping street. Then it had been a scavenger bazaar. Then it had become a place where people sold knives, soup, and rumors with equal conviction. A woman at the first stall watched them pass and said, “You’re either brave or stupid.” Midas said, “I’ve been told both.” Chewy turned his head toward her and emitted, with grave cheerfulness: “You deserve a holiday.” She frowned. “What?” “We all do,” said Midas quickly, and hurried on. Past the market, the road narrowed between buildings that leaned together as if sharing gossip. Somewhere overhead, things clattered with the busy certainty of roofs deciding to become lower floors. They crossed a square where old statues had been repurposed by birds into civic monuments. They passed a child with a catapult who offered to charge them for directions. “Don’t know where I’m going,” said Midas. “Then you’re overqualified,” said the child, and asked for a biscuit. By noon they reached the clinic. It was still there, which in the current era counted as a form of optimism. The front windows were boarded. The sign hung by one chain and described itself as a health care facility to anyone who could read and was in desperate need of one. Ivy had invaded with a triumph that suggested it had long resented architecture. Midas pushed the door. It did not open. He pushed harder. “Of course.” Chewy patted his shoulder. “Relentless? You bet.” “That one was definitely advertising.” A side window had been smashed long ago by persons unknown and presumably regretted by no one. Midas climbed through, landed in a corridor that smelled of mildew, old disinfectant, and a memory of fear, and helped Chewy through after him. Inside, the clinic was a museum of the pre-collapse habit of assuming tomorrow would resemble yesterday. There were posters about washing hands, which now seemed an oddly aristocratic aspiration. There were wheelchairs turned into nests. There were files piled in corners like old guilt. And there, at the end of the corridor, was the basement door. It was locked. Midas stared at it with the deep, tender hatred of a man encountering the same problem in a new room. “Please tell me you have a trick.” Chewy stared back. “I am stuffed.” “That’s not what I meant.” Chewy’s paws twitched. “Here comes the fun!” The lock, it turned out, was less a lock and more a suggestion made of rust. Midas gave it a determined kick. The door burst inward with such enthusiasm that he fell down the stairs behind it. The generator room was dark, except for one small window high above and a faint green glow from something on the far wall that Midas sincerely hoped was not alive. It was the generator. The generator was also on fire. “Midas,” said Chewy solemnly from the top of the stairs, “that seems suboptimal.” Then the floor trembled. At first Midas thought this was another old building settling into its sins. Then came the barking. Not one dog. Several. And not the friendly sort. They had come, as all the worst things do, because someone else had already found the generator and left behind a trail of noise. The basement back door—previously invisible—burst open and in came three scavengers in patchwork armor and bad moods, followed by two dogs the size of small furniture. One of the scavengers spotted Midas and shouted, “Oi! That’s ours!” Midas looked from them to the burning generator and said, “You can have it back!” The first dog leapt. Midas dodged. The dog hit the generator. The generator, which had already been having a disagreeable afternoon, exploded in sparks and smoke. Chewy, who had no instinct for self-preservation but enormous instinct for commentary, cried: “Now with extra power!” This may have been the slogan for a battery or a dessert. It did not matter. The basement lights flickered. One dog yelped and bolted. A scavenger slipped on coolant, collided with a filing cabinet, and was buried under thirty years of medical records. Midas grabbed Chewy, ducked a flying wrench, and ran for the stairs as the room filled with the smell of hot copper and regret. They reached the corridor just as the clinic alarm, apparently powered by spite, began to shriek. “What now?” yelled Midas. Chewy pointed with a paw. “Exit!” The front doors, however, had been forced open by the scavengers’ friends, who were now very angry and even less organized. Midas made a quick decision, which was an excellent way to describe an act of desperation pretending to be strategy. He chose the roof. In the end, it is often the roof. The roof is where bad plans go when they want to be dramatic. They climbed a staircase barely holding together, burst through a hatch, and found themselves on a patchwork rooftop overgrown with moss and the beginnings of a small tree. Smoke rose from the clinic windows below. Somewhere behind them, someone was shouting about a trap, a theft, and an unpaid debt, which was unfair because Midas had not had time to steal anything except the generator’s dignity. Across the street, the old laundromat roof sat lower and almost inviting. Midas judged the gap. “No heroics,” said Chewy. “Agreed.” Midas jumped anyway. He made it, but only technically. The landing was shoulder-first, and he slid down a sloping sheet-metal roof, through a gap in a sign, and into a stack of old towels in the laundromat’s upper room. Chewy followed by bouncing off Midas’s back, through the same gap, and into a laundry basket. When Midas emerged, bruised and covered in lint, the laundromat’s back door was open. In the alley beyond stood a cart piled high with scrap and, more importantly, an old bicycle with one flat tire. He looked at it. It looked at him. There was a deep, mutual lack of confidence. “Home?” he asked. Chewy’s eyes clicked. “Home is where the heart is.” “That’s nice.” “Also where the snacks are.” Midas sighed, strapped Chewy into the cart, and began the slow, humiliating trek back to base. They did not get far before the scavengers found them again, because the universe enjoys a tidy reprise. A brief chase followed through alleys, over a fence, through a market stall full of onions, and into a puddle that turned out to be much deeper than it looked. At one point Midas pushed the cart. At another, the cart pushed him. At a third, the bicycle was no longer attached to any philosophy of motion and simply fell over. By the time they reached home base, dusk had arrived with the smug grace of someone who had been right all along. The generator had not survived. But Midas had returned with the clinic’s brass plaque, which he claimed would be “useful for parts,” and Chewy had acquired a scarf made from a curtain and the kind of expression that suggested he’d been built to witness history and disapprove of it. A small crowd gathered as they stumbled through the gate. “Well?” called someone. Midas dropped the plaque, sat down in the dust, and said, “We have not improved our lighting situation.” Chewy lifted one paw and, with great seriousness, emitted: “Don’t be afraid of the dark.” Everyone looked at him. Then Midas laughed, because if you didn’t laugh, you’d have to reckon with the fact that the day had involved a bridge, a burning basement, two dogs, a bicycle, and a generator that had tried to murder them before dying. He leaned back against the wall of home base, scratched at his bruised shoulder, and looked at Chewy. “Tomorrow,” he said, “we’re sending someone else.” Chewy’s eyes gleamed in the twilight. “Tomorrow,” he said, with the finality of a sacred promise and the warmth of a pre-collapse supermarket, “we save money.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, observational humor, and philosophical undertones wrapped in absurdist situations. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic buddy comedy that feels authentically Pratchettesque while maintaining narrative coherence throughout its episodic structure. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose competence is perpetually undermined by circumstances, while Chewy's malfunctioning animatronic responses provide both humor and unexpected wisdom. The dynamic between them feels genuine, with Chewy's random corporate slogans serving as a clever commentary device that mirrors Pratchett's use of footnotes and asides. The prose style successfully emulates Pratchett's voice through its use of understated observations ("the world had already finished falling apart in the practical way"), dry commentary on human nature, and the kind of philosophical asides that made Pratchett's work distinctive. Phrases like "manufacturing pride" and "the universe enjoys a tidy reprise" capture his particular blend of whimsy and cynicism. The mission structure follows the prompt perfectly - deliberation, inevitable complications, and return home worse for wear. The escalating series of mishaps (bridge collapse, burning generator, scavenger chase) maintains momentum while each setback feels organic rather than forced. The pacing is well-controlled, building from small inconveniences to larger catastrophes. However, some elements feel slightly derivative rather than truly original. While the Pratchett pastiche is skillful, it occasionally relies too heavily on familiar patterns rather than developing its own voice. The dialogue, while functional, lacks the razor-sharp wit that made Pratchett's conversations memorable. Some descriptive passages, while atmospheric, don't quite achieve the effortless elegance of the master's work. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - they fail in their mission but maintain their partnership and humor, which feels earned rather than artificially upbeat. The final exchange about saving money provides a satisfying callback to Chewy's corporate programming while suggesting their adventures will continue. The world-building is economical but effective, sketching a believable post-collapse society through telling details like the Market of Many Bad Decisions and repurposed civic monuments. The imagery consistently supports both the setting and tone without becoming overwrought. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 83.4
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the time Ewan Vale ducked through the little bell-chiming door, the lane outside had become a churn of shouted names and camera flashes. He shut the door behind him with the quick, theatrical precision of a man who’d shut a thousand doors for cameras. The bell above it gave one bright, ridiculous jingle. Inside, the bookstore was all warm lamplight, old paper, and the dry, comforting smell of dust and rain-soaked wool. A narrow place, crooked with age, its shelves packed to the ceiling. A hand-lettered sign by the till read: **NO PHONES NEAR THE FIRST EDITION TABLE, PLEASE. I WILL CRY.** Behind the counter, the owner looked up from a stack of paperbacks. They wore a cardigan the color of storm clouds and the expression of someone who had just discovered a fox in the poultry shed and was deciding whether to be annoyed or impressed. “You’re either very lost,” they said, “or very expensive.” Ewan pressed a hand to his chest, gasping in exaggerated offense. “Is that how you greet all frightened men in need?” “I greet frightened men in need with a minimum of fuss,” they said. “You’re in the wrong shop if you’re looking for fuss. That’s next door. They sell wool and panic.” A shouting voice outside, then the stutter of camera shutters. Ewan glanced toward the window. “I’d be grateful if, for the next ten minutes, this establishment could become a place of sanctuary.” “Ten minutes?” They shut the book they’d been holding and finally stood. “You must be new to Wales.” He turned back with a grin already in place, the kind that usually made entire rooms soften around him. “Ewan Vale.” They blinked once. “I know.” That should have pleased him. It usually did. Instead, the answer landed with the irritating weight of something that did not need to be explained. “Of course you do,” he said, lightly. “Then you know I’m harmless.” “I know you’re loud,” they said. He put a hand over his heart again. “Cruel.” The owner came around the counter, gaze flicking once to the front windows, once to the door, as if assessing the likelihood of trouble and deciding that trouble was already here in tailored trousers. “Take off your coat if you’re staying,” they said. “You’re dripping on the floor.” He looked down. “I’m being cared for. This is new.” “It’s not care. It’s preservation of oak boards.” “Heartless.” “Correct.” He laughed then, unexpectedly delighted, because that was not the script. Not at all. Usually people knew him at once and spun out into smiles, gratitude, questions, and that half-awed eagerness that fed him like sunlight. This person—this maddening, cool-eyed person—seemed to have filed him under *inclement weather*. He removed his coat, revealing a dark jumper and the expensive kind of casual that photographers liked to pretend had happened by accident. The owner’s eyes dipped, briefly, in spite of themselves. He saw it. His mouth curved. “You looked.” “I was checking for hidden knives.” “Devastating. And did you find any?” “Not yet.” He hung the coat over a chair. “I’m Ewan.” “Yes, you said.” “I’m told it’s polite to offer a second chance.” “I’m told it’s polite not to arrive pursued by photographers and act as though the shop is your personal green room.” He spread his hands. “I’m not acting. I’m improvising.” A bang sounded from outside, a voice calling, “Ewan! Ewan, over here!” The owner rolled their eyes toward the ceiling. “Brilliant. And now you’ve brought the plague.” “Only a small, glamorous strain.” “Behind the history section,” they said. “Through there. If anyone asks, you’re a very committed customer with appalling spatial awareness.” “History section,” he repeated. “You’re just letting me hide there?” “I’m letting you stop blocking my door like a golden retriever with tax problems.” He made a faint, wounded noise. “That’s unkind.” “It’s accurate.” But he was already moving, and because he moved as if the world had rarely told him no, he paused just long enough to glance back and offer the look that usually made directors forgive him, make-up artists blush, waiters smile, and strangers feel they’d shared something intimate with him over a ten-second exchange. The owner did not smile. They simply said, “If you’re trying to charm me, Mr. Vale, I advise you save your energy.” He froze. Then, very slowly, his expression changed. The easy, practiced warmth thinned into something more focused, more alert. “And if I am?” “Then you’re wasting your time.” Outside, another burst of shouting. Closer now. Ewan leaned one shoulder against the shelf labeled **WELSH LITERATURE / POETRY / MISC. THINGS WE’RE PRETENDING ARE NOT MISC.** and studied them. “You’re immune to flattery.” “I’m a bookseller. We’re hardened by customer service and bad lighting.” “That’s not an answer.” “It’s the only one you’re getting.” He smiled again, but this one was different—smaller, genuinely intrigued. “What’s your name?” They hesitated just long enough for him to notice. That, perversely, pleased him. “Gwen,” they said at last. “Gwen,” he repeated, and there was a little reverence in it he couldn’t quite help. “Of course it is.” “Of course?” “It suits you.” “It’s a name.” “It’s a judgment.” Their mouth twitched, betraying them by a fraction. “You’re very pleased with yourself.” “I’m usually popular for it.” “I’m sure.” The owner moved to the window and nudged the blind aside with one finger. They peered out, then let it fall. “They’re still there.” “Do they ever stop?” “Not if you’re interesting enough.” He looked at them for a beat too long. “That was meant to be comforting, wasn’t it?” “No.” He laughed softly, then lowered his voice as if the books might be listening. “You really don’t care who I am.” “I know who you are,” Gwen said. “That’s different.” “Is it?” “Yes.” There was a silence then, one of those small, tense silences that can be either a wall or a doorway depending on what happens next. Ewan broke it first, because he almost always did. “So what do you do in a place like this when there aren’t famous men hiding from the press?” Gwen lifted a shoulder. “Shelve books. Order books. Chase children out of the rare editions corner. Drink tea. Read.” “And?” “And try to keep the business afloat in a town where everyone says they love books but mostly wants discounted postcards and a copy of something they saw on television.” He made a face. “That sounds tragic.” “It’s literature.” He laughed, then glanced past them to a display near the front window. A stack of books on walking routes, coastal paths, and mountain trails. “You do hiking books?” “We do actual hiking, too,” Gwen said, following his gaze. “On weekends. Or whenever the weather’s offended enough to feel dramatic.” “You hike?” “Shocking, I know. Some of us possess ankles.” He put a hand to his chest again, but this time it was not performative. It was an honest little blow. “I’ve been insulted in many forms, but never by ankles.” “I’m trying to broaden your experience.” A grin tugged at his mouth. “Careful. If you keep talking to me like this, I’ll start enjoying it.” “I’d hate to inconvenience you.” He took a step closer to the display, and because the shop was small, the movement shifted the air between them. Gwen’s attention caught on it, irritating and acute. He smelled faintly of rain and expensive soap and something that might have been cold outside world shaken off his coat. “You hike,” he said again, softer now. “Do you go alone?” “Sometimes.” “That must be peaceful.” “It is.” “Must be useful for thinking.” “It can be.” “Do you think about me when you’re walking the hills?” Gwen stared at him. Then, very deliberately, they reached for a book beside him and tapped his shoulder with it. Not hard. Just enough. “No.” He looked down at the book in their hand. “That’s A Walk Through the Cambrian Mountains.” “Yes.” “You were going to hit me with topography.” “I considered it.” A shout outside, then the sound of footsteps moving away. Ewan’s shoulders eased. “You’ve saved me.” “I haven’t,” Gwen said. “They’ll be back.” “That’s not what I meant.” He was looking at them now in a way that had abandoned the usual polished mischief. There was something hungry in it. Not cruel. Not even greedy, exactly. Just exposed. “Most people,” he said quietly, “ask for a photo.” Gwen’s expression shifted, almost imperceptibly. “I’m not most people.” “No,” he said. “You’re not.” The words hung there between the shelves. Gwen looked at the book in their hand as if it had suddenly become very interesting. “If you’re going to stand there being mysterious and handsome, I’m going to charge you by the minute.” “Am I mysterious?” “No. You’re famous.” He laughed, but it came out rougher than before. “That’s worse.” “It’s honest.” He tilted his head. “And handsome?” Now it was Gwen’s turn to look briefly, helplessly, at the floor. When they answered, their voice was drier than before. “You’re certainly made in a way that other people find handsome.” His face brightened at once, instantly and absurdly. “Other people.” “Don’t push it.” “I wouldn’t dream of it.” But he was smiling as if he absolutely would. Gwen set the book back on the shelf with care that looked suspiciously like composure. “So. If I let you stay until the mob disperses, you may browse quietly and not recite anything dramatic at my customers.” “May I ask for tea?” “You may ask.” “And if I promise to behave, may I know what you’re reading?” Gwen narrowed their eyes. “That sounded almost sincere.” “It was.” “Mm.” He took that as a victory, because he was the sort of man who needed victories fed to him in small, regular portions or he became dangerous to himself and everyone around him. Gwen crossed to the back counter and lifted a kettle. “Tea?” “Yes, please.” “Milk?” “Only if it’s good milk.” “It’s Wales. The milk is excellent.” He smiled into the shelves, then back at them. “You’re going to be the death of me, Gwen.” “Unlikely.” “I’m serious.” “So am I.” And though the words were blunt enough to pass for dismissal, neither of them believed it for a second. Outside, the voices faded into the lane. Inside, the kettle began to hum. Ewan took one step closer to the counter, and Gwen did not step away.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates the requested Welsh bookstore setting and establishes the core dynamic between a charming but ego-driven actor and a seemingly immune bookstore owner. The dialogue-heavy approach works well, with each character maintaining distinct voices throughout. The characterization is particularly effective. Ewan is portrayed as genuinely charming yet needy, his practiced charisma faltering when met with Gwen's resistance. The author captures his fragile ego through subtle moments like his disappointment when Gwen's recognition doesn't provide the usual gratification. Gwen's character is well-balanced - outwardly resistant but with glimpses of genuine attraction that feel earned rather than forced. The prose quality is high, with evocative descriptions like "the bookstore was all warm lamplight, old paper, and the dry, comforting smell of dust and rain-soaked wool." The hand-lettered sign detail ("I WILL CRY") efficiently establishes Gwen's personality. The writing maintains an appropriate tone throughout - neither too heavy nor too light for a romance scene. The dialogue crackles with wit and subtext. Lines like "You're either very lost or very expensive" and "I'm a bookseller. We're hardened by customer service and bad lighting" feel natural while revealing character. The sexual tension builds organically through verbal sparring rather than forced romantic beats. However, some elements feel slightly overwrought. Descriptions like "golden retriever with tax problems" and "expensive soap and something that might have been cold outside world shaken off his coat" occasionally tip toward preciousness. The piece also relies heavily on Ewan's performative gestures (hand to chest repeatedly) which becomes somewhat repetitive. The pacing is generally strong, though the middle section with the hiking books discussion feels slightly less essential. The ending strikes an appropriate note - suggestive of mutual attraction without rushing into unrealistic romantic declarations. The chemistry between characters feels genuine, built through conflict and wit rather than instant attraction. Both characters remain true to their established personalities while showing subtle vulnerability. The Welsh setting is well-integrated without becoming heavy-handed. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door gave under Aya’s hand with a sigh like a dying thing. For one suspended instant the underhold beyond seemed less a room than a wound in the earth: black brick sweating salt-damp, iron ribs arching overhead, the air thick with lamp-smoke and old brine and something colder beneath it, something that did not belong to any honest cellar. The underhold had always smelled faintly of coin, wet wool, and fear. Tonight it smelt of all three, sharpened by a fourth—sharper still, metallic and clean, the stink of oiled gun barrels. Aya stepped through first. The chamber beyond the threshold was larger than she had expected, a vaulted vault sunk deep beneath the guildhouse foundations, lit by a row of gas lamps that hissed and guttered in their brass cages. Their light turned the walls the colour of old teeth. Racks of confiscated weapons lined one side: cutlasses, cudgels, boarding pistols, lock-knives with pearl inlay, all laid out with almost ecclesiastical care. At the chamber’s heart, on a plinth of black stone veined with green mineral bloom, sat the Scythan artifact they had come for—an oblong relic wrapped in tarnished silver chains and marked with grooves too regular to be natural. It seemed to drink the lamplight rather than reflect it. Aya’s breath caught despite herself. Then she saw the woman standing before it. Not bound. Not bruised. Not in irons. Morska herself stood in the centre of the underhold as if the room had been built around her and not the other way about. She wore a long coat of dark teal velvet, heavy at the hem with damp, her black hair pinned back in a severe knot that made the planes of her face look carved rather than born. A pistol rested loose in one hand; a short sabre hung at her hip; and at her shoulders and behind the plinth stood four men in guild colours, broad as dock pilings and silent as grave-stones. One had a cheek stitched fresh and red. Another wore a brass knuckle on each hand. All of them had the expression of men who had already decided how many bones they were willing to break. Aya felt Katra’s hand near her elbow tense. Simeon made a small, involuntary noise in his throat. Morska smiled. “Well,” she said, her voice level and dry as old parchment. “That is disappointingly forward of you.” Aya’s mind attempted several possible exits and found none of them promising. Of course. Of course the raid had been theatre. Of course the arrest had been bait, or a bargain, or some private arrangement with the city watch and the magistrates that had not reached her. The underhold’s silence was not emptiness, but waiting. She had to keep still. Had to keep her face arranged. Morska’s gaze moved over the trio with all the leisure of a woman appraising fabric. “Katra. Simeon. And you.” Her eyes lingered on Aya with a faint, corrosive amusement. “I should have suspected you’d have the least sense among you and therefore the most ambition.” Aya had the absurd, immediate urge to laugh. The sound lodged in her throat and came out as a breath instead. “We came to see the artifact,” she said, trying for careless and hearing the strain in it at once. “Since it appears the guild is in no fit state to keep it secure.” One of the bodyguards snorted. Morska lifted a finger without looking. The sound died. “In no fit state?” she repeated. “My dear, if I were in any less fit a state, you would already be dead.” Aya’s pulse gave a hard, ugly throb. She felt, rather than saw, Katra shifting beside her—ready, always ready, to be all teeth and wire. Simeon, poor anxious Simeon, was standing too straight, his hand near the chain of his pocket watch as if the tiny brass thing could be a talisman. The watch ticked audibly in the hush: crisp, relentless, obscene. Tick. Morska looked down. “Is that thing still going?” Simeon swallowed. “Yes, ma’am.” “Infuriating.” Before Aya could warn him, one of the guards crossed the space in two strides and seized her arm. Another hand clamped over her shoulder from behind. She twisted on instinct, but the grip was iron and practised, and the second guard at her flank was already there with a truncheon at her ribs. Katra swore sharply and lunged, only to be caught by a third man hooking an arm under her throat and pinning her with brutal efficiency. Simeon stumbled back one pace and then froze when the fourth guard drew a pistol and levelled it at his chest. Aya’s stomach went cold. “Now,” Morska said, almost conversationally, “let us all acquire the minimum amount of dignity necessary to survive the next minute.” The guard holding Aya yanked her forward. She bit down on a cry as a rough hand wrenched at the clasp beneath her coat. “No—” The mask came away. For one terrible second the world seemed to expand and sharpen around the fact of her unveiled face. The cool air touched skin that had never been meant for these eyes. She heard, with sick clarity, a tiny intake of breath from somewhere behind Morska. Recognition, maybe. Or curiosity. In the underhold’s yellow light, Aya felt suddenly too young, too pale, too much daughter of the dead woman with the hanged neck and the gifted hands. Morska’s expression altered almost imperceptibly. “Well,” she said softly. “There it is.” Aya’s mouth had gone dry. She could not trust herself to speak, so she lifted her chin instead. It was an old aristocratic habit, useless here and yet stubbornly hers. Katra made a noise like a cat in a sack. “You knew.” “I suspected,” said Morska. “Your grip was too careful. Your language too polished on occasion. And your appetite for risk too expensive to be mere criminal instinct.” Her eyes glittered. “I confess, however, I did not expect the baron’s girl to be quite so hungry for dockside filth.” Aya flinched before she could stop herself. That, more than any blow, landed. Behind her ribs, something dark and old stirred at the edge of thought—the thing she kept leashed in the immateria, the cold doorway she had learned not to open unless she meant to drown the room in shadows and teeth. Her mother’s gift. Her mother’s execution. The noose. The crowd. The smell of rain on rope. Aya forced the memory down so hard it felt like swallowing glass. Morska saw the flicker in her face. Of course she did. “Careful,” she murmured. “I do love a dramatist, but this room is already crowded.” “Is this really necessary?” Simeon blurted, and his voice cracked on the last word. He hated confrontation; everyone knew it. His fear made him sound younger. “We can talk. We can—” “Talk?” Morska said. “You three have broken into my underhold, come within a room of my prize, and expected what? A cordial conference?” Katra strained against her holder, eyes bright with fury. “We expected you to be in a cell.” “A charming lapse.” Aya’s mind ran ahead of itself, tumbling over consequences with appalling speed. The guild leader free. The arrest staged or reversed. A trap. Someone had spoken. Someone had sold them. Her rise through the ranks, her nights in a mask, her careful cultivation of secrets—all of it had led her here, to the edge of exposure, with her face bare and her mother’s blood in the room between them. If Morska denounced her, if the guild learned what she was, what she had done, what she could do— No. Not could. Would. They feared immateria magick. They feared what they did not understand. Men trusted brass and oil and steel because those things obeyed. Magicka did not. Magicka listened. Morska stepped closer, boots striking softly on the stone. “You are clever, Aya. I will grant you that. You have value. But you are not in a position to bargain from strength.” Aya drew one breath. Then another. Felt the pressure of the guard’s hand on her shoulder; the cold awareness of Simeon’s watch still ticking, marking time as though time might be persuaded to care. She made her voice steady by force. “No,” she said. “I’m not. But you’re also not in a position to waste me.” Morska paused. Aya met her gaze, even as her pulse thundered. “If you kill us, you lose the only leverage you have against whoever arranged this arrest in the first place. You think the watch has forgotten you? You think the magistrate’s men acted alone? There’s money in this. Influence. Someone wanted the guild cut open and distracted.” One of the bodyguards shifted. Morska did not look away. Aya pressed on, hating the tremor she could hear at the edges of her own words. “You want the artifact. You want your claim intact. I can help with that. I know the routes, the names, the accounts, the men who’d rather follow coin than loyalty.” A bitter taste rose in her mouth. “And if you have any sense, you’ll keep me alive until you’ve decided whether I’m a liability or an asset.” Silence. Then Morska smiled again, slow and dangerous. “A hostage,” she said. “How original.” Aya did not let her eyes drop. Did not let herself look at Katra or Simeon, though she could feel Katra’s anger like heat and Simeon’s dread like the quiver of a tuning fork. The pocket watch kept ticking, each second another nail driven into the floorboards of her life. Morska tilted her head. “Do you know what I hear in your voice, Aya?” Aya said nothing. “I hear a woman who has already begun calculating how to survive being known.” The words struck too close. Aya felt naked in the truest sense, stripped not only of the mask but of the lie that had carried her this far. Around them the underhold seemed to breathe—lamplight trembling, shadows thickening in the seams between bricks, the artifact on its plinth waiting like a sealed mouth. Morska lifted her pistol and, with the casual finality of a judge closing a file, pointed it at Aya’s heart. “For now,” she said, “you will all be accommodated.” The guard behind Aya tightened his grip. Simeon went white. Katra bared her teeth. Aya lifted her hands slowly, not in surrender exactly, but in purchase. In her mind she reached toward the cold door behind the world, toward the thing that answered when she called in darkness. Not yet, she told it. Not yet. Her face remained calm by sheer and desperate discipline. “Then,” Aya said, voice low, “I suggest we all remain civil. There is still a great deal to discuss.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric writing within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid underground chamber with gothic sensibilities, employing rich sensory details like "black brick sweating salt-damp" and "the colour of old teeth" that establish both the Victorian aesthetic and the oppressive mood. The dialogue feels authentic to the world and characters, with Morska's voice particularly well-crafted - her dry, authoritative tone ("That is disappointingly forward of you") and calculated menace come through clearly. Aya's internal struggle between aristocratic composure and genuine fear is well-rendered, and her attempt to negotiate from weakness feels believable given her character background. The pacing effectively builds tension, moving from the initial shock of discovery through the unmasking to the final standoff. The author handles the "no fight scene" requirement well, instead focusing on psychological tension and power dynamics. The incorporation of Aya's magical abilities as both a threat and a vulnerability adds depth to the confrontation. However, some descriptions veer toward purple prose ("a wound in the earth," "seemed to drink the lamplight rather than reflect it"), though this generally serves the gothic atmosphere rather than detracting from it. The piece occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states, particularly with Simeon's characterization, though this is relatively minor. The worldbuilding elements are seamlessly integrated - the tension between magic and technology, the class dynamics, and the criminal underworld all feel cohesive. The ending maintains appropriate darkness without false hope, and Aya's character development feels earned through the scene's events. The writing maintains consistent voice throughout, and the sentences generally flow well, though some longer descriptive passages could be tightened. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls like unearned character transformations or incongruent tone shifts. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 81.2
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bar is called The Saint, which is a joke so bad nobody bothers laughing. It sits under a flickering neon cross on the wrong side of a city that forgot it had a right side. The windows are too dirty to see through, the floor is sticky in a way that suggests old beer, old blood, and older regrets, and the music leaking from the jukebox sounds like somebody strangling a trumpet. Spike Spiegel pushes through the door with his hands in his coat pockets and a cigarette hanging from his mouth, looking like he’s wandered in by accident and might drift back out at any second. He doesn’t. He spots the bounty immediately. Not because the target is distinctive—just a middling-level fence with a broken nose and an expensive coat—but because somebody else spots him first. Revy sits three stools down from the target, one boot hooked on the brass rail, a whiskey glass in one hand and a cigarette in the other. Black crop top, absurdly short shorts, bare thighs crossed like she owns the room and the air in it. The dragon tattoo on her arm curls into the bar’s dim light when she lifts her drink. She looks Spike over once and grins without humor. “Great,” she says. “The pretty boy’s here too.” Spike inclines his head, lazy as a cat in the sun. “Evening.” “Don’t ‘evening’ me.” She turns to the bartender. “Another drink. And make it strong enough to forget this asshole exists.” Spike reaches the bar and slides onto the stool beside her like there’s no tension in the world worth noticing. He drops a few wrinkled credits and nods at the bottle of cheap bourbon. “Same.” The bartender looks from one of them to the other, senses fate and bad choices, and pours. Revy watches Spike’s hand as he takes the glass. “You’re Spike Spiegel.” He gives her a small, amused glance. “Depends who’s asking.” “Name’s Revy.” She taps ash into the tray. “I know who you are. You’re the bastard who thinks he can talk his way out of getting shot.” Spike’s mouth quirks. “Sometimes it works.” “That so?” “Whatever happens, happens.” Revy snorts into her drink. “Yeah? That’s a real comforting philosophy. You always sound this full of shit?” “Only on weekdays.” The target in the corner laughs too loudly at something nobody’s said. Spike and Revy both glance his way at the same time, and the synchronization lands between them like a spark. Recognition, then. They’ve both been hired for the same job. That much is obvious. What isn’t obvious is who the better killer is, which makes the room feel smaller. Revy leans in first, too close to be casual, her voice low. “Here’s how this goes, cowboy. I take the mark, you walk away. You can keep your pride and your pretty face.” Spike sips his bourbon. It’s terrible. He drinks it anyway. “I was going to suggest you walk away.” “Bold.” “Been called worse.” “Been called better?” Her grin sharpens. “By who, exactly?” Spike considers her, cigarette ember glowing in the dim. There’s a violence to her that doesn’t need an introduction; it comes with the scent of whiskey and gun oil, with the way her fingers rest near her holsters like they’re part of her body. He’s seen that kind of danger before. He’s also seen worse. That doesn’t make it any less interesting. “Maybe by people with better aim,” he says. Revy laughs once, hard and delighted. “Oh, you are asking for it.” The target stands, finally. He’s seen them. His hand goes to his jacket, and the whole bar seems to inhale. Spike’s left hand moves first. Revy’s right hand moves first. Both of them are faster than everyone else in the room. The first shot cracks like a snapped bone. The target jerks, winded and confused, because Spike’s bullet has taken the glass from his hand without touching his wrist. Revy’s second shot slams into the wall by his head, splintering wood and sending half the bar diving under tables. He goes down anyway, knocked off balance by panic and bad luck. Then the real shooting starts. Spike slides off the stool in a smooth pivot, the cigarette still in his mouth until he flicks it away with two fingers. His pistol is in his hand, then not, then in his other hand, moving with that relaxed, impossible grace that makes it look like he’s dancing. Revy doesn’t dance. She detonates. Two guns appear in her hands, one in each, and the room fills with staccato thunder as she walks shots into the ceiling, into bottles, into the legs of men who make the mistake of reaching for their own weapons. Glass rains down. The jukebox dies in a whine of static. Somebody screams. The target scrambles toward the back exit. Spike’s prosthetic eye catches the angle wrong for a fraction of a second, depth slipping just enough to annoy him. He compensates by turning his shoulder, firing low and fast. Revy sees him miss by an inch and smirks like she’s been handed proof of something. “Need a telescope, pretty boy?” “Need better manners, Two Hands?” She bares her teeth at that and unloads a burst that shatters the target’s escape route. He tumbles into a stack of liquor crates, dazed and bleeding. Spike reaches him first. Revy’s muzzle is there an instant later, pointed at Spike’s ribs. Spike’s own gun is leveled at her throat. The room goes still except for the groans of the wounded and the drip of spilled liquor. The target whimpers between them. “You can split it,” he says. “I can pay—” “No,” they say at the same time. Revy’s smile is all knife. “I saw him first.” Spike’s expression remains pleasantly bored. “Did you? Hard to tell with all the shooting.” “You’re really committed to being annoying, huh?” “It’s a skill.” Her eyes flick to his mouth, then back to his face. The glance lasts less than a second, but it lands with a heat neither of them acknowledges. Then the cops arrive, drawn by the noise and the kind of neighborhood where gunfire is a local language. Spike curses under his breath. Revy does not bother with words at all. She grabs the target by the collar, hauls him up, and shoves him toward the back door. “Move, cowboy.” Spike falls in beside her because, for the moment, their priorities happen to match. Bullets chip the doorway behind them as they run into the alley, rain-slick and stinking of garbage. The target tries to wriggle free and gets a pistol butt to the mouth for his trouble. They lose the cops two streets later in a maze of neon, steam, and bad roads. By then the target is zip-tied, bleeding, and swearing. Revy has his bounty chip. Spike has the only clean jacket either of them is likely to see tonight. They make it to a motel on the outskirts of the district because neither of them is sentimental enough to care where they sleep, and because the room clerk is too tired to ask questions. The room is one bed, one chair, one buzzing lamp, and one window with a crack down the middle. Revy tosses the target onto the mattress like luggage. Spike closes the door, checks the lock, and then checks it again out of habit. “Don’t get any ideas,” she says, stripping off her gloves and reaching for the bottle in the minibar. “I’m turning him in tomorrow.” Spike leans against the dresser. “That’s fine. I’m only here for the sleep.” “You always talk like you’re above all this?” He lights a cigarette. The lighter clicks twice before catching; there’s a ring of rust around the wheel, proof it has been dropped in water more than once. He glances at it, then at her. “I’m not above anything,” he says. “Just too tired to pretend otherwise.” For a moment, Revy doesn’t answer. She takes a long pull from the bottle, wipes her mouth with the back of her hand, and studies him with the same blunt intensity she uses on a target. “You look like a guy who’s already dead,” she says. Spike exhales smoke. “Been there.” That gets her attention in a different way. Her expression shifts, almost imperceptibly, from mockery to something like understanding, which in Revy is practically an act of charity. She steps closer. Not enough to touch. Enough to make the air between them feel crowded. “You always this calm after trying to kill somebody?” “Only when I win.” “I didn’t let you win.” Spike smiles. “Didn’t say you did.” The target, still tied up, lets out a frightened sound. Neither of them looks at him. Revy’s hand drifts near one holster. Spike notices. Of course he notices. He also notices the tension in her shoulders, the way her jaw works around whatever she isn’t saying, the danger of her up close: all that anger held together by muscle and habit and maybe one or two dangerous cracks. She notices him noticing. “So what now?” she asks. Spike flicks ash into the sink. “Depends.” “On what?” He looks at her for a long second, blue eye steady, prosthetic giving him that half-misaligned stare that makes him seem even less trustworthy than he is. “Whether you’re aiming at me or the bed.” Revy’s laugh is short and low. “Fuck you.” “Still undecided?” Her smile fades into something sharper, more vulnerable around the edges than she’d ever allow in daylight. “I don’t trust you.” “Good.” “That’s good?” “It means you’re paying attention.” She stares at him like she wants to shoot him, kiss him, or both in no particular order. Spike pushes off the dresser, slow enough not to read as a threat, and lets his hand fall near his own gun without touching it. Revy mirrors him automatically. Two predators in a cramped motel room, both pretending they’re not circling. Neither of them moves first. The fluorescent light hums. The city outside keeps bleeding neon through the cracked window. On the bed, the bounty moans through a split lip. Revy takes one more step forward, close enough that Spike can smell whiskey, smoke, and gunpowder on her. Close enough that she can probably smell cigarettes and rain and whatever old ghost still clings to him. Her eyes drop to his mouth again. Spike’s expression says nothing. His pulse says something else entirely. Revy lifts her pistol and presses the cold barrel lightly against his chest. Spike doesn’t flinch. He raises his own gun and rests it under her chin. For a long moment, they stand there in perfect balance, breathing the same thin air, each one waiting for the other to blink. Neither does. Then Revy smiles, slow and dangerous, and Spike smiles back like he’s already accepted the outcome. “Whatever happens,” she murmurs. “Happens,” Spike finishes. Outside, sirens pass farther away. Inside, the standoff holds—unfinished, unresolved, and burning hot enough to light the whole damn room.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding. The author captures both Spike and Revy's voices authentically - Spike's laconic fatalism ("Whatever happens, happens") and Revy's aggressive, profane directness come through clearly. The dialogue crackles with tension and feels natural to both characters, avoiding exposition dumps while revealing personality. The setting work is particularly effective. The seedy bar description ("sticky floor suggesting old beer, old blood, and older regrets") establishes atmosphere without overwrought detail. The action sequences are well-choreographed, showing rather than telling the characters' skills through specific details like Spike's depth perception issues and Revy's dual-wielding prowess. The sexual tension builds organically through the competitive dynamic, escalating naturally from verbal sparring to gunplay to the final standoff. The author wisely leaves the ending unresolved, which fits both characters' trust issues and the prompt's requirements. The mutual gun-pointing scene effectively crystallizes their attraction/danger dynamic. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the "both hired for same bounty" setup leads exactly where expected. The prose occasionally veers toward overwrought ("detonates" for Revy's fighting style), though it generally maintains restraint. A few descriptions border on purple prose ("breathing the same thin air"). The characterization remains consistent throughout, with Spike's prosthetic eye detail and Revy's vulnerability cracks adding depth. The piece successfully balances action, character development, and sexual tension within the word limit. The ending standoff is earned through the escalating dynamic rather than forced. The writing flows well with varied sentence structure, though some metaphors feel slightly strained. Overall, this is competent genre fiction that understands its source material and delivers on the prompt's requirements effectively. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
I remember the cold first. Not the fear, though that came too, or the shame of following Astrid out into the dark as if I had not been raised in Stockholm with enough sense to know better. I remember the cold because it was sharp and clean and immediate, biting through the wool of my coat the moment we left the last streetlamp behind and stepped onto the track that led past the fields. It was one of those northern nights in late autumn when the sky never quite became black, only a deep blue bruise full of stars. The pines stood on either side of the path like witnesses. Every so often the wind moved through them and the sound was like whispering—soft, deliberate, almost human if you were already frightened. Astrid walked ahead of me with the confidence of someone who had no doubt the night belonged to her. “Don’t step on the stones in the wrong order,” she said without turning around. “I’m not stepping on any stones in any order,” I muttered. She stopped and looked back at me, her pale face half-hidden under the brim of her knitted cap. “Then you’ll have to follow me exactly.” I should have gone home then. I knew that even while I was standing there with my breath steaming in front of me and my hands shoved deep into my pockets. But Astrid had a way of making everything sound like a dare and a secret at once, and I had already gone too far with her too many times to back out neatly. At school she was the one who knew how to say the right thing at the wrong time. She wore secondhand clothes she altered herself and had a grandmother who, according to Astrid, remembered things no one else did. Not prayers exactly, she said, not really, but old ways. Ways for finding out what people hid. Ways for making them look your way. Ways for knowing if the land itself was angry. At first I thought she was inventing all of it to impress me. That was how she presented most things—half joke, half challenge, eyes bright with the thrill of seeing whether I’d believe her. We had started with small things: tying three knots in red thread to make a boy at school think about you, burying an apple core under a birch tree so a teacher would answer you more kindly, whispering a name into a glass of water and placing it on the windowsill until morning. She always explained each step carefully, as if she were showing me how to bake a cake. “This is older than your city nonsense,” she had said once, with a grin that made it sound like a compliment and an insult at the same time. Now, climbing over the uneven ground toward the burial mound, I wished I had laughed harder and refused more often. The mound rose out of the field beyond the trees, a dark swell in the earth, easy to miss in daylight if you didn’t know where to look. Astrid said people had been buried there before the church came, before roads, before anyone asked permission from the right authorities. She said her grandmother called such places søm or gravhögar in the old tongue, depending on who in the family was speaking and how careful they wanted to be. I had nodded when she told me, as though any of it meant something to me besides getting under my skin. At the base of the mound she set down her bag and took out the things she had brought wrapped in a dish towel: a stub of black candle, a box of matches, two small pieces of bread, a pinch of salt in folded paper, and a tiny bell no bigger than a thimble. “What’s the bell for?” I whispered. “To ask politely,” she said. “And to answer if something answers back.” “That’s not funny.” “I’m not joking.” She lit the candle with one match, shielding the flame with her cupped hand. The little glow made her face look older. It made all of us look older, I think, because there we were at fifteen or sixteen, pretending to be brave in a place where every instinct in my body said to be small and quiet and go home. The candle sputtered in the wind, then steadied. Astrid pressed the bread into my hand. “We each eat a bite,” she said. “For the living. Then we leave some for the dead.” I stared at the bread. “You’re serious.” “Linnea.” There was no teasing in her voice now. That frightened me more than if she had laughed. We climbed the mound on the side she indicated, not the topmost path but a slanted shoulder where the grass was shorter, worn down by rain and time. The ground felt springy and uncertain beneath my boots. I could hear my own heartbeat under the layers of wool. Astrid knelt first, then motioned for me to sit beside her. The grass was damp enough to seep through my jeans almost immediately. Cold spread into my knees. “Don’t cross your legs,” she said. “Why?” “It blocks the way.” “The way to what?” She gave me a look that said I was ruining the mood by asking sensible questions. “Just don’t.” So I sat with my legs straight and the bread balanced in my lap and tried not to think about how stupid I felt. The candle flickered between us. The pine trees rustled again and again with the wind, and every time the sound came I told myself it was only the branches talking to one another. Astrid removed the bell from her pocket and placed it on the earth between us. “First we make the circle,” she murmured. “There’s no circle.” “There is if you know how to make one.” She drew an invisible ring in the air with her finger, then laid the salt in a thin crescent around the candle, not a full circle. “You leave a way out,” she said, as if reciting something she had heard a hundred times. “Always. You don’t trap what you can’t send away.” The words made the hairs rise along my neck. She looked at me until I took the pinch of salt between my fingers and sprinkled it where she indicated. Then she broke off a piece of bread, ate it, and put the rest on the ground beside the candle. “For the one below,” she said. I swallowed. “Below what?” She did not answer. Instead she closed her eyes and bowed her head. I copied her because I didn’t know what else to do. The grass was wet against my palms. Somewhere far off a dog barked once and then was quiet. Astrid began to whisper. It wasn’t Swedish exactly, not the Swedish we spoke at school or at home. It had the cadence of something older, the hard and soft sounds rubbing against each other in a way that made the words feel shaped by mouths used to other prayers. I caught fragments—a name perhaps, then something like a request, then the same phrase repeated three times. When she opened her eyes they were fixed on the dark beyond the candle, not on me. My mouth had gone dry. “What are you saying?” She didn’t look at me. “Just sit. Listen.” So I listened. At first there was only the wind moving through the pines, the faint hiss of it, the occasional cracking of the candle flame. Then I heard something else—not outside me, exactly. A pressure in the air maybe. A change. The kind of silence that is not truly silent because it is waiting. Astrid lifted the bell and gave it the smallest tap. The sound was tiny, almost swallowed by the night, but it seemed to travel farther than it should have. It vanished into the field and came back thinner, as if the darkness had taken a bite out of it. I laughed then, because I couldn’t help myself. It was a nervous, ugly sound. Astrid shot me a look. “Don’t.” “I just—” My voice shook. “This is ridiculous.” “Maybe.” But she didn’t smile, and that made my stomach tighten. We sat. The candle burned lower. My legs had gone numb from the cold. The wind lifted, then eased. Somewhere under the mound, or maybe only in my head, I thought I heard a faint answering tap—like knuckles against wood, or stone against stone. I froze. Astrid heard it too. Her eyes widened a fraction, though she kept very still. Again came the sound. Not loud. Not enough to be certain. A soft knock from below the earth. “Was that you?” I whispered. She shook her head once. The night seemed to lean closer. I wanted to stand up, to say we were done, to laugh at how terrified I was and force the moment back into something ordinary. But Astrid’s hand had gone cold over my wrist, and her grip was not hard, only warning. Her face had changed. Not frightened exactly. Focused. Listening with her whole body. Then the candle bent sideways as if in a draft I could not feel. The flame stretched long and blue at the base before snapping upright again. I stared at it, unable to decide whether I had imagined the movement. The wind had stopped. The pines were still. No birds, no dogs, no distant tractor, nothing from the village beyond us. Just the two of us on the mound and the small, absurd candle. Astrid whispered, “Ask.” “Me?” She nodded. “You’re the one who wants to know.” “I don’t know what to ask.” “Anything true.” That was the worst part, I think. Not the possibility of ghosts or buried dead or old gods under the hill. It was the way she said it like a game, as though truth were just another thing you could shake loose if you were patient and rude enough. My throat tightened. I thought of school, of the girls who spoke to me only because Astrid had adopted me, of the boy in my math class who never looked at me until Astrid told me which day to wear my red scarf. I thought of my mother unpacking boxes in the new kitchen and saying, too brightly, that it would take time but we would adjust. I thought of how small the village seemed in daylight and how much smaller it felt at night. I swallowed and leaned toward the dark. “Is anyone there?” I asked, hating myself the moment I said it. For a moment nothing happened. Then the bell gave a tiny, clear ring without either of us touching it. I jerked back so fast I nearly fell over. Astrid inhaled sharply. The candle guttered. In the same instant the ground beneath my hands seemed to thrum once, very faintly, like something shifting in sleep. Or maybe my own pulse had become loud enough to shake the earth. Astrid’s face had gone pale, but her voice was steady when she said, “Don’t move.” I couldn’t have moved if I wanted to. I was staring at the dark edge of the mound, where the grass ended and the field began, convinced that something—someone—was standing just beyond the candlelight. Nothing appeared. The wind moved again through the pines, sounding almost exactly like a whisper spoken into an ear. Then the candle went out. The darkness rushed in around us, complete and immediate. I grabbed Astrid’s sleeve. She grabbed mine back. We sat there in the cold with the dead candle between us and no one speaking. I could hear my own breathing, shallow and fast. I could hear Astrid’s too. That was all. After a long time she stood, fingers fumbling in the dark for the matches, and said in a voice that had gone oddly flat, “We should go.” We did not run. That would have been too much like admitting something. But we left quickly, stumbling down the side of the mound with the candle stub, the bread, the little bell, and the salt forgotten behind us in the grass. Only once did I look back. The mound was only a mound. The field was only a field. The pines were only trees. The wind rustled through them like whispering. And still I remember wondering, with a coldness that had nothing to do with the night air, whether we had heard something answer us—or whether we had crossed a line so old that the land itself had simply turned its face toward us to see who was careless enough to call.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric control while successfully fulfilling the prompt's complex requirements. The author establishes an authentic folk horror atmosphere through careful attention to setting, character dynamics, and the gradual escalation of supernatural ambiguity. The narrative voice is particularly well-crafted, maintaining the promised "largely transparent" present-day recollection device while keeping Linnea's teenage perspective authentic. The prose strikes an effective balance between literary sophistication and accessibility, with sensory details that ground the reader in the cold Swedish night without becoming overwrought. Character development is nuanced, especially in the portrayal of Astrid as both manipulative friend and genuine believer in her grandmother's traditions. Linnea's internal conflict between skepticism and curiosity feels psychologically authentic for a displaced teenager seeking belonging. The power dynamic between the girls is subtly rendered through dialogue and action rather than exposition. The ritual sequence builds tension effectively through specific, researched-feeling details (the salt circle with an opening, the bell, the bread offering) that lend credibility to Astrid's claimed knowledge. The author maintains the crucial ambiguity between supernatural and psychological explanations throughout, never definitively confirming either interpretation. The pacing is well-controlled, moving from reluctant participation through mounting unease to genuine fear without rushing or dragging. The ending strikes an appropriate note of lingering uncertainty that serves the folk horror genre well. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious in its literary aspirations, and a few instances where Linnea's modern Stockholm background could be more distinctly contrasted with the rural setting. The dialogue, while generally natural, occasionally feels slightly formal for teenagers. The piece succeeds in treating ancient practices with appropriate gravity while showing how they might be trivialized by modern youth, creating the "dangerous line" the prompt requested. The supernatural elements feel earned rather than gratuitous, emerging organically from the established atmosphere and character motivations. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
