He enjoys the rare uncertainty in her. “And you,” he says, voice softening into something almost intimate, “are not merely pretty magic.” Her nostrils flare. “You flirt like a corpse.” “I am a corpse.” “That explains the charm.” One of the broken altar stones lifts behind her, suspended by invisible force. Alucard does not look at it. He looks at her. “You wear your power well,” he says. “But you hide yourself with it.” Her eyes flash. “And you wear your nature like a carnival costume, which is somehow worse.” The stone launches. Alucard’s body blurs. The rock tears through where his throat was a moment before, exploding against the far wall. He is suddenly behind her, impossibly close, one gloved hand resting lightly near her shoulder as if they were dancing. Yennefer reacts instantly, driving an elbow back. He is already gone. Her spell bursts from her palm in a lance of violet force, detonating the air where he stood. The chapel windows shatter the rest of the way. Dust rains from the rafters. The wolves go silent. Alucard appears in the far corner of the ruin, unruffled, one lens of his orange sunglasses reflecting her in jagged fragments. “You strike like a woman with something to prove.” “And you speak like a man who believes he’s immortal.” “I am immortal.” “Then you’ll be bored forever.” He smiles, and for the first time there is something sharper than amusement in it. Interest. Genuine, dangerous interest. “Perhaps not,” he says. Yennefer notices the shift and hates that she notices it. She hates that some traitorous part of her body responds to the intensity of him: not desire, exactly, but the terrible recognition of a force that could flatten a village and still remain dissatisfied. A creature like that should inspire only revulsion. Instead, it awakens an older, colder instinct—the one that tells her to stand her ground because no one else will. She lowers her hand, though the air remains primed around her. “What are you doing here?” “Walking.” “In a place where magic gathers?” “I was following the scent.” “My scent?” “That too.” He pauses. “But more the magic. There are things loose in these woods that do not belong to this world. They’ve been feeding.” Yennefer’s gaze sharpens. “I know. I’ve been hunting them.” “Then we share a quarry.” “I don’t share.” He laughs again, delighted by her stubbornness. “No. I noticed.” The wolves howl once more, and this time the sound is nearer, accompanied by the wet cracking of branches and a chittering noise far too many throats should make at once. Something pushes through the fog outside the chapel: shapes too tall, too thin, too many limbs. Not wolves. Not men. Yennefer turns, face composed. “Those aren’t natural.” Alucard’s smile broadens into something almost hungry. “Neither am I.” He draws both pistols in a single fluid motion. The metal gleams. Yennefer summons a shield of blue-white force around herself, then hesitates—just a fraction—before extending part of it toward him. Alucard notices. His head tilts. “Cooperation?” “Don’t make me regret it.” “I make a point of disappointing people first.” The creatures slam into the chapel entrance, shrieking. Alucard fires. The handguns bark in thunderous succession, silver rounds tearing through the first monstrosity and disassembling it in fountains of black blood. Yennefer lashes out with a burst of force that hurls two more into the shattered wall. A third lunges for her; Alucard is suddenly there, his hand closing around its skull, and the thing combusts under his grip as if he has crushed a lantern full of tar. Yennefer does not thank him. She simply keeps moving, elegant and lethal, threads of magic snaring, breaking, and flaying the attackers apart. Alucard feeds on the violence with obvious pleasure. He is all motion and mockery, all savage grace and insolent endurance. She is precision—each spell a blade, each gesture a verdict. Together, they are catastrophic. When the last creature falls, the chapel falls silent again except for the wolves, whose distant howls have resumed as if the forest is breathing once more. Yennefer lowers her hand slowly. Her breathing remains calm, but her eyes are brighter now, sharpened by adrenaline and reluctant curiosity. “You’re insufferable.” “You survived.” “That’s not the same thing.” “It often is.” She studies him for a long moment. Up close, with blood misting the air and his coat torn and blackened, he should look less like a man and more like a mistake. Instead he looks inevitable. An old, indestructible wound in the shape of a person. At last she says, “You could have let me die.” “You could have tried harder to die.” Her lips twitch despite her. “Arrogant.” “Beautiful.” “Don’t.” “Why?” “Because I don’t need a monster’s approval.” His expression changes at that. Not offended. Pleased. As if she has passed some private test. “The bird of Hermes,” he says softly, almost reverently, “is my name, eating my wings to make me tame.” Yennefer’s brows lift. “Poetry. How unexpected.” “Mockery, coming from you?” “An instinct.” The cold air between them crackles, but not with hostility alone. It is something more dangerous: recognition. Two beings who have twisted themselves around power and pain, who refuse submission because it would feel too much like death, each looking for the hidden fracture in the other. Yennefer breaks the silence first. “If you’re stalking these woods for sport, I’ll finish what they started.” Alucard tips his hat. “If I were stalking you for sport, sorceress, you’d know.” “And if I wanted you dead?” “You’d need to be far more inventive.” She steps closer, close enough to smell the iron on him beneath the smoke and old blood. Her violet eyes lift to his sunglasses, as if trying to see through the absurd mask he wears to whatever remains of the ancient thing beneath. “I’m not sweet,” she says, voice level, “and I’m not a maiden in distress in need of saving.” His grin returns, slow and sharp. “Good. I would have been disappointed.” The wolves howl again, nearer now, but the sound no longer matters. Somewhere in the ruined chapel, blue fire sputters and dies. Outside, the fog shifts like a living thing, and the road beyond waits in darkness. Yennefer turns first, because she will not be the one to retreat from this encounter like a scolded child. Alucard follows at a measured distance, because he chooses to let her believe she leads. For now. The balance between them is a knife-edge, constantly shifting, never settled. She knows he is dangerous beyond reason. He knows she is clever enough to be interesting. Neither trusts the other. Neither yields. And yet, as they step out into the dead forest with wolves circling somewhere beyond the mist, each feels the same unwelcome certainty. This is not over. Not by blood. Not by magic. Not by will. And perhaps, in some deeper and more terrible way, not by desire either.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical writing ability with vivid atmospheric description and solid character work. The author successfully captures the essence of both Alucard and Yennefer, maintaining their distinctive personalities while creating believable interactions between them. The medieval European setting is well-established through environmental details and the supernatural threat they face together. The prose quality is generally high, with effective use of imagery and metaphor. Lines like "The wolves begin before the road does" and "Medieval Europe is full of such places: roads laid over older roads, ruins built atop older ruins" establish atmosphere efficiently. The author demonstrates skill in balancing action sequences with character development. However, the piece suffers from occasional overwrought passages that border on purple prose. Descriptions sometimes become unnecessarily elaborate, such as "blue fire hovers in a ring above the floor, painting the broken nave in an unnatural glow" followed immediately by more ornate scene-setting. The dialogue, while generally strong, occasionally feels stilted or overly formal for the characters. The power dynamic between the characters is well-executed, with neither character yielding completely to the other. Their verbal sparring feels authentic to their personalities, and the sexual tension is handled with appropriate subtlety. The action sequence with the creatures provides effective pacing relief while allowing both characters to demonstrate their abilities. The ending maintains the tension appropriately without forcing resolution, which suits the prompt's requirements. The piece avoids major structural problems and maintains coherence throughout. While not groundbreaking in its creativity, it executes its premise competently with solid character work and atmospheric writing. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 9 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 7.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 8.0, Overwrought: 9.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Fanfic-crossover: Prison Strings — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
Prison Strings Jolyne Cujoh & Makima Write a story where Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure) and Makima (Chainsaw Man) cross paths at a supernatural prison facility. Jolyne is immediately suspicious of Makima's falsely sweet demeanor, while Makima is intrigued by Jolyne's Stand ability. Their battle of wits escalates dangerously as Jolyne realizes how manipulative Makima truly is. **Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure)** Jolyne is the daughter of Jotaro Kujo and the first female JoJo protagonist. Her Stand, Stone Free, allows her to unravel her body into string that she can control. She's hot-headed, foul-mouthed, and doesn't take disrespect from anyone. She was framed for murder and sent to prison. She has a distinctive butterfly tattoo on her arm and wears her hair in two buns. Despite her tough exterior, she's deeply loyal to her friends and family. She can be impulsive but is highly adaptable in battle. She inherited her father's ability to stay cool under pressure but is more emotionally expressive. Famous quote: "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney!" **Makima (Chainsaw Man)** Makima appears as a young woman with auburn hair and ringed yellow eyes. She's the Control Devil who can dominate anyone she considers beneath her (nearly everyone). She maintains a pleasant, soft-spoken demeanor that masks her manipulative, cold nature. She can create contracts with humans and devils, often exploiting their desires. She's obsessed with Chainsaw Man and wants to control him. She keeps numerous people and devils as pets on leashes. She's practically immortal due to a contract with the Prime Minister. She views humans as lesser beings but mimics human emotions perfectly. Famous quote: "It's okay. Dogs are supposed to die for their owners." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The facility is called Saint Bell Penitentiary by the staff, though the inmates call it what it really is: a cathedral built for monsters. It sits inland behind three walls, two electric fences, and a ring of floodlights that turn the night white as bone. Beneath it, buried in layers of reinforced concrete, are the things nobody wants in the world anymore—curses, Stand users, devil-blooded convicts, and the staff who are either brave enough or foolish enough to guard them. Jolyne Cujoh hates the place the moment she sees it. She stands in the intake corridor with her wrists cuffed, orange prison fabric clinging to her shoulders, her butterfly tattoo visible beneath the fluorescent glare. Her eyes move once over the camera in the ceiling, then again over the guard at her left, then over the woman waiting farther down the hall. The woman looks harmless. That is exactly why Jolyne does not trust her. Auburn hair. Pale skin. A neat navy suit that makes her look more like a corporate visitor than anyone meant to be in prison. Her smile is soft, almost apologetic. Gentle hands folded in front of her. Ringed yellow eyes that never blink long enough to feel human. Makima. The name passes through the intake staff like a religious warning. The guards straighten when she turns her head. Even the cameras above her seem to stutter, their red recording lights flickering. Jolyne notices that immediately. “Why’re the cameras acting weird around her?” she mutters. The guard escorting her doesn’t answer. He’s too busy trying not to look at Makima directly. Makima steps forward with a quiet click of heels. “Jolyne Cujoh,” she says, as if tasting the name. “We’ve heard a great deal about you.” “Yeah?” Jolyne gives her a sharp grin that isn’t friendly in the slightest. “I wish I could say the same.” Makima’s smile doesn’t shift. “You have a remarkable ability. Stone Free, correct?” Jolyne’s posture changes in an instant. Her shoulders tighten. “Who told you that?” “No one needed to tell me.” Makima tilts her head. “People who break prisons tend to become interesting.” Jolyne hates the way that sounds. Not complimentary. Appraising. Like a veterinarian speaking about a dangerous animal. “Look,” Jolyne says, pulling once against the cuffs for emphasis, “if this is some kind of welcome committee, you can fuck right off. I’ve been framed, drugged, shot at, and buried alive enough times to know when somebody’s being fake.” Makima’s expression warms by exactly the correct amount, as if she has just heard something charmingly spirited. “That’s good. Suspicion keeps you alive here.” Jolyne narrows her eyes. “You say that like you know.” “I do.” Somewhere in the walls, a metal door slams. The staff lead Jolyne toward the inner block, but Makima walks beside them without asking permission. That alone is enough to make every prisoner in the adjacent corridor go quiet. Men and women alike press back against the bars. One devil-marked inmate actually makes a sign to ward off evil. Jolyne sees why. Where Makima passes, the cameras above flicker and die, one by one, their feeds drowning in static. A technician at the junction box swears and slaps the machine. The screen comes back only long enough to show Makima’s back before going black again. Jolyne stares. “Okay. That’s not normal.” Makima glances up, mild as rain. “Is anything here?” That answer is too smooth. Too practiced. They reach the central observation block: a circular chamber with layered windows overlooking the prison yard and the containment wings below. There are restraints built into the floor. Chains hang from the ceiling. The room itself feels designed to host something that should never be allowed to stand upright. Makima takes the chair at the center without being invited. Jolyne stops at the edge of the room. “You’re not staff.” “No,” Makima says. “I’m an advisor.” “On what, exactly?” Makima folds her hands. “Managing people who are difficult to manage.” Jolyne barks a laugh. “That’s one hell of a way to say ‘controlling idiots.’” For the first time, Makima’s smile thins by a hair. It is so small most people would miss it. Jolyne does not. Stone Free stirs under her skin. Threads of invisible tension seem to gather around her arms, ready to unravel. Makima watches her with bright, patient interest. “Your Stand is a remarkable form of self-extension,” she says. “You can disperse, bind, listen, stitch, and strike from impossible angles. You turn your body into a tool.” “Don’t talk about my body like you own it.” “I don’t need to own something to appreciate its utility.” That lands wrong in Jolyne’s chest, like a hooked wire. A voice crackles over the intercom. “Intake incident in Wing B. Level Four hostile refusing containment.” Makima rises at once. “Take C block. I’ll handle it.” The guard nearest the door hesitates. “Ma’am, protocol says—” Makima looks at him. He falls silent immediately, pupils shrinking as if a leash has jerked tight around his throat. He swallows, then nods. “Yes, ma’am.” Jolyne’s stomach drops. Not just the guard. The whole room shifts. A few staff members go slack-eyed for half a second, then force themselves upright, faces empty with obedience. Jolyne steps closer, voice low and dangerous. “What the hell was that?” Makima turns, calm as ever. “Efficiency.” “No. Don’t give me that bullshit. You made him—” “People here respond better when they know their place.” Jolyne’s jaw clenches so hard it aches. “You can do that to anybody?” “Not anybody.” Makima’s gaze settles on her. “Only those beneath me.” The words are spoken softly. Matter-of-factly. Like gravity. Jolyne feels Stone Free’s threads buzz all the way down her spine. A siren blares. Red lights flood the hallway outside. The guards begin moving to the disturbance, and Makima walks with them as if she belongs in the center of every command chain. Jolyne follows because there is no way she’s letting that woman out of sight. Wing B is chaos. A containment cell has torn open from the inside. Something pale and shrieking thrashes behind broken bars, flinging blood against the glass. The floor is laced with symbols etched in silver, but they’re cracking. Two guards are down. One is screaming. Another is dragging himself away with his arm bent backwards at the elbow. Makima looks at the creature with mild curiosity. “That one will be useful.” The inmate-thing snarls and hurls itself forward. Jolyne moves first. Her body peels into string in a flash, skin unraveling into gleaming threads that whip through the air and knot around the broken cell door. Stone Free yanks hard, redirecting the beast’s lunge so it slams shoulder-first into the wall. The impact shakes dust from the ceiling. “Nice try, asshole!” Jolyne snaps, re-forming as she lands. The creature twists, mouth opening too wide. Makima lifts one hand. The creature stops. Not slows. Stops. Its head jerks upright like an obedient dog hearing a whistle. The bloodshot rage drains from its face and is replaced by a vacant, terrified stillness. Jolyne stares. “What the fuck did you just do?” Makima does not answer immediately. She walks closer, the monster trembling as she approaches, every inch of it now frozen by something far worse than a cage. “There,” she says softly, almost fondly. “That’s better.” The creature whines. Jolyne’s skin goes cold. No one in the prison should be able to do that. Not to a devil, not to a cursed prisoner, not to a Stand user. The thing is looking at Makima the way a beaten animal looks at a raised hand. Jolyne steps between them. “Back up.” Makima looks at her, expression serene. “You shouldn’t interfere.” “Yeah? Watch me.” Stone Free lashes out, strings snapping around a shattered length of pipe. Jolyne whips it forward like a spear, not at Makima’s body but at the overhead camera in the corner. The lens cracks. Makima’s eyes shift toward the ruined camera, then back to Jolyne. For a second, the air changes. The smile remains. The warmth remains. But something beneath it opens like a trapdoor. “You’re very perceptive,” Makima says. “Damn right I am.” “And very difficult.” “Funny,” Jolyne spits. “I was about to say the same about you.” The creature in the cell whimpers, still frozen in place. Makima extends one finger toward it and gives a tiny, dismissive motion. The thing folds to the floor as if its strings have been cut from the inside. Jolyne’s breath catches. Makima turns to the guards. “Clean this up.” They move instantly. Jolyne watches them with disgust. “You’re controlling the staff.” Makima’s expression is almost indulgent. “They were already eager to obey. I simply removed the hesitation.” “That’s not how people work.” “It is here.” Jolyne steps in closer until they are nearly nose to nose. “I don’t care how many cameras go out when you walk by, or how many weak-ass guards you can brainwash with a look. If you think I’m gonna be one of your pets, you’re out of your fucking mind.” The smile on Makima’s face is thin enough to cut. “Pet?” she repeats, as if amused by the concept. “No, Jolyne. You’re different.” Jolyne’s stomach tightens. Makima’s ringed eyes narrow with interest. “You resist. You adapt. You keep trying even when the odds are impossible. People like that are valuable.” Jolyne does not like the way that sounds either. It sounds like ownership. Like a hand closing around a collar. Without warning, Stone Free bursts from her arm in a dense ribbon of strings, threading itself through the broken room in a web so fine it becomes nearly invisible. Jolyne’s plan is simple: trap Makima, break her line of sight, test what happens when the control fails. Makima does not move. The strings stop. Jolyne feels the interruption through Stone Free like a sharp tug from the soul. The threads shiver, then slacken, as if someone has reached out from the dark and pinched them between two fingers. Jolyne’s eyes widen. “What?” Makima smiles, and this time it is not soft at all. “Did you think control only applies to people?” Jolyne rips the threads back with all her force, nearly stumbling. “You can control my Stand?” “I can control what belongs to you if you think of it as part of you.” Makima inclines her head. “Your power is still something you use. Something that can be directed.” Jolyne’s face hardens into something furious and brilliant. “Then you’re gonna have to work harder than that.” She lunges. The fight explodes through the corridor in a storm of impacts and impossible angles. Jolyne’s string whips around railings, ceiling beams, and ankles, turning the prison itself into a weapon. She slides under a guard, snaps a cable around a hydraulic door, and slams it shut inches from Makima’s shoulder. Makima moves with uncanny calm, stepping through chaos as if the world politely parts for her. And every time Makima speaks, someone nearby stiffens. A guard reaches for his weapon, then lowers it. A technician forgets to run. A prisoner in a nearby observation cage kneels without knowing why. Jolyne sees it all. The pattern. The sickness. Makima does not command with force alone. She makes obedience feel inevitable. That is what scares Jolyne most. Because fear can be punched. Fear can be outsmarted. But inevitability is a cage built inside your own head. Jolyne grits her teeth and slashes a string across her own forearm, sending a strand of Stone Free into the air behind Makima. It weaves around the corridor camera conduit, then knots around a steel bracket, anchoring itself through a blind spot. Jolyne yanks, hard enough to rip a support panel free. The ceiling grid collapses between them. Makima is briefly obscured by dust and falling metal. Jolyne grins fiercely. “Gotcha.” When the dust settles, Makima is standing on the other side of the wreckage, head slightly tilted. No blood. No visible injury. Not even a wrinkle in her suit. Jolyne’s grin dies. Makima smiles again, small and patient and absolutely maddening. “You’re learning,” she says. “That’s good.” Jolyne’s fists shake. Then she sees it—one of the fallen guards dragging himself backward, eyes wide, reaching for the emergency trigger on the wall. His mouth moves silently. Not for help. For warning. The guard’s gaze is fixed not on Makima, but on Jolyne. Because he knows. Or thinks he knows. And suddenly Jolyne understands something terrible: Makima hasn’t just been controlling people in this prison. She has been arranging the entire place around the idea that resistance is useful only until it becomes predictable. A test. A selection. Jolyne’s voice drops to a razor’s edge. “You brought me here on purpose.” Makima’s expression does not change. That is answer enough. Jolyne’s blood turns to ice. This wasn’t a meeting. It was a trap designed to see what she would do under pressure, how her Stand behaves, how much she’ll break before she bends. Makima wanted to measure her. Like a weapon. Like a future problem. Like an animal worth keeping. Jolyne straightens slowly, fury settling into something colder and more dangerous. “You know what?” she says. “You can keep your prison. You can keep your little obedient freak show. But if you think I’m gonna let you put a leash on me, you are so fucking dead.” Makima watches her with what might be genuine fascination. Somewhere above them, a camera clicks back to life. The red light blinks once, steadier than before. Makima glances up at it, then back to Jolyne. “I look forward to seeing you try.” Jolyne bares her teeth. Stone Free blooms around her like a storm made of silk and knives. And in the heart of the supernatural prison, with alarms screaming and the cameras flickering in Makima’s wake, two monsters who wear human faces prepare to tear the facility apart before one of them can decide the other belongs on a leash.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding within the crossover premise. The author successfully captures both Jolyne's hot-headed, suspicious nature and Makima's manipulative, falsely pleasant demeanor. The prison setting feels appropriately supernatural and oppressive, with effective worldbuilding that establishes the facility as a place for containing dangerous supernatural entities. The characterization is particularly strong. Jolyne's voice comes through authentically - her immediate distrust, foul language, and combative attitude all ring true to the source material. Makima's controlled, soft-spoken manipulation paired with her underlying menace is well-executed, especially in moments like her casual control of the guards and the creature. The power dynamics between them create genuine tension. The prose quality is generally high, with vivid imagery and strong atmospheric details. Descriptions like "a cathedral built for monsters" and "floodlights that turn the night white as bone" establish mood effectively. The action sequences are well-choreographed and easy to follow, particularly the climactic confrontation where Jolyne uses her Stand creatively. However, the piece suffers from some pacing issues. The middle section drags somewhat as it establishes Makima's control abilities, and some exposition feels heavy-handed. The dialogue, while generally strong, occasionally becomes overly explanatory, particularly when characters spell out their abilities or motivations too directly. The ending builds to an appropriate climax but stops just short of resolution, which works for the prompt but leaves the reader somewhat unsatisfied. The revelation that Makima orchestrated Jolyne's presence feels somewhat predictable, though it's executed competently. The writing occasionally veers toward purple prose, particularly in action sequences, but generally maintains good control. The supernatural elements are integrated naturally without feeling forced or over-explained. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 5.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 6.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-████ **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** SCP-████ is contained in Temporal Anomaly Wing-3, Room 14, under standard anti-memetic observation protocols. All personnel assigned to SCP-████ are to undergo daily cognitive screening before and after contact. Research logs, camera feeds, and note paper used in SCP-████’s suite are to be archived separately and cross-referenced by the Temporal Anomalies Division. No writing implements are to be left unsecured in the cell. SCP-████ is permitted a single monitored journal under the supervision of Dr. Lasky, who is to collect and review all entries every twelve hours. If handwritten notes appear on SCP-████’s desk, the area is to be isolated immediately, and all security footage from the prior ninety seconds is to be flagged for frame-by-frame analysis. If notes contain operational details not yet known to staff, they are to be treated as possible precognitive contamination until verified. --- **Addendum 1: Initial Incident Report** I first found the note at 06:13. I had entered my office ten minutes earlier to review interview transcripts from SCP-████-2, a contained entity suspected of low-grade mnemonic interference. My desk had been clear. I remembered that distinctly, because I had stacked three folders on the left side and a mug of coffee on the right, and because the room had felt ordinary in that annoying, temporary way all Foundation offices did. Then I looked down, and there was paper in the center of the desk. No sound. No movement. No sign of entry. The security camera mounted in the corner had produced a ten-second smear of static exactly at the moment I later estimated the note appeared. The handwriting was mine. > **DON’T TRUST THE CAMERA.** > > **IT HAPPENS AFTER THE GLITCH.** > > **YOU ARE ALREADY LATE.** I remember laughing when I read it. Not because it was funny, but because the alternative was to admit that my own handwriting had materialized on my desk without any measurable cause. I carried the note to Dr. Lasky and asked whether SCP-████-2 had somehow reached me through the interview glass. She told me that was impossible. Then she asked, carefully, whether I had slept. I had slept fine. I had not. --- **Addendum 2: Subsequent Notes** The notes continued to appear at irregular intervals, always on my desk, always during brief camera interference lasting between four and twelve seconds. The interference was identical in every case: a white block of static followed by a single warped frame in which the desk appeared empty even when I knew the note had already been there. The second note was shorter. > **DON’T LET THEM SAY “BREACH CONTAINMENT.”** > > **THAT’S THE TRIGGER.** That phrase bothered Operations. It had not yet occurred anywhere in our schedules, and the current containment status of SCP-████-2 was stable. We increased surveillance on both my office and the entity’s chamber. The third note arrived at 14:02. > **I HEARD IT IN THE WALLS BEFORE IT HAPPENED.** > > **NOT A BREACH. A RECOGNITION EVENT.** > > **THE GLASS IS LYING.** By then, I had started photographing each note before touching it. In every image, the paper appeared normal. In every camera recording, there was a gap where normal should have been. The Foundation’s temporal analysts called it a “localized observational discontinuity.” Security called it “the thing in the room with us.” I called it evidence. Then I began to wonder whether evidence was something I could still recognize. --- **Addendum 3: Interview with Dr. Lasky** *Interviewed: Dr. [REDACTED]* *Interviewer: Dr. Lasky* *Foreword:* Conducted after note six. **Lasky:** You’ve been checking the notes against your own handwriting samples? **Me:** Yes. **Lasky:** And? **Me:** It’s mine. **Lasky:** That doesn’t prove— **Me:** I know. But it’s mine. **Lasky:** Do you remember writing them? **Me:** No. **Lasky:** Have you considered the possibility that SCP-████-2 is manipulating your perception? Inducing auto-scripted output? We know it has some weak suggestive properties. **Me:** You think I’m writing these under compulsion. **Lasky:** I think we have to consider it. **Me:** Then explain the camera glitching. **Lasky:** We’ve considered a correlation, not causation. **Me:** The note said the glitch would happen. **Lasky:** It was a vague statement. **Me:** “It happens after the glitch.” That is not vague. At this point I became aware that my hands were shaking. I had not noticed until she placed a hand over mine on the table. That brief touch, more than the notes, made me feel profoundly unreal. **Lasky:** If this is temporal, why would your future self write to you in fragments? **Me:** Because by the time I got here, I was already too far gone to be coherent. **Lasky:** That isn’t a diagnosis. **Me:** No. It was a warning. --- **Addendum 4: Containment Breach Prediction** The fourth note was the first to contain a time. > **19:40. EAST CORRIDOR.** > > **WHEN YOU HEAR THE SECOND ALARM, DO NOT OPEN THE DOOR.** > > **I KNOW YOU WILL WANT TO CHECK ON HIM.** > > **DON’T.** At 19:40, the east corridor alarm sounded. At 19:41, the internal door to SCP-████-2’s chamber reported an unauthorized pressure change. At 19:42, two guards requested permission to enter and confirm the entity’s status. I ordered the corridor sealed. No breach occurred. That was the problem. The note had not described a breach. It had described my response to the possibility of one. The containment systems remained intact. SCP-████-2 remained in its cell. No escape, no violence, no damage. And yet the note’s last line had been underlined twice in a pressure-heavy stroke I recognized with a cold certainty I could not explain. > **THAT WAS THE FIRST ONE.** I did not ask what “first” meant. I was too afraid of the answer. --- **Addendum 5: Notes Become Less Legible** By the seventh note, my future self had started to sound frightened. > **THEY’RE GOING TO SHOW YOU THE OTHER FILE.** > > **IT ISN’T TRUE.** > > **IF YOU SEE YOUR NAME ON THE WALL, DON’T READ IT OUT LOUD.** The eighth note: > **I CAN’T REMEMBER WHICH VERSION KILLED HER.** > > **OR IF SHE DIED.** > > **OR IF SHE WAS EVER BORN.** The ninth note was almost entirely crossed out. > **[illegible]** > > **DON’T LET THEM PUT THE MASK ON ME AGAIN** > > **I KNOW IT’S NOT A MASK** > > **I KNOW IT’S ME** > > **I KNOW IT’S NOT ME** The Foundation ran a battery of tests. Temporal residue scans were inconclusive. Psychological stress tests indicated elevated dissociation but no evidence of deliberate fabrication. Hallucination screening returned normal. A redacted consultation with the Antimemetics Division suggested that the notes “behaved like messages from a self-consistent causal loop contaminated by hostile interpretive pressure.” I did not understand that phrase. I understood the feeling of reading it and realizing I no longer trusted the shape of my own thoughts. I started keeping my hands flat on my desk so I would know if I moved them. I stopped drinking coffee because I feared the cup might be empty in a way that would become significant later. I began checking the clock every minute, then every few seconds, then not at all, because I could not tell whether time was advancing or merely rehearsing. Then came the note that made me ask for sedatives. > **IF YOU HEAR ME SAY “OPEN THE ARCHIVE,”** > > **IT IS NOT ME.** > > **IF I SAY “DON’T OPEN IT,”** > > **IT MIGHT BE.** --- **Addendum 6: Final Recorded Note** The last note appeared at 03:17. The camera glitched. When the image returned, the note was there, and so was a second page beneath it, one I had not seen before. My handwriting had become jagged, as though written by someone trying to imitate panic from memory. > **I THINK THIS IS THE PART WHERE YOU REALIZE THE BREACH WAS NEVER IN THE BUILDING.** > > **IT WAS IN THE MOMENT YOU STARTED READING ME.** > > **NO—LISTEN—** > > **THE ENTITY ISN’T IN THE CELL.** > > **I AM.** > > **OR I WAS.** > > **OR I AM THE THING IT MADE TO KEEP YOU HERE.** > > **IF THE CAMERA BLANKS, DO NOT LOOK AT YOUR REFLECTION.** > > **IT HAS BEEN ANSWERING BACK FOR DAYS.** > > **I AM SO TIRED.** > > **I DON’T KNOW WHICH OF US WROTE THIS.** Below it, on the second page, were four words, written smaller and so forcefully that the paper had torn. > **PLEASE TRUST ME THIS TIME.** --- **Addendum 7: Research Conclusions** No conclusive determination was ever reached. The Temporal Anomalies Division could not confirm that the notes originated from a future iteration of my person. The chain of custody, camera discontinuities, and absent intermediate evidence made verification impossible. The Behavioral Health Division could not demonstrate that SCP-████-2 or any other contained anomaly had caused the events through direct psychological manipulation. SCP-████-2 maintained its silence throughout the incident period and displayed no measurable emission of anomalous effect beyond baseline. I was temporarily removed from active duty pending review. I objected to the review. I insisted that the notes were real. Then I began receiving them in the interview room. No desk. No office. No camera—only a ceiling lens that always glitched at the exact instant the paper appeared in my hand. The last note I received was unsigned. > **IF YOU ARE READING THIS IN A REPORT,** > > **THEN YOU SURVIVED LONG ENOUGH TO BECOME THE FIRST VERSION.** > > **DO NOT ASK WHERE THE OTHERS WENT.** > > **YOU ALREADY KNOW.** I have not written anything since. I still don’t know whether these notes came from my future self, from SCP-████-2, or from whatever part of me the Foundation has not yet learned how to classify. I only know that when the camera glitches, something arrives. And that every time I read it, I become less certain I was the one who picked up the paper.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the SCP format and successfully creates an unsettling psychological horror narrative. The author skillfully blends the clinical documentation style typical of SCP reports with first-person narrative sections, creating an effective contrast that heightens the uncanny atmosphere. The structure is well-executed, moving from standard containment procedures through increasingly disturbing personal accounts. The progression from rational skepticism to paranoid uncertainty feels organic and believable. The researcher's voice remains consistent throughout, maintaining the professional tone expected of Foundation personnel while gradually revealing mounting psychological strain. The temporal anomaly concept is handled with impressive restraint. Rather than over-explaining the mechanics, the author allows ambiguity to drive the horror. The notes themselves are effectively written - they feel authentically desperate and fragmented, with the handwriting deterioration serving as a visual metaphor for mental decline. The dialogue in the interview section feels natural and serves multiple purposes: advancing plot, revealing character psychology, and maintaining the clinical atmosphere. Dr. Lasky's responses feel appropriately professional yet concerned. The imagery is subtle but effective, particularly the recurring motif of camera glitches and the physical details of note appearance. The author avoids purple prose while still creating vivid, unsettling scenes. However, some elements feel slightly predictable within the SCP genre - the "is it real or psychological manipulation" question is well-trodden territory. The ending, while atmospheric, doesn't quite achieve the devastating impact it seems to reach for. The piece succeeds in creating genuine uncertainty about reality, which is the core strength of good psychological horror. The clinical format serves the story rather than constraining it, and the first-person perspective effectively draws readers into the researcher's deteriorating mental state. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 15.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-████ **Object Class:** Keter **Special Containment Procedures:** All reports of unmarked interior doors appearing in abandoned structures, condemned buildings, sealed commercial properties, and otherwise unoccupied facilities are to be escalated immediately to Site-19 Anomalous Infrastructure Response. Local law enforcement, municipal agencies, and private contractors are to be redirected with standard cover stories involving unsafe construction, mold remediation, or asbestos abatement. If the door is encountered in complete darkness, personnel are advised that a faint glow may be visible seeping from the edges of the frame. This glow is not to be interpreted as electrical leakage, light pollution, or heat. No attempt is to force entry without authorization from at least two Level 4 personnel and one representative of the O5 Council. Any recovered keys, hinges, locksets, labels, architectural fragments, paper records, or media sourced from beyond SCP-████ are to be transferred to the Records and Information Security Administration immediately. Due to repeated evidence of unauthorized high-level access, all O5 Council travel logs, biometric records, and off-site movements are to be audited weekly. This requirement is not optional. Under no circumstances are Foundation personnel to follow entities resembling hazmat or decontamination teams into SCP-████-related structures unless accompanied by armed containment teams and a Class-III navigational anchor. These entities have not been confirmed to be human. --- **Description:** SCP-████ refers to a series of identical unmarked doors, each appearing without warning in abandoned buildings worldwide. The doors are always placed flush against interior walls, regardless of the surrounding architecture. They are constructed of white-painted steel with no visible handle on the outer side, no manufacturer’s marks, and no identifying signage. When observed in complete darkness, a faint pale glow is often visible escaping through the seams of the frame. Opening any SCP-████ instance from the correct side reveals an interior space that is not consistent with the volume of the building containing it. The threshold opens into a massive archive of indeterminate extent containing stacked shelves, suspended catwalks, rolling ladders, index corridors, and chambers whose geometry is non-Euclidean. Distances within the archive cannot be mapped reliably. Corridor lengths change when unobserved. Stairwells terminate in reading rooms above or below their expected locations. Some sections appear to fold through themselves. The archive contains vast quantities of documents, media, index cards, sealed boxes, data spools, wall-mounted plaques, and bound volumes. Many records describe a civilization or global administrative order predating current human history by an unknown period. The records indicate this civilization possessed anomalous infrastructure, global archives, and containment procedures similar in principle to modern Foundation methods. Numerous documents reference a “containment failure” of unspecified origin, after which the civilization ceased functioning as a planetary system. The exact nature of the failure is absent from all records recovered to date, though repeated references imply it was widespread, self-amplifying, and possibly linked to the archive itself. Most concerningly, recovered materials contain repeated references to the Foundation under the designation **“The Custodial Directorate”**, a name never used in official records. Personnel rosters, security clearances, memos, and incident reports found within SCP-████ include entries matching current Foundation personnel. Several files are dated between 200 and 900 years in the past and describe those personnel as already employed in roles identical to present assignments. The archive is also subject to ongoing removal activity by unidentified entities. These entities consistently resemble hazmat technicians in full-body protective equipment: yellow, white, or charcoal suits; respirators; gloves; and opaque face shields. Their insignia are absent or deliberately obscured. They are seen transporting shelves, crates, file cabinets, and document bundles deeper into the archive or through auxiliary doors that vanish after passage. When approached, the entities abandon materials and flee with notable speed, navigating the archive as if familiar with its changing layout. Recovered footage additionally confirms that one or more members of the O5 Council have entered SCP-████ without authorization on multiple occasions. --- ### Exploration Log ████-A **Exploring Team:** Dr. Soria, Agent Venn, Researcher Hale, MTF Eta-10 escort **Location:** Abandoned municipal records annex, ██████, Poland **[00:01]** A door matching SCP-████ description appears in a sealed archive sub-basement during a power outage. Faint glow visible around edges despite no light sources in room. Door opens inward. Interior is a broad archive corridor extending beyond flashlight range. **[00:07]** Team enters. Floor is marble; walls lined with metal shelving and wooden card catalogues. Air is cool, dry, and smells of paper, ozone, and antiseptic. Labels on nearby drawers are in several extinct languages. **[00:13]** Researcher Hale locates a binder marked: **“CUSTODIAL DIRECTORATE — ACTIVE PERSONNEL / EUROPEAN NODE.”** Inside are personnel files. Three names match current Foundation staff, including Dr. Soria’s predecessor and Agent Venn’s father. Dates on pages range from 1712 to 1829. **[00:18]** Dr. Soria discovers a document excerpt: > **ARCHIVAL MEMORANDUM 4-19-88** > _If the containment lattice fails at the planetary level, initiate withdrawal of all record strata not essential to continuity. The archive must survive the civilization. The civilization is not guaranteed to survive the archive._ **[00:21]** Audio picks up distant rolling carts. Lights appear at corridor intersection. Team takes cover. **[00:22]** Three entities enter view. All resemble hazmat workers. Suits are scuffed, patched, and covered in white residue. One carries a hand truck stacked with index boxes. Another wheels a cabinet on castors. Third appears to be inspecting wall labels. No visible faces. Movement is purposeful and coordinated. **[00:23]** Agent Venn calls out. Entities stop. All three turn simultaneously. The nearest drops the cabinet. The entities retreat at high speed, maintaining formation. They disappear around a bend that is later found to connect to a dead end. **[00:31]** Team recovers dropped index cards. One card reads: > **SUBJECT: FOUNDATION, CURRENT ERA** > **Note:** They have resumed the old architecture. They do not remember the previous one yet. > **Status:** Council-level contamination possible. > **Action:** Continue removal. Avoid direct contact. Do not allow them to see the missing shelves. **[00:34]** Entry terminated when corridor lighting reversed and the archive floor developed a slope toward a lower shelf level not present moments earlier. Team withdrew. --- ### Recovered Document Excerpt 1 > **PERSONNEL FILE: SORIA, ELENA** > **Rank:** Assistant Archivist, Custodial Directorate > **Clearance:** Vault Black / Segment 13 > **Assignment:** Monitor cyclical institutions; suppress redundant memory drift; maintain continuity of names. > **Notes:** Subject exhibits stable recognition of post-failure protocols. Recommend promotion upon next recurrence. > **Date:** 14 Winter 1776 --- ### Recovered Document Excerpt 2 > **INCIDENT SUMMARY: GLOBAL CONTAINMENT FAILURE** > At 03:11 local standard, all seal systems in twelve capitals reported simultaneous breach, not by intrusion but by internal disagreement. Archival doors began appearing in uninhabited structures. Entire districts were converted into records storage. The failure was not the release of a contained thing. It was the loss of the agreement that containment existed. --- ### Exploration Log ████-C **Exploring Team:** Dr. Soria, Agent Venn, O5-7, O5-9, two unidentified security personnel **Note:** Log recovered from sealed camera buffer; entry undocumented in mission records. **[00:02]** Team exits through SCP-████ into a chamber already marked with Foundation-style wayfinding tape, but using obsolete nomenclature. O5-7 states, “This floor was supposed to be inaccessible.” **[00:09]** Camera captures framed portraits mounted in a hall of records. Several portraits depict staff in historical dress. One is labeled **“O5-9 / Provisional.”** Another bears Dr. Soria’s name. Dates span 1600s to present. **[00:12]** O5-7 is heard saying, “We removed the wrong index.” **[00:14]** Security personnel open a file cabinet and find a travel docket signed by O5-9 authorizing entry to SCP-████ “for continuity review.” Signature appears genuine. Metadata indicates the docket was created fourteen years before the Foundation’s founding. **[00:15]** O5-9 orders camera off. Recording continues regardless. **[00:19]** A hazmat-suited entity is observed dragging a row of shelves through a side passage. It pauses, apparently noticing the team. It drops the shelves and retreats. The following exchange is audible: O5-7: “They know us.” O5-9: “No. They know what we were called.” --- ### Addendum ████-1 Following interrogation, O5-7 denied unauthorized entry. O5-9 neither confirmed nor denied participation, stating only: “Some archives are not discoveries. They are returns.” Further scans of SCP-████ have identified a chamber labeled **“FOUNDATION / FUTURE HOLDING”** containing empty folders with current personnel names, including several still living. In one folder, a note written in handwriting matching O5-9 reads: > **DO NOT LET THEM LEARN THE FAILURE WAS ADMINISTRATIVE.** Analysis is ongoing. --- ### Addendum ████-2: Incident Report At 02:14 UTC on 2026-██-██, all known SCP-████ instances briefly emitted identical light visible through closed doors even in daylight. Witnesses reported the glow as “thin” and “paper-white.” For 17 seconds, every connected archive door in containment sites worldwide opened inward by approximately two centimeters. No breach occurred. Inside each doorway, personnel heard the sound of file boxes being moved and multiple voices saying, in various languages, the same phrase: **“The archive preserves what the world cannot.”** Immediately afterward, all active SCP-████ instances ceased glowing. One hour later, a new personnel file appeared in Site-19 records, assigned to **O5-██**, dated 231 years in the future. The file was blank except for a single line: > **AUTHORIZED VISITOR: PENDING.**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving together multiple complex narrative threads. The author successfully captures the bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP entries, maintaining consistency throughout the technical language, containment procedures, and exploration logs. The piece excels in world-building and conceptual creativity. The idea of a cyclical civilization where the Foundation exists under different names across time periods is genuinely intriguing and well-executed. The archive's non-Euclidean properties are described with appropriate scientific detachment, and the mysterious hazmat entities add an effective layer of tension and mystery. The exploration logs feel authentic to the SCP format, with realistic team dynamics and procedural elements. The recovered documents are particularly well-crafted, especially the personnel files that mirror current staff but are dated centuries ago. This creates an unsettling temporal paradox that drives the narrative effectively. However, the piece suffers from some pacing issues in the middle sections. The multiple exploration logs, while individually strong, create a somewhat repetitive structure that could have been streamlined. The revelation about O5 Council involvement feels slightly rushed and could have been developed more gradually. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity while providing enough resolution to feel satisfying. The final addendum with the synchronized door opening and the future-dated personnel file creates an effective sense of ongoing mystery without over-explaining the phenomenon. The prose is generally clean and professional, though occasionally veers toward exposition-heavy passages. The dialogue in the exploration logs feels natural and serves the story well. The piece successfully balances technical documentation with narrative intrigue, creating genuine unease about the Foundation's own history and purpose. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 14.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 87.5
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The first thing they told her at Landing House was not to look surprised. The second thing was not to wander. The third thing, given in the same tone as the first two, was that the people of Aster Reach were “divergent in morphology and custom” and that this divergence had been “managed toward stability.” Managed. As if a river could be managed into patience. Envoy Sera Venn stood at the window of her assigned chamber and looked down at the city in the amber dusk. From above, Aster Reach seemed built from repeated arguments: low stone terraces, narrow bridges, roofs of translucent green shell, and beyond them the long market lanes where lamps were being lit one by one. The air carried salt from the inland sea and something sweeter, resinous, unfamiliar. A briefing packet lay open on the table behind her. It had said that the colony’s human populations had split over generations into distinct lineages: the Basin Line, short-limbed and broad-chested, adapted to the lower pressure of the salt flats; the Ridge Line, narrow and dark-eyed, living in the high dry districts; the Shoreborn, taller, with webbing between fingers and a tolerance for damp cold; and smaller communities whose histories were still categorized as “transitional” or “unconfirmed.” The packet had also said that the divisions were “largely ceremonial” and “politically nonrigid.” Sera had spent enough time in government to know what that meant. At the evening reception, the Minister of Settlement had spoken for twenty minutes about unity while standing with one foot on a brass threshold marked by the old household sigils of his own line. A woman from the Hill Water Board had not once removed her gloves. Two men in the colonial uniform had bowed to each other with the same precision and the same distance one might use to handle a blade. Sera listened, asked the right questions, and watched. When the reception broke up she excused herself, declined the escort assigned to her door, and left by the side stair before anyone could object. No one stopped her. That, too, was information. The streets were narrow but not crowded, lit by hanging lamps shaded with woven reed. People passed in small currents, each current with its own rhythm. Some wore the severe, layered coats of the upland districts; others had loose open sleeves and bare throats, skin gleaming with salt. She saw a child carrying a bundle of fish scales as if they were coins. She saw an old man whose eyes had a pale sheen, almost translucent, and a woman with a throat ringed in silver wire who moved with the slow grace of someone built for thin air. There were no obvious signs of panic, no militarized checkpoints, no the brittle glare of a city under siege. Only the ordinary work of life. The market lay under a long canopy of sailcloth stretched between stone pillars. The smells reached her before the lights did: fermented fruit, oil, brine, spice, and the warm mineral scent of dye vats. Stalls ran in careful rows. One sold tiny metal tools, each wrapped in leaf fiber. Another sold bundles of dried sea grass. A child with one arm shorter than the other was arguing over the price of sweet cakes in a tone so brisk and fluent that Sera nearly smiled. Then she stopped at a fabric stall. The cloth hung in long lengths from crossbeams, stirring in the evening draft like banners from a country she had never visited. Some were woven with a tight, almost granular texture; others were soft and loose, patterned in bands of deep blue-black, rust red, and a green so dark it appeared to swallow light. The dyes were not Earth dyes. She knew that at once. One cloth shone with a coppery sheen like mica; another held a violet so rich it looked bruised and alive. The merchant, a woman with broad hands and a face marked by faint silver scars at the temples, watched her with open curiosity. “You’re from offworld,” she said. Sera did not reach for her badge. “Is it that obvious?” “Your shoes,” the woman said. “Also your eyes are trying very hard not to be your eyes.” Sera laughed despite herself. The woman held up a length of cloth threaded with black and gold. “This is from the low marshes. Woven by hand. Dyed with lake moss and ashflower.” “Beautiful.” “Too beautiful for the governor’s wife,” the merchant said. “Too costly for the dock clerks. So it waits.” “For whom?” “For whoever knows that waiting is also a trade.” Sera looked again at the fabric. “Your markets are segregated?” The woman’s mouth twitched. “That’s the word you people use when you think there should be a wall. We use the word ‘aligned.’” “Aligned by lineage?” “By water rights, by marriage contracts, by which lungs can bear which stairways, by who wants to eat first and who wants to go home before the lamps burn low.” She folded the cloth with practiced hands. “Lineage is only one of the old ways we sort ourselves. Sometimes it matters. Sometimes it’s the least interesting thing in the room.” Sera wanted to ask more, but another customer approached, a Ridge Line boy with a messenger pouch and the careful, steady gaze of one used to being underestimated. He paid in clipped notes and did not look twice at the envoy. She moved on through the market, buying nothing, listening. In one lane she found a tea house where Basin laborers and Shoreborn clerks sat at adjacent tables without drama or invitation. In another, two women with the same family marks on their wrists sat apart by choice, each attended by her own kin. There were social distances everywhere, but they were not the clean geometry of the briefings. They bent. They overlapped. They were negotiated in glances, in seating, in who touched whose shoulder and for how long. It was, Sera thought, not stability. It was a living arrangement. By the time she returned to Landing House the sky had gone violet-black, and the corridors smelled faintly of polish and electric current. She had not intended to be gone so long. That fact did not improve matters. Command was waiting in her quarters. Not a projection, not a message cube. An actual officer, senior and impatient, wearing the crisp gray of Colonial Coordination. His nameplate read HALDEN. “You were observed leaving the reception without escort,” he said. Sera removed her coat slowly. “Observed by whom?” “By personnel whose job it is not to answer you.” His voice was controlled, which made it worse. “You were briefed on security. You were briefed on local sensitivities. You were not authorized to conduct an informal survey.” “Then it was an excellent survey,” she said. Halden’s face tightened. “Do not play clever. You are here to reduce tensions.” “I can’t reduce what I haven’t understood.” “You can understand from reports.” “No,” Sera said, and surprised herself with the firmness of it. “I can’t.” For a moment he only looked at her. Then he crossed to the table, touched the data panel, and brought up a seal-marked portrait. “The Minister of Science,” he said. “You will request a meeting at 0900. The title is ceremonial. The office is political. He is one of the principal gatekeepers in the eastern bloc. He likes to believe himself underestimated. That is a useful belief. You will speak with him. You will not improvise beyond your mandate.” “What is his name?” Halden shut the panel with a sharp gesture. “You will be given the name. Learn this now: he will test you. He will expect the offworlder to be either dazzled or offended. Do neither.” “And if he offends me?” “Then remember that offense is a luxury. This meeting is to establish whether you can be trusted to speak for more than the polished phrases in your file.” He turned to go, then paused at the door. “Also,” he said, without looking back, “stop going into the city alone. This isn’t a holiday.” When he was gone, Sera sat down on the edge of the bed and thought of the market woman’s scarred hands. Waiting is also a trade. At 0900 she was admitted to the Minister’s office, a room set high in the civic tower with windows of smoked glass and shelves full of symbolic instruments that were presumably meant to suggest science: brass astrolabes, mineral cores in polished cases, a constellation model hanging above the desk. The man behind the desk had the easy posture of someone who had spent his life in rooms where other people were careful. Minister Rellan, the escort announced. Rellan rose. He was not old exactly, but seasoned in the way old knives are seasoned: by use, by polish, by the habit of being held correctly. His hair was silver at the temples, his hands long and unadorned. His eyes assessed her in one glance and then, more interestingly, pretended not to. “Envoy Venn,” he said. “You came without your ceremonial outriders. Either you are brave or your staff is incompetent.” “A bit of both, Minister.” He smiled faintly. “Good. Sit.” She did. He did not begin with the crisis. He began with weather, with sea level, with transit schedules, with an anecdote about a transport delay on the western line. Only after several minutes did he say, casually, “You went to the market.” It was not a question. “Yes.” “You saw the fabrics.” “Yes.” “And what did you conclude?” Sera folded her hands. “That your official briefings are incomplete.” “Of course they are incomplete. Briefings are not life.” “They omitted the degree to which your populations intermix.” “They intermix when useful.” “They intermarry, trade, share households, share work.” “Sometimes.” “More than ‘sometimes,’ Minister.” He leaned back. “You’re here to address tensions. If you want to flatter yourself by discovering that a colony is made of people rather than categories, you may do so. But if you’re honest, you’ll admit that categories are what make negotiations possible.” Sera met his gaze. “And if categories harden into borders?” “Then you have conflict,” he said, as if reciting a child’s lesson. “If you came for innocence, you came to the wrong planet.” She let that pass. “The market I walked through,” she said, “was not organized by simple lineage. The seating in the tea house wasn’t either. There are obligations, affinities, and exclusions layered over one another. Your people have built a social architecture more complicated than the administration understands.” Rellan’s fingers tapped once against the desk. “Our people built this colony. Then built it again when the first system of governance failed. Then again when the inland droughts came. Complexity is what remains after survival.” “Who gets to speak for it?” He smiled now, fully. “Ah. There it is. The offworld question.” “And what is the local answer?” “That depends who is asking.” Sera sat very still. “Then ask me something.” He regarded her a moment, as if deciding whether she had passed a preliminary test or merely requested the harder one. “At market,” he said, “did anyone bow to you?” “No.” “Did anyone avoid you?” “Yes.” “Did anyone sell to you?” “Yes.” “Then you have your answer. They understand how to survive strangers.” He let the silence widen. Sera said, “You’re trying to measure whether I can be manipulated.” “No,” Rellan said. “I am trying to measure whether you can bear ambiguity without immediately trying to stamp it into doctrine.” She considered him. “And?” “Not enough evidence.” He stood, walked to the window, and looked down over the city. From this height the market was only a pattern of light. “You will meet with the eastern bloc council tomorrow,” he said. “You will listen more than you speak. If they ask whether Earth intends to ‘correct’ our divergences, you will say that Earth intends to maintain treaty obligations and open channels for mutual exchange.” “That is the approved line.” “Yes,” he said, and glanced back at her with a glint of dry amusement. “Sometimes approved lines are merely honest ones in uniform.” He turned from the glass. “One more thing, Envoy.” “Yes?” “Do not mistake our survival for consent.” Sera rose. “I wouldn’t.” “No,” he said. “I think perhaps you wouldn’t.” When she left the office, the corridor felt different. Not safer. More legible, and therefore more dangerous. She thought of the cloth stall, of pigments no Earth chemist had ever cultivated, of the market woman’s remark that waiting was a trade. In the evening, back at her quarters, Sera opened her packet again and made notes in the margins until the page filled with questions the briefings had not asked. Below the window, the city brightened as lamps came on in sequence, district by district, like a message being written in light. She had come to address tensions. She began, at last, to understand that tension was not the problem. It was the medium.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Ursula K. Le Guin's literary style, successfully capturing her anthropological approach to science fiction, her measured prose rhythm, and her focus on cultural complexity over action. The author has created a believable alien world where human divergence feels organic rather than contrived, and the social structures are presented with the kind of nuanced observation that characterizes Le Guin's best work. The narrative structure is well-executed, moving logically from briefing to exploration to confrontation to reflection. Sera's character development is subtle but effective - she begins as a dutiful envoy following protocol and evolves into someone willing to challenge assumptions and seek deeper understanding. Her transformation feels earned through her experiences rather than imposed by plot necessity. The dialogue is particularly strong, especially in the exchanges with the market woman and Minister Rellan. These conversations feel natural while serving multiple purposes: revealing character, advancing plot, and exploring themes. The Minister's testing of Sera is handled with sophistication, avoiding heavy-handed exposition while revealing the political complexities of the situation. The world-building is exceptional, presenting a colony where human adaptation has created not just physical differences but complex social arrangements that resist simple categorization. The author avoids the trap of making these differences either utopian or dystopian, instead presenting them as practical adaptations with their own internal logic. The prose style successfully emulates Le Guin's voice - measured, observational, with moments of poetic insight that never feel forced. Phrases like "built from repeated arguments" and "waiting is also a trade" demonstrate the kind of metaphorical thinking that characterizes literary science fiction. The piece maintains thematic coherence throughout, exploring questions of cultural understanding, the limitations of official knowledge, and the complexity of human social organization. The ending's insight that "tension was not the problem. It was the medium" provides a satisfying intellectual resolution that feels earned. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Le Guin pastiche becomes slightly self-conscious, and some descriptions that border on being overly crafted, though these never cross into purple prose territory. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 18
