+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 78.0
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By 8:12 a.m. I had learned three important things about New Orleans: 1. It is hot enough to make you feel personally insulted. 2. Everything smells like coffee, frying oil, river mud, or stale beer, sometimes all at once. 3. If you look lost, somebody will either ignore you, pity you, or try to sell you something that may or may not be legal. I was, in fact, all three varieties of lost. I stood on a corner near some cracked sidewalk and a church that looked older than the idea of bad decisions, hugging my backpack like it might grow teeth and defend me. My phone said 12% in the top corner, which felt less like a battery percentage and more like a countdown to being truly, catastrophically alone. No charger. No home. No plan beyond the one I’d made in a panic at 1:00 a.m. with a shaking hand and a backpack full of stupid optimism. I unlocked the screen anyway and stared at the map for the fiftieth time. The blue dot drifted around like it was drunk. Helpful. “Okay,” I muttered to myself. “Great. Love this for us.” A streetcar bell clanged somewhere down the block. People moved past me with that aggressive city speed that makes you feel like a decorative object. Tourists in bright shirts. Two women carrying coffee. A man in a Saints cap dragging a cart full of aluminum cans. Somewhere a motorcycle roared like it had something to prove. My stomach was doing this hollow, traitorous thing. I hadn’t eaten since, I dunno, yesterday afternoon? I’d had nerves for breakfast and maybe one packet of crackers from a gas station, which should not count as sustenance unless you’re a raccoon. I picked a direction more out of stubbornness than confidence and started walking. I told myself I was fine. Actually, no—I told myself I was not fine, but that was not the same as doomed. There’s a difference. Fine is a lie. Doomed is a performance. I was somewhere in the exhausted middle. The neighborhood shifted around me as I walked. French Quarter-ish, I think? Or maybe close enough that the buildings were doing that picturesque peeling paint thing that probably costs millions. Balconies with ironwork curling like black lace. Green shutters. Windows reflecting the sky so hard it felt like the city was staring back. I passed a bakery and the smell hit me like a physical object. Butter. Sugar. Warm bread. My body basically tried to drag me through the glass. I dug in my pocket for the few crumpled bills I had left. Enough for one pastry, maybe. Enough to keep me from collapsing dramatically on someone’s steps. I went in. The bell over the door jingled. Inside, it was cool and dim and full of cinnamon and people who looked like they belonged somewhere. I did not. The woman behind the counter gave me a look that was both sharp and not unkind. “Mornin’,” she said. “Morning,” I said, trying for normal and landing somewhere near haunted. “What can I get you?” I glanced at the pastries like they were written in a foreign language. “Uh. Something that won’t make me pass out.” She snorted, which I respected immediately. “That narrows it down. You want sweet or savory?” “Surprise me.” “Dangerous answer in this town.” She handed me a warm ham-and-cheese thing the size of my face and a cup of water without me asking. I almost cried about it in public, which would’ve been humiliating, so I looked down hard and focused on paying. “Thanks,” I said. “Mm-hm.” She watched me a second longer than necessary. “You runnin’ from somethin’, baby?” I nearly choked. “No.” That was such a lie it basically stood up on its own. Her eyebrows lifted just enough to tell me she knew it too. “Well,” she said, “whatever it is, don’t bring it into my shop.” “Fair.” I ate outside on the curb because dignity was already a lost cause and because the little park across the street looked like the kind of place where people pretended not to see each other. Which, honestly? Ideal. Halfway through my pastry, my phone buzzed. My whole body went cold. Mom. I stared at the screen until it went dark again. No voicemail. Just a missed call and then, a minute later, a text that read: Where are you My throat tightened so hard it hurt. There it was. The feeling I’d been trying not to feel since last night. Not guilt exactly. More like being split in half and expected to walk around normal anyway. I typed: Safe Then deleted it. Typed: Don’t worry Deleted it too because that was insane. She was definitely already worrying. Probably furious. Probably crying. Probably calling everybody she knew in Tennessee and making my life a searchable database. I put the phone away before I could do something even stupider and stared at my shoes. “You look like your soul just got audited.” I looked up. A kid—maybe my age, maybe a little older—was leaning against a lamppost across the little park, watching me with open curiosity and the kind of caution people wear like armor. Brown skin, black hair under a battered cap, backpack slung over one shoulder. Their jeans were ripped at the knee and their T-shirt had some faded band logo I didn’t recognize. They looked like they’d either been awake for three days or were having an especially committed relationship with sleep deprivation. “Excuse me?” I said. They grinned. “You heard me.” I hated that I smiled back a little. “Who are you?” I asked. “Depends who’s asking.” I blinked. “That is the worst answer imaginable.” “Yeah, but it’s versatile.” They crossed over and stopped a few feet away. Close enough to talk, far enough not to feel threatening. That should not have impressed me as much as it did. “I’m Noah,” they said. Then, after a beat: “Not, like, my real name. But close enough for strangers.” “Taylor.” “Also probably not your government name.” I huffed a laugh before I could stop it. “Yeah. Close enough.” Noah tilted their head, taking in my backpack, my too-thin hoodie, my probably-shell-shocked face. “You got anywhere to be?” I thought about lying. Thought about saying yes, and then maybe somewhere safe, maybe anywhere, maybe I have a life, thank you. Instead I said, “No.” Their expression softened in a way that made my chest ache for no reason I wanted to examine. “Same,” they said. That was when I got nervous. Not because they seemed dangerous. Because they seemed too much like me. That’s a different kind of risk. The kind you don’t recognize until you’re already standing in it. Noah jerked their chin down the street. “There’s a place on Dauphine that does cheap breakfast if you’re desperate. Also coffee that’ll put hair on your chest.” “I’m good on the hair.” “Okay, fair.” We started walking, side by side but not too close. The city around us got louder as the morning deepened. A bus exhaled at the curb. Somewhere behind us, a trumpet started up, bright and lonely and impossible to ignore. After a minute Noah said, “You’re not from here.” “Is it that obvious?” “You have the face of somebody who has never once had to worry about a parade blocking traffic.” “That’s a strangely specific insult.” “It’s a talent.” I looked over at them. “You always talk like this?” “No,” they said. “Only when I’m trying not to ask personal questions.” “Ah.” “Yeah.” We walked a little farther in silence. I could feel the questions building in my own head, stacking up against my ribs. Why are you here? Where are your parents? Are you safe? Are you lying to me? Are you running? Are you like me? The last one hit hardest. I hated how badly I wanted the answer. At the corner, Noah paused and glanced both ways, even though the street was basically empty. “So,” they said, “you want the honest version or the polite version?” “Of what?” “This city.” I snorted weakly. “Do I get a choice?” “No.” “Honest.” Noah’s face changed. Not much. Just enough to show me there was a different person under the easy grin—someone older, or sadder, or both. “Honest version is: people will ignore you if you want them to. That’s good. It’s bad. Sometimes it’s both.” I nodded. “And,” they added, “if you’re broke, alone, and look young, don’t trust anybody who seems *too* helpful.” I felt my stomach drop a little. “Why?” “Because the city has saints and monsters, and a lot of them wear the same face.” We stood there in the heat while that sank in. Then Noah said, lighter, “But mostly? Keep your bag on your front in crowds. Don’t flash your phone. And if somebody calls you ‘baby’ in a way that makes your skin crawl, leave.” That got a real laugh out of me, small and ugly and relieved. “Noted.” “Good.” They shoved their hands in their pockets. “You got somewhere to sleep?” The question landed hard. Too hard. I looked away. “Not yet.” Noah nodded like they understood more than they were saying. “There’s a shelter near the river. Couple blocks from here. Rules suck, but it’s a roof.” I swallowed. “You know it?” “I know of it.” That answer should’ve scared me. It did, a little. But not enough to walk away. “Why are you helping me?” I asked. Noah’s mouth tightened in a way that said I’d hit something tender. “Because somebody helped me once.” That was all they gave me. Which was enough, somehow. My phone buzzed again. I flinched so hard Noah noticed. “Family?” they asked quietly. I looked at the screen. Another text from Mom. Please answer. We can talk. My eyes burned. Of course they could talk now. After everything. After the words I’d said and the faces they’d made and the awful silence that followed. After I’d left because staying felt like being erased in slow motion. “I don’t know,” I said, and hated how small I sounded. Noah didn’t push. “You don’t have to know today.” That should have been a simple sentence. It wasn’t. It hit like somebody opening a window in a room I’d forgotten had air. We reached the breakfast place, which turned out to be a narrow storefront with mismatched chairs and a handwritten menu taped to the glass. The kind of place that had seen better days and didn’t care. I liked it immediately. Before we went in, Noah stopped me with two fingers lightly touching my sleeve. “You can say no to anything,” they said. “To me, to them, to whoever. Don’t let this city teach you otherwise.” I stared at them. Then, because I was me and apparently incapable of acting normal under any circumstance, I said, “You always say the weirdest heartfelt stuff.” “No,” Noah said, deadpan. “I’m emotionally unavailable.” I laughed again, and this time it didn’t sound like breaking. Inside, the bell over the door chimed, and the smell of coffee hit me like a promise. I still had 12% battery. Still had no map that made sense. Still had a family somewhere back east and a life that felt like it had been cut loose and left floating. But for the first time since I got dumped into New Orleans like unwanted luggage, I wasn’t entirely alone. Which was terrifying. And, worse—if I was being honest, and I was trying to be—kind of hopeful.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and authentic voice work, successfully capturing the disoriented, vulnerable state of a transgender teen runaway in New Orleans. The first-person narration feels genuine and age-appropriate, with Taylor's internal monologue balancing cynicism, fear, and fragile hope in believable ways. The writing excels in several key areas. The voice is consistently maintained throughout - Taylor's observations are sharp, self-deprecating, and tinged with the particular brand of defensive humor that rings true for a traumatized teenager. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development well, particularly in the exchanges with Noah, where subtext carries much of the emotional weight. The author skillfully uses concrete details to ground the reader in New Orleans without resorting to tourist-brochure descriptions. The characterization is nuanced, especially in Taylor's complex emotional state. The protagonist isn't simply a victim or a hero, but a confused kid making impulsive decisions while grappling with rejection and identity. Noah is introduced with appropriate mystery while avoiding the trap of being either too helpful (suspicious) or too damaged (melodramatic). The pacing works well, moving from immediate survival concerns to tentative human connection without rushing the emotional beats. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - Taylor remains in a precarious situation but has found a small measure of hope and companionship. This feels earned rather than artificially optimistic. However, the piece has some weaknesses. Certain phrases occasionally veer toward trying too hard for cleverness ("dignity was already a lost cause," "soul just got audited"). While the voice is generally strong, there are moments where the internal monologue becomes slightly overwrought, particularly in the more philosophical observations about the city. The structure follows a somewhat predictable arc - lost kid meets other lost kid, they bond over shared circumstances. While executed well, it doesn't break much new ground narratively. Some of the descriptive passages, while evocative, occasionally slow the momentum. The piece successfully adheres to most instructions, delivering the requested word count, setting, character dynamics, and emotional complexity. The mixed tense approach works naturally within the first-person framework. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the third year after the Collapse, everyone had agreed on two things. First, that civilization had not so much fallen as it had been quietly, rudely pushed down a flight of stairs by a man in a hard hat. Second, that if you survived long enough, you would eventually start talking to a stuffed koala as if it were sensible company. Midas sat on an upended milk crate in the shell of what had once been a grocery store, cleaning a knife that had, in better times, been used for slicing fruit and in worse times, been used for slicing problems. Across from him sat Chewy. Chewy was animo-tronic, which had once meant he could wag his little plush tail, blink, and say things like “Hug me!” in a voice intended to produce consumer loyalty and mild emotional dependence. Now his fur was dusty, one ear was stitched back on, and his plastic nose had the solemn sheen of a saint’s relic. The real miracle, however, was his eyes. They flickered in colors depending on his emotional state, which had been a novelty in the before time and was now useful in the same way a barometer is useful on a ship that is already on fire. His eyes were amber, which meant caution. Midas followed their glow to the map he had spread over his knees. It was a hand-drawn thing, copied from three other hand-drawn things, and showed the surrounding district in the vague confidence that only the nearly illiterate can manage. “So,” said Midas, because someone had to break the silence and he had learned early that if you let silence hang around too long in the world after the fall, it started making suggestions. Chewy’s eyes blinked from amber to pale blue. Thoughtful. Encouraging, or at least pretending to be. “So,” Midas repeated. “We go to the old municipal archive, retrieve whatever records are still in there, and return without being eaten, shot, boiled, recruited, or accidentally married.” Chewy emitted a small plastic whirr. His eyes turned green. “Ah,” said Midas. “Optimism. That’s how you know we’ve both gone mad.” The archive was a day’s walk east, assuming one did not become distracted by one’s own mortality. It sat in the center of the drowned district, where the old city had once maintained a proud alliance with gravity and drainage. According to rumor, the archive still held technical plans, route maps, and a sealed cabinet of pre-Collapse batteries, which in practical terms meant it was either the most valuable place for miles or the easiest place to die for miles. Midas tapped the map. “We get in. We take the documents. We maybe find battery cells. We come home. Straightforward.” Chewy’s eyes flashed red. Midas narrowed his own. “You don’t need to look at me like that. I know ‘straightforward’ is a loaded word. I’m using it in a metaphorical sense.” Chewy’s eyes flicked to yellow. “Yes, yes. ‘Metaphorical’ may also be a loaded word. You’re very clever for a plush toy.” Chewy made a tiny affirmative chirp, which was one of the more irritating things about him. It suggested agreement in a tone that implied he had been born wiser than everyone else and was simply waiting for the universe to catch up. They set out shortly after dawn, passing through the usual geography of human collapse: cracked roads, leaning billboards, and houses with plants growing through them as if nature had found the whole arrangement faintly embarrassing and wanted to tidy up. A windmill turned somewhere on the ridge, though it was less an instrument of power than an extended argument with the air. Midas had just enough food, two waterskins, and a map made by a man who had clearly interpreted distances emotionally. Chewy rode in a sling against his chest, his soft weight oddly comforting. This was not because he was warm. He had all the warmth of a decorative pillow. It was because in a world where everything seemed capable of becoming a problem, a koala whose eyes changed color was at least honest about it. The first sign that the mission was going sideways arrived in the form of the bridge. Or rather, the lack of a bridge. Where the bridge should have been, there was a gap over the river and a sign reading DANGER, which in the old world would have been a warning and now functioned more like a proverb. Midas looked at the missing span. “Well,” he said, “that complicates matters.” Chewy’s eyes went white with what looked suspiciously like innocent curiosity. Midas pointed. “No, absolutely not. We are not ‘trying the rope bridge.’ We do not have a rope bridge. We have a rope. Singular. That’s a different social class of bridge altogether.” Chewy blinked pink. Amusement. Midas sighed, tied the rope to a broken bollard, and slung himself across the gap on a rusted maintenance cable that had been attached, in some forgotten act of optimism, to the bridge’s remains. He made it halfway before the cable gave a noise like a drunk violin and dropped him into the river with a splash of cold, humiliating water. Chewy, mercifully, remained attached to his chest. “I hate you,” Midas informed the sky. Chewy’s eyes turned green again, which Midas took to mean either encouragement or that the koala found this educational. He crawled out on the far bank, soaked, furious, and carrying a smell of algae that would later be described by a particularly rude scavenger as “prettily awful.” The archive itself was a concrete block with shattered windows and a front door hung half off its hinges. There were signs of habitation: smoke, tracks, and a bicycle mounted on a pole as a warning to thieves. Somewhere inside, a voice was singing off-key about a woman and a mule, which was generally either a warning or a weather report. Midas crept to the side entrance. Chewy’s eyes shifted to amber. “Danger,” whispered Midas. Chewy gave the tiniest nod possible from inside a stuffed body. Then a movement in the alley behind him made Midas spin around too fast, strike his elbow on the wall, and inadvertently trigger the squeaky mechanism hidden in Chewy’s torso. The resulting sound—half chirp, half dying toy orchestra—brought three armed scavengers around the corner with the briskness of men who had heard a free meal being offered. There was no time for dignity. Dignity, in any case, had become a luxury item. Midas bolted through the archive door. The scavengers followed. Somewhere deeper inside, the off-key singer stopped singing. What ensued could not honestly be called a plan. It was more the collision of several bad decisions with an architectural error. Midas sprinted down a corridor lined with shelves and toppled filing cabinets, pursued by shouts, boots, and one deeply offended librarian of indeterminate allegiance. He swerved through a room full of maps, ducked under a swinging lamp, and crashed into a storage closet full of paper, which instantly filled the air with the smell of mold and old authority. Chewy’s eyes flickered red. “Don’t start,” Midas hissed, brushing paper off his face. “I know this is bad. I can tell by the man with the spear.” The closet door burst open. Midas threw a rusted chair, which was not an especially heroic weapon but did at least occupy one of the scavengers long enough for Midas to grab a satchel of documents and flee through the back stairwell. The stairwell collapsed on the second step. He fell through three feet of rotten planks, landed in a pile of broken office furniture, and discovered, with the kind of exhausted clarity only disaster can provide, that he had come to rest in the archive’s basement. The basement, as it happened, contained the battery cabinet. Also a generator. Also three survivors who had been living there in a mutually suspicious arrangement. Also, according to the handwritten note on the wall, a rat the size of a dog and a strict policy about noise. Midas raised one hand in an attempt at peace. One of the basement survivors raised a crossbow. Chewy’s eyes flashed bright blue. Midas took this, reasonably enough, to mean “run.” He did. The next few minutes became a theological debate between chance and gravity. There was a fire, started by an oil lamp knocked over in the struggle. There was shouting. There was a battery rack falling into a puddle with a blinding shower of sparks. There was the rat, which turned out to be exactly the size of a dog and considerably more motivated. Midas escaped through a side hatch, clutching the satchel, with Chewy jammed under one arm and his eyebrows singed into a permanent expression of mild surprise. He did not return home by the road. He returned home by the rain culvert, then the storm drain, then a maintenance tunnel full of bats and one remarkably judgmental corpse, and finally by way of the old tram line, which was supposed to be impossible because the trams had not run in decades. However, one of them was still attached to the cable, and while it was not moving under its own power, it did move when Midas, several cuts wiser and much less arrogant, accidently kicked the release mechanism while trying to climb out. So he arrived at home base in the most humiliating way possible: hanging by one hand from a tram car that lurched into the settlement’s eastern yard, trailing wet documents, a satchel of batteries, and a koala with flickering eyes that had gone from amber to gold. Gold, in Chewy’s case, meant triumphant. Or very amused. Midas dropped to the ground in a heap. Around him, the home crew stared, then began to applaud with the slow, respectful clapping reserved for idiots who had somehow come back alive. He groaned. “We got the records.” Someone yelled, “And the batteries?” Midas held up a soot-stained pouch. “And these.” Someone else said, “Didn’t you go for one day?” Midas looked at Chewy, whose eyes were now a gentle, infuriating green. “Yes,” said Midas. “And technically, it’s still morning.” Chewy gave a tiny satisfied chirp and settled against his chest like a victory that had been stuffed, stitched, and mildly haunted. Then Midas heard the archive smoke on the wind, the distant shouts of angry scavengers, and the unmistakable sound of something large and hungry following his trail. He closed his eyes. “Chewy,” he said. The koala’s eyes flickered bright red. “Next time,” said Midas, “we stay home and die of boredom like civilized people.” Chewy chirped, which in the circumstances was either agreement or the beginning of another disaster.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, philosophical observations, and ability to find humor in dire circumstances. The author effectively establishes the post-apocalyptic setting through clever details like "civilization had not so much fallen as it had been quietly, rudely pushed down a flight of stairs by a man in a hard hat," which perfectly encapsulates Pratchett's approach to describing catastrophe with understated absurdity. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose self-awareness about his predicament adds depth to what could have been a simple bumbling character. His relationship with Chewy is the story's strongest element - the animatronic koala's color-changing eyes serve as both a clever mechanical detail and an effective way to create dialogue-like exchanges without actual speech. The dynamic between them feels genuine and develops naturally throughout the narrative. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with numerous memorable phrases and observations that feel authentically Pratchett-esque. Lines like "if you let silence hang around too long in the world after the fall, it started making suggestions" demonstrate the author's ability to blend philosophical insight with humor. The pacing is well-managed, building from the deliberative opening through escalating complications to a satisfying conclusion. The mission structure follows the prompt requirements effectively, beginning with planning, proceeding through multiple complications (missing bridge, river dunking, archive chaos, basement survivors, fire, rat, escape), and returning home via an absurd route. Each setback feels organic rather than forced, and the accumulating disasters create genuine momentum. However, some elements feel slightly overwrought. Certain descriptive passages, while generally well-crafted, occasionally push toward purple prose territory. The ending, while satisfying, edges toward incongruent positivity given the severity of the disasters encountered. The story also shows minor signs of meandering in the middle sections, though this doesn't significantly impact overall coherence. The dialogue, though limited, works well within the constraints of having one non-speaking character. Midas's one-sided conversations with Chewy feel natural and reveal character effectively. The piece successfully avoids major tell-don't-show issues, generally allowing actions and details to convey information rather than stating it directly. Overall, this is a accomplished piece that successfully fulfills its brief while demonstrating genuine creative flair and technical competence. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 7 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 80.5
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Rain worried at the front window of the bookshop, turning the glass into a wavering sheet of gray. Beyond it, the narrow Cardiff street shone with wet cobbles and the blurred tails of passing buses. Inside, the bell over the door gave a frantic jangle as a man in a dark coat ducked in, breathless, hair mussed by wind and panic. “Closed?” he asked, already in motion, glancing back over one shoulder. “It very much is not,” said the woman behind the counter, without looking up from her ledger. “Though if you’re trying to rob the place, you’re a bit late. The till’s tragic.” He stopped. Then he turned slowly, as if the room itself had just suggested something rude about him. He was tall, broad-shouldered, recognizably handsome in that irritating, polished way that made other people look underdressed by comparison, even in a plain black coat and scarf. Not local-handsome. Cinematic handsome. The sort of face that belonged to billboards and press junkets and red carpets. He looked vaguely familiar to her in the way famous people did before the brain admitted the obvious. His mouth curved. “Well,” he said, with a voice smooth enough to be sold separately, “that’s reassuring.” She lifted her eyes at last. “It was meant to be.” “Then I’m relieved.” He glanced back through the pane again. “Are there people out there?” “Yes.” “Photographers?” “Yes.” “A lot?” She shut her ledger with deliberate care. “Enough to make me wonder what on earth you’ve done.” His expression flickered—wounded pride, quickly hidden beneath a grin. “Nothing,” he said. “Which is usually the problem.” “Is that so.” He took in the shop then: the leaning shelves, the old wooden ladder, the display of Welsh poetry near the window, the cat in the armchair by the radiator pretending not to be awake. His face brightened in a way that was probably practiced but still undeniably effective. “This is charming,” he said. “Very literary. Very… intimate.” “Thank you. We prefer our books alive and our customers quiet.” “That rules me out on both counts, I’m afraid.” “It does.” He blinked, then laughed under his breath as if not often corrected by women who wore spectacles and practical jumpers and looked entirely unimpressed by his existence. The laugh was warm, unforced. Annoyingly nice. “I’m Oliver Vale,” he said, offering a hand with the assurance of a man accustomed to being known. “And before you ask, yes, that Oliver Vale.” She looked at his hand rather than taking it. “Should I be impressed?” “You should at least pretend.” “Why?” He seemed momentarily at a loss, which transformed his face in a way that made him younger and less polished, more vulnerable beneath the expensive coat. “Because I’m hiding in your shop,” he said. “That’s not a reason.” “It’s quite a good one.” She leaned back against the counter. “I’m Siân.” “Just Siân?” “Most people manage it.” “Ah.” His smile tilted. “You’re one of those.” “One of what?” “The people who refuse to be charmed.” “I haven’t refused. I simply haven’t noticed any charm worth refusing.” That earned him a hand to the chest, mock-wounded. “Ouch.” “You’ll recover.” “I might not.” “You’re an actor. I’m sure you can manage.” He paused, then let out a soft breath through his nose, studying her with new interest. “There it is again.” “What?” “Immunity. Fascinating.” She raised a brow. “Do you always speak like you’re in an interview?” “Only when I’m being judged in a cardigan.” “I’m not judging your cardigan.” “No?” “No. It’s the coat I dislike. The cardigan would suggest effort.” He laughed properly then, head tipping back a little, and the sound filled the quiet shop as pleasantly as the rain. To her own irritation, something in her chest gave a small, traitorous flutter. Before she could decide whether to resent it, the bell over the door gave another frantic jangle from outside. Oliver went still. She glanced to the window. Two figures in hoods and long lenses hovered on the pavement, peering in with the intense, predatory patience of people who had made a profession of ruining other people’s afternoons. “Right,” he said softly. “Those are definitely for me.” “Yes,” she said, dryly. “You’ve been spotted, Mr. Vale.” “I prefer Oliver.” “I don’t.” His eyes slid to hers. “That’s a shame.” “Not really.” He smiled, but it came a touch too quickly, as if he needed the smile to be returned to him. “Could I trouble you for somewhere less… visible?” “You’re already in the most visible part of the shop.” “I was hoping for a back room. Basement. Secret tunnel. Preferably something with dramatic possibilities.” “There’s no tunnel.” “No?” “It’s a bookshop in Cardiff, not a spy novel.” “Pity. You look like you’d know where to find one.” She gave him a look. “I’m beginning to think you say things like that to everyone.” “Only the interesting ones.” “That’s a very well-worn line.” “Yes,” he said, and for the first time his charm slipped just enough to reveal the nakedness beneath. “But it usually works.” She surprised herself by saying, “Perhaps people like being worked.” His eyebrows lifted. Good God, that had sounded worse than intended. His smile went slow and dangerous. “And what, exactly, are you implying?” “Nothing. I’m implying absolutely nothing.” “Mm.” He stepped closer to the counter, lowering his voice instinctively, as if the paparazzi might hear through glass and rain. “You do know you’re flushing.” “I am not.” “You are.” “I’m in a warm shop near a radiator.” “You’re flustered.” “I’m not.” “You are.” His grin softened, making him look suddenly, unfairly boyish. “That’s much better than indifference.” “If you say so.” “I do.” He rested his hands lightly on the counter, not touching her, but close enough that she noticed the clean line of his fingers, the faint scar at one knuckle. “Tell me the truth, Siân. You’ve heard of me.” “Yes.” “And you’re not impressed.” “No.” “Not even a little.” She held his gaze. “Should I be?” He looked startled—actually startled—then delighted in a way that seemed to come from genuine hunger rather than vanity. Like a man who had expected applause and had instead been handed a challenge. “Now that,” he said, “is rude.” “You broke into my shop. You may survive the insult.” His laughter was quieter this time. “Fair.” Outside, a camera flash strobed white against the rain. Oliver glanced toward it, jaw tightening. For a brief moment his face changed; the charm fell away and there was something raw and irritated beneath it, something like fear disguised as entitlement. “They’re relentless,” he muttered. “Because you’re famous.” “Because they’re vultures.” She folded her arms. “And because you keep giving them reasons.” His head snapped back to her, offended pride flaring. “Excuse me?” “You know exactly what I mean.” “That’s rich, coming from a woman who hasn’t offered me a cup of tea or a reassuring lie since I entered.” “Would tea make them go away?” “No.” “Then no tea.” He stared at her for a beat, then huffed a laugh despite himself. “You’re terrible.” “I’ve been told.” “But interesting.” “I suppose I’ll survive.” “You will,” he said, and something in his voice changed again, dropping the performance for a second too long. “You absolutely would.” The words landed between them with unexpected weight. Siân looked down at the ledger to busy her hands, annoyed at herself for noticing his tone, his mouth, the way the wet collar of his coat had darkened at the neck. “Well,” she said, more briskly than she felt, “if you’re staying, you’ll need to be useful.” “Useful?” “Yes. Since you’ve brought a mob to my door, you can at least shelve something.” He blinked. “You want me to shelve books.” “I do.” “I am a celebrated actor.” “And I am a bookseller in Cardiff with wet floors and no tolerance for drama. Shelve.” His expression was a picture—outrage, disbelief, and underneath both, unmistakable amusement. Then, to her astonishment, he laughed and took off his scarf. “All right,” he said. “What are the rules?” “The alphabet is a popular system.” “Very funny.” “I thought so.” He moved around the counter with the easy grace of a man who knew he was being watched and liked it, but not nearly as much as he liked being tested. He paused near the shelves, glancing back at her. “And if I do this,” he asked, “do I get tea?” “After.” “Any chance of biscuits?” “Not unless you prove competent.” He put a hand over his heart again. “Cruel woman.” Yet when she pointed him toward the Welsh-language fiction shelf, he set about sorting books with surprising concentration, the rogue’s grin fading into something thoughtful. He had beautiful hands, she noticed with immediate and humiliating clarity. He handled the books carefully, as though they mattered. After a moment, he said without looking up, “You’re still staring.” “I’m making sure you don’t mis-shelve Dylan Thomas.” “I’m an actor, not a barbarian.” “That is not always the same thing.” He looked up then, and the exchange of their gazes went still and electric. Rain ticked against the windows. Somewhere in the back of the shop, the cat yawned. Oliver’s smile returned, slower this time, less practiced and more real. “You know,” he said, “you’re the first person in weeks who’s looked at me like I’m not a disaster waiting to happen.” Siân meant to answer lightly. Something sensible. Something resistant. Instead she heard herself say, “You do seem to be one.” He laughed, but his eyes stayed on hers, bright and intent. “Yes,” he said softly. “But you’re not afraid of me.” “No.” “Pity.” She looked away first, infuriated by the warmth under her skin. “Keep shelving,” she said. Behind her, his voice dropped into something smooth as velvet and much more dangerous than his charm had been at the start. “Gladly, Siân.” And despite herself—despite the flashing cameras outside, the rain, the ridiculousness of him, the way her pulse had started behaving like an ill-trained thing—she felt the bookshop tilt, just slightly, toward the impossible.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates the requested Welsh setting (Cardiff bookstore), establishes the paparazzi-escape scenario, and develops both characters according to their specified archetypes. Oliver Vale emerges as the charming rogue with fragile ego seeking validation, while Siân embodies the seemingly immune bookstore owner who is inwardly affected despite her resistance. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Oliver's speech patterns reflect his performative nature and need for approval, while Siân's responses are clipped, practical, and deflating to his charm offensive. The banter feels natural and serves the dual purpose of revealing character while building romantic tension. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative descriptions like "Rain worried at the front window" and "the exchange of their gazes went still and electric." The author demonstrates skill in balancing dialogue with atmospheric detail and internal observation. However, the piece suffers from some overwrought moments, particularly in the final paragraphs where phrases like "smooth as velvet and much more dangerous" and "the bookshop tilt, just slightly, toward the impossible" veer into purple prose territory. The writing occasionally becomes self-conscious about its own cleverness. The character development feels authentic, particularly Oliver's vulnerability beneath his polished exterior and Siân's grudging attraction despite her resistance. The chemistry builds believably through their verbal sparring, and both characters remain consistent throughout. The pacing works well, moving from initial encounter through escalating tension to the moment of mutual recognition of attraction. The ending avoids being overly positive while still suggesting romantic possibility, which feels earned given the preceding interaction. Some minor weaknesses include occasional telling rather than showing (explicitly stating Siân is "flustered" rather than letting actions convey this) and a few moments where the dialogue becomes slightly artificial in service of wit. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Aya’s fingers found the iron ring and hesitated only a heartbeat before she pulled. The door sighed open on tarnished hinges, and cold breathed out of the underhold like something alive. Gas lamps guttered along the stone corridor beyond, their flames shivering in the draught and throwing long, jagged shadows over the floor. The light was poor, yellowed with age and soot, and it made the masonry look diseased—every damp seam in the wall a vein, every stain a bruise. Aya stepped through first, one hand tucked close to the hidden knife at her wrist, the other brushing the edge of her coat as if to steady herself. Behind her, Katra moved like a cat over wet slate, silent and broad-shouldered, while Simeon followed with the careful, almost scholarly tension of a man who distrusted every bolt, hinge, and lock he had not set himself. Too quiet, Aya thought. Far too quiet. The underhold should have been alive with the usual mire of guild business: whispered arguments, the scrape of boots, the clatter of tools, the stink of spilled gin and lamp oil, some watchman cursing over a jammed mechanism or a runner panting out a warning. Instead there was only the faint drip of water somewhere in the black below, and the whisper of the gas flames. Then a voice, dry as old paper. “Good evening, my dears. I do hate when children come visiting unannounced.” Aya stopped so abruptly Katra nearly bumped her. The corridor opened into the vault chamber beyond, a vaulted room of blackened stone and iron ribs, the floor inset with drain channels and marked by old salt lines meant to ward off things the guild liked not to name aloud. At the far end, beneath the largest lamp, stood the guild leader. She was very much not in a cell. Madame Morska wore her authority as if it had been stitched to her bones. Her coat was deep sea-green velvet cut with black braid, her gloves immaculate, her silver hair pinned severe beneath a hat with a narrow veil of mourning lace. One hand rested on the polished grip of a revolver. The other held a thin cane tipped in brass, though no one in the underhold mistook it for anything less than a weapon. Flanking her were four bodyguards in dark workmen’s coats, all of them armed with truncheons, pistols, and the hard-eyed patience of professional violence. Aya caught the glint of brass fittings, the oily sheen on their weapon-cylinders, the disciplined weight of men who trusted steel because it could be counted on to bleed. At the sight of them, Simeon’s breath shortened. Katra made a low sound in her throat. “Oh, that’s inconvenient.” Madame Morska smiled without warmth. “Katra. Simeon. And…” Her gaze slid to Aya, lingering just enough to be cruel. “A new face.” Aya felt the prickling heat of dread begin in her chest and spread outward. No. Not a new face. Not to her. But the underhold had never seen the face under her mask, and now, with one sentence, the entire rotten, fragile architecture of her double life seemed to creak. One of the bodyguards moved with stunning speed—no theatrical flourish, no wasted motion. Aya barely had time to draw breath before a hard arm hooked around her throat from behind and yanked her backward. The floor vanished beneath her boots for a sickening instant, and then she was dragged to one knee, the cold press of a revolver barrel biting beneath her jaw. “Don’t,” said the guard into her ear. His voice smelled of tobacco and machine grease. Aya’s hand snapped up, instinctively reaching for the dark seam between worlds, the place in her own blood where immateria answered when called. It was fear that unlocked it, always fear first. She felt the familiar chill stir—something vast and hungry and half-remembered. But Madame Morska lifted two fingers, and one of the other guards produced a narrow iron hoop etched with prayer-figures and binding runes, snapping it toward Aya’s wrist. The moment the metal touched her skin, the sensation of reach and pull vanished. Her stomach dropped. “No,” she whispered before she could stop herself. “Oh, yes,” said Madame Morska pleasantly. “We have rather a lot of experience with little scandals such as yours.” Aya’s masked face was wrenched sideways by the man behind her. Fingers found the strap at the back of her head and tugged. She twisted, but the revolver pressed harder against her throat. “Easy,” the guard said. “Hold still.” The mask came free. Cold air touched Aya’s cheeks. The world seemed to narrow to the sting of exposure, the humiliating sensation of being seen. She knew, even before the gas lamps caught her full face, what that reveal meant. Not merely the guild’s suspicion. Not merely arrest. Her mother’s hanging. The whispers in the drawing rooms upstairs. The sermons. The fear in the servants’ eyes. Magicka was outlawed because men had decided it was simpler to fear the unmeasurable than to understand it. And now the underhold would know. Aya, who had slipped among them in a velvet mask and borrowed swagger, was aristocracy’s daughter and the dead woman’s heir. Her face was a traitor. Across the chamber, Katra swore softly. Simeon went pale enough to match the limestone walls. Madame Morska looked at Aya with the faint, appraising interest one might reserve for a newly opened lock. “Ah. There you are.” Aya lifted her chin despite the hand on her throat. “You let us come this far.” “I did,” said the woman. “It was remarkably easy. One grows fond of watching ambitious young things mistake access for trust.” Simeon swallowed. “Madame, there’s been—” “There has been,” Madame Morska cut in, “a rather ill-advised attempt to rummage in my house while I was otherwise occupied. You may imagine my disappointment.” Katra’s eyes darted once, quickly, toward Aya. A question. A warning. Aya could feel the trap closing. The power vacuum had been a mirage. A stage set for fools. If the leader had been arrested, then who had returned in her place? Or had she never left? The thought chilled her more than the stone underfoot. The bodyguard tightened his grip and hauled Aya upright. Her boots scraped on the floor. She did not struggle; the iron band at her wrist had already done the work of making her helpless. To fight now would be only theatre, and doomed theatre at that. Madame Morska inclined her head toward the trio. “Bind them.” Two more men advanced with manacles and cord. Katra’s jaw set, but she did not lunge. Simeon’s hands came up slowly, grimly, as the cuffs closed around his wrists with a metallic click that sounded absurdly final. Aya watched it happen with a strange, floating detachment. She ought to be thinking of escape routes, of hidden blades, of the ward-lines below the floor. Instead she found herself thinking of her mother’s hands, ink-stained and clever, turning pages in secret by candlelight while the whole city pretended not to notice the woman in the attic. Aya had thought herself clever to imitate that quiet defiance. She had thought the guild a place where masks were merely another form of truth. Now she saw how little truth had protected anyone. Madame Morska walked toward her, cane tapping softly against stone. Up close, her face was lined and elegant and terrible, the sort of face that had watched men beg and found them tedious. “You must understand, child,” she said, almost kindly. “I have no fondness for traitors, nor for thieves who imagine themselves invisible.” Aya’s mouth had gone dry. “If you already knew, why wait?” The older woman’s smile sharpened. “Because I wished to see what you would risk when you believed the door open.” Katra made a low, angry noise. Simeon said, “Aya…” Aya did not look at them. She could not bear it—not their shock, not their likely betrayal, not the terrible fact that they had stood beside her through smoke and blood and back-alley bargains and now saw her as something cursed. Her mind raced, frantic and cold. Stay alive. That was the first commandment. Her mother had told her that once, years ago, when the city bells were ringing and the servants had been told not to ask why. Madame Morska paused before her. “So. Let us speak plainly. You have attempted to take my property, my authority, and perhaps my life. You have also, if I am not mistaken, been consorting with the very practice the Crown considers worth a rope.” Aya tasted iron at the back of her throat. “You can’t afford to hang me.” The chamber seemed to still. Even the bodyguards looked at her then, with that particular interest men reserved for either lunatics or useful prey. Madame Morska’s brows lifted a fraction. “No?” Aya forced herself to breathe. The bruise of the revolver barrel at her throat had faded, but the memory of it remained like a thumbprint. “I’m worth more breathing than dead. You know what I am. You know what I can do.” Her voice trembled once, infuriatingly, and she made it hard again. “And if the city learns who has me, they’ll come with warrants and iron and prayers. If the Crown wants the guild broken, they’ll use my name to do it.” A silence followed, thin and perilous. Katra stared at her as if seeing her for the first time. Simeon’s expression had gone to something close to grief. Aya swallowed and pushed on, each word costing her. “Keep me alive and you have a shield. A bargaining piece. A demonstration. Kill me, and you get a corpse. Kill me, and you get my mother’s story all over again, only louder.” Madame Morska watched her for a long moment, one gloved hand resting against the brass head of her cane. At last she said, “How very aristocratic of you, child. To turn your own hanging into a negotiation.” Aya’s pulse thudded in her ears. She could feel the immateria beyond the iron, vast and patient and unreachable, like a sea behind locked glass. She lifted her chin anyway. “I learned from the best,” she said. And in the gaslight, with the shadows climbing the walls like black water, the guild leader smiled as if she had just been offered an interesting new kind of knife.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric writing within the whalepunk fantasy setting. The author successfully establishes the gritty Victorian aesthetic through detailed descriptions of gas lamps, iron fixtures, and industrial elements, while weaving in the gothic horror elements through the underground setting and magical suppression themes. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Aya emerges as a complex protagonist - aristocratic yet rebellious, magically gifted yet constrained, desperate yet calculating. Her internal struggle between her privileged background and criminal activities creates genuine tension. Madame Morska is effectively portrayed as a cunning antagonist who has orchestrated the entire scenario, subverting the expected power vacuum plot. The dialogue feels authentic to the Victorian-inspired setting, with formal speech patterns that avoid feeling stilted. Madame Morska's lines particularly capture the menacing politeness of someone in absolute control. The exchanges between characters reveal personality and advance the plot efficiently. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from the ominous quiet of the entrance through the reveal of Morska's presence to Aya's unmasking and desperate negotiation. The author handles the capture scene without resorting to action sequences as requested, instead focusing on psychological tension. The worldbuilding elements integrate naturally - the iron binding that blocks magic, the fear of immateria, the class tensions between aristocracy and the criminal underworld. The magic system feels grounded in the world's logic rather than arbitrary. However, some descriptions occasionally veer toward the overwrought, particularly in the opening paragraphs where metaphors pile up ("masonry look diseased—every damp seam in the wall a vein, every stain a bruise"). While atmospheric, this sometimes slows the narrative momentum. The ending negotiation feels earned given Aya's established intelligence and desperation, though her sudden shift to confident bargaining after being thoroughly outmaneuvered requires some suspension of disbelief. The piece successfully avoids unearned optimism while setting up future conflict. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
A lazy ceiling fan barely stirs the smoky air inside the bar. It chops the haze into ragged ribbons above the room, pushing cigarette smoke from one dim corner to another without ever clearing it. The place calls itself the Cinder Lounge, which is generous. It’s a stained carpet, sticky tables, neon that buzzes like an insect caught in a jar, and a bartender with the expression of a man who has already been disappointed by every customer who has ever walked through his door. Spike Spiegel slides onto a stool like he’s late for a joke only he understands. He looks as if he belongs anywhere he can lean one shoulder against something and pretend the world is too tired to bother him. Tan suit, loosened tie, one hand in a pocket, a cigarette hanging from his mouth. He glances at the handwritten bounty notice nailed to the wall beside the cash register, then at the folded paper in his own hand. Same face. Same name. Same payout. He exhales through his nose. “Figures.” The bartender eyes him warily. “If you’re here for the bounty, you’re not the only one.” Spike’s mouth twitches. “Yeah, I noticed.” Across the bar, a woman in shorts so short they look like a dare is already halfway through a bottle of cheap whiskey. She’s sprawled with all the relaxed hostility of a loaded gun left on a table. Black crop top, dragon tattoo climbing her right arm, boots braced on the rung of a stool. Her hair hangs messy around a face that looks carved from trouble and sharpened on it. Revy doesn’t look over immediately. She takes another drink first, then lowers the bottle and says, “You got a staring problem, blondie?” Spike blinks once, slowly. “Only when something interesting walks in.” She finally turns. Her eyes sweep over him, measuring: suit, cigarette, too-calm smile, the lazy hands, the practiced slouch. The kind of man who pretends not to be dangerous until the room is already full of broken teeth. “Cute,” she says. “You always talk like that, or are you trying to impress me?” “Maybe both.” The bartender chooses that moment to set a fresh ashtray between them with the delicacy of a bomb disposal expert. “If you two are gonna shoot each other, do it outside.” Revy smirks into her glass. “Depends who gets the first shot.” Spike tilts his head. “I was thinking the same thing.” They both know the bounty poster without needing to look at it again: a courier-turned-arms broker named Halden Voss, holed up somewhere in the industrial maze on the edge of town. Worth enough to make a bad night better. Worth enough to make a good night worse. The kind of target people like them are always sent after because no one expects the hunter to care whether the prey is alive, dead, or lying. Revy drinks. Spike orders something strong and cheap. The bartender brings it with the look of a man serving judgment. For a while they don’t speak. The silence isn’t comfortable; it’s armed. Revy keeps one boot hooked around the stool rung, one hand draped near the inside of her jacket where the butt of a pistol is visible if you know how to look. Spike, for his part, looks half-asleep and fully ready to kill the first person who reaches for him. They both notice the same thing at the same time: the man in the back booth with the newspaper folded too neatly on the table. His shoes are wrong for the neighborhood. He keeps glancing up at them. Revy snorts. “We got tail.” Spike sips his drink. “Yeah.” “Your tail or mine?” He eyes her over the rim of the glass. “You got a lot of people watching you?” “Enough.” “Then maybe it’s ours.” She gives a sharp, humorless laugh. “Look at you, sharing already.” “Whatever happens, happens.” Revy’s brow lifts. “That’s the dumbest philosophy I ever heard.” Spike smiles around the cigarette. “Works for me.” The booth man rises too quickly. His jacket parts just enough to show the grip of a handgun. Then another man gets up from the far end of the bar, and another near the jukebox, and the bartender ducks behind the counter with the resignation of someone who knows exactly how this ends. Revy’s expression hardens into a grin. “Ah, hell. Ambush.” Spike slides off his stool. “We should probably leave before they get ideas.” Revy stands too, rolling her neck. “I was gonna say the same thing.” Then the first shot cracks through the bar. Glass explodes. Someone screams. Spike moves sideways with almost lazy precision, cigarette still pinched between his teeth, while Revy yanks her pistol clear and fires twice without seeming to aim. One attacker drops from the back booth, chair overturned under him. Another staggers, clutching his shoulder. Spike kicks a table into the path of two men rushing from the door, uses the momentary cover to draw, and fires with clean, economical bursts. He doesn’t waste motion. He never does. One man’s gun hand jerks wide, another slams into a wall and slides down it. Revy laughs, bright and wild. “Not bad, suit.” “Likewise, short shorts.” “Watch your mouth.” “Make me.” She does not, but the way her eyes flash says she’s considering it, and that thought is immediately interrupted by another volley from the remaining shooters. They move without discussing it, which somehow makes it feel like a conversation. Spike takes the left side of the room, popping from cover to cover with a boxer’s rhythm, one eye tracking angles, the other compensating with experience where depth perception fails. Revy claims the right side like it’s her territory, both hands occupied, both pistols spitting fire. She shoots from the hip, over her shoulder, through a mirror that shatters into glittering fragments. The bar becomes a battlefield of splinters, brass casings, and smoke. At one point Spike steps into a lane too soon and Revy’s shot cuts through where his head had been an instant earlier. He freezes just long enough to glance at her. She grins. “You’re welcome.” Spike’s smile turns razor-thin. “You almost hit me.” “Almost doesn’t count.” “Good to know.” He returns the favor a second later, knocking a gun out of a shooter’s hand just as the man tries to flank her. Revy sees it, and for half a second her expression shifts—not soft, exactly, but aware. Then it’s gone, buried under another string of profanity and gunfire. When the last of the ambushers falls or flees, the bar is a wreck. The ceiling fan still turns, patient as ever, nudging smoke through the wreckage. Revy lowers her pistols and exhales. “So. That was fun.” Spike checks his magazine, then pockets the empty one. “You have a weird definition of fun.” She reaches for her bottle, finds it shattered, and curses. He plucks a fresh cigarette from his pack and offers it. Revy stares at it, then at him. “What’s the catch?” “No catch.” “That’s what I don’t trust.” “Fair.” She takes the cigarette anyway, lets him light it. Their hands are close enough for a beat too long. Spike notices the calluses on her fingers, the steady way she holds herself despite the blood and noise and the fact that her pulse is probably still racing under the skin. Revy notices his missing depth in the way he looks at the room—slightly offset, always a fraction off from where a lesser man would stand. She files it away without comment. “Maybe we should finish the bounty together,” Spike says. Revy takes a drag and studies him through the smoke. “And split it?” “Unless you want to duel for it.” Her smile is slow and dangerous. “I do like a man with confidence.” He leans back against the bar, hands in his pockets, relaxed in the way only a genuinely lethal person can be relaxed. “You keep looking at me like that, I might start thinking this is personal.” “Don’t get ahead of yourself, cowboy.” That gets a small, amused huff out of him. “Too late.” They head out separately, naturally, because neither one of them is stupid enough to trust the other with their back. But they end up in the same parking lot, under a flickering sodium lamp, the air cool and metallic after the bar’s sour heat. The motel is half a mile away, all peeling paint and broken signage. They arrive there in silence, having tracked Voss to room 12 on the second floor by following a trail of burned-out headlights and one terrified informant. Spike takes the stairs without hurry. Revy takes them like she expects the building itself to attack. At the door, they pause. Inside: movement. A man breathing too hard. Probably armed. Probably desperate. Spike glances at Revy. “You want left or right?” She snorts. “You think I’m taking orders from you?” “I was hoping we could avoid shooting each other.” “Then stop talking like you know me.” He smiles faintly. “Too late.” Revy’s gaze lingers on his mouth for a fraction of a second, then sharpens again. “Fine. On three.” “Sure.” “One.” They both smile a little, each recognizing the other’s lie. “Two.” The room goes still. “Three.” The door bursts inward. Gunfire erupts instantly. Voss, panicked and pale, is behind the bed with a shotgun. Spike slides low, Revy angles high, and in the cramped room the fight becomes pure instinct. A lamp shatters. The mirror over the dresser dies in a spray of glass. Voss fires wild and misses both of them by inches. Spike disarms him with a swift Jeet Kune Do strike to the wrist; Revy’s pistol is at Voss’s temple before he can scream. And then they both realize they’re aiming at the same man from opposite sides of the bed, each with one gun up, one hand braced, their shoulders nearly touching, their breathing in sync despite themselves. Voss starts to sob. Revy doesn’t look at him. She looks at Spike. Spike looks back. The tension in the room tightens, not around the bounty but between them—hot, dangerous, and impossible to mistake. Revy’s mouth is set in that hard line that means she’s annoyed, interested, or both. Spike’s expression is unreadable, but his eyes are intent in a way he doesn’t bother disguising. For one suspended second, neither of them moves. Then Spike says quietly, “You gonna shoot him?” Revy’s lips curve. “You gonna make me?” The question hangs there, loaded with all the things neither of them says. Trust. Challenge. Hunger. The ugly little thrill of finding someone who can keep up. Spike lowers his gun first, just a fraction. Revy notices immediately. Her own gun stays up a heartbeat longer, then lowers too—but not all the way. “Don’t get any ideas,” she says. Spike’s smile is small, tired, and dangerous in the way a lit match is dangerous in dry grass. “Wouldn’t dream of it.” Neither of them believes him. Neither of them wants to. Outside, sirens begin to wail somewhere far off. Voss whimpers. The motel room smells like powder, sweat, and stale carpet glue. Revy and Spike stand in the wreckage, close enough to touch, far enough to deny it. Unresolved, armed, and very much alive. For now, that is enough.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character work within the crossover fanfiction framework. The author successfully captures the distinct voices and mannerisms of both Spike Spiegel and Revy, maintaining their canonical personalities while creating believable chemistry between them. The dialogue feels authentic to both characters, with Spike's laconic wit and Revy's aggressive edge coming through clearly. The narrative structure is well-paced, moving from initial meeting through confrontation to the climactic motel standoff. The action sequences are choreographed effectively, showing both characters' combat skills without becoming overly technical or losing narrative momentum. The author demonstrates good understanding of both source materials, incorporating character details like Spike's prosthetic eye and depth perception issues, and Revy's dual-wielding style. The prose quality is generally strong, with evocative descriptions that establish atmosphere without becoming overwrought. Lines like "The place calls itself the Cinder Lounge, which is generous" and "sprawled with all the relaxed hostility of a loaded gun left on a table" show skillful metaphorical language that enhances rather than distracts from the narrative. The sexual tension is handled with appropriate restraint, building through action and dialogue rather than explicit description. The ending maintains the unresolved nature requested in the prompt while providing satisfying closure to the immediate conflict. However, the piece occasionally veers toward purple prose, particularly in some of the more elaborate metaphors. Some transitions feel slightly abrupt, and while the characters are well-rendered, they don't evolve significantly beyond their established archetypes. The plot, while competently executed, follows fairly predictable beats for this type of story. The writing demonstrates professional-level craft in most areas, with strong character voice, effective action sequences, and atmospheric description. The dialogue crackles with tension and personality, and the pacing maintains reader engagement throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
