+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The red safelight made everything look like a memory that hadn’t happened yet. I stood at the sink with my sleeves rolled to my elbows, rinsing developer from a tray while Elliot hovered near the enlarger, long fingers tucked into the sleeves of his black thrifted sweater like he was trying to hide inside his own silhouette. The darkroom was warm and damp and smelled like chemicals and wet paper and the faint metallic tang of old pipes. Outside the thin walls, the art building had gone quiet hours ago. Inside, it felt like we were the only two people left on campus. Elliot looked especially unreal under red light. His hair, that soft black mess with the silver clip he’d found at a flea market, fell into his eyes whenever he leaned down to examine a negative. He wore those battered platform boots he somehow made look delicate instead of aggressive, and his nails were painted chipped charcoal. Every time I saw him, I had the same stupid thought: how can someone look that sharp and that gentle at the same time? For months I’d told myself I was just admiring his style. His composition choices. His ability to say one sentence in critique and somehow destroy everyone else’s work in the room. But that wasn’t the whole truth. I liked the way he moved carefully, as if he was always asking permission from the air around him. I liked that my usual instinct—to take up space, to speak first, to flirt like I was laying claim to a thing—had no obvious move with him. Elliot didn’t seem claimable. He seemed… unfolding. “Can you pass me the tongs?” he asked, voice low enough that I had to step closer to hear. “Here.” I handed them over, and my fingers brushed his wrist. He flinched, not away exactly, just startled, like contact still surprised him. That made something inside me soften and tighten at the same time. We were printing contact sheets for the final project, but the assignment had turned into an excuse for staying late, for the two of us orbiting each other in the darkroom’s narrow heat. We’d spent the last hour arguing lightly about contrast and grain, our shoulders occasionally bumping over the sink. Nothing obvious. Nothing I could point to and say this means something. And yet. Elliot lifted the tongs, checked the paper in the tray, then set it down with a little frown. “I think this one’s coming up too dark,” he murmured. “It’s not dark. It’s dramatic.” He gave me a look from under his lashes. “That’s your answer to everything.” “It usually works.” A tiny smile tugged at his mouth, and I felt it like a hand at the base of my spine. I turned back to the counter to keep from staring. That was the thing with Elliot: he made me want to be less armored, which was inconvenient because I liked being armored. I liked knowing what I wanted and how to get it. I liked clear lines. I liked when attraction had a shape I could name. With him, all my usual certainty had started to smear around the edges. Behind me, the tray clinked. “Dani?” I looked over my shoulder. “Yeah?” He was still facing the sink, but his posture had changed. Tighter. More deliberate, like he’d stepped onto a ledge in the dark and was trying to decide whether to jump. “I wanted to say something,” he began, then stopped. The darkroom hummed with the low mechanical breath of the ventilation fan. Water dripped somewhere. I set the tongs down slowly. “Okay,” I said, trying to make it sound easy. Casual. Not like my heart had started beating so hard I could hear it in my ears. He took a breath. “I think I’ve been… I don’t know. Questioning stuff.” “Stuff,” I repeated gently, because I could tell he needed the sentence to stay open. He huffed a tiny laugh. “Yeah. Stuff. Gender stuff.” The red light seemed to deepen. I felt, absurdly, like the room had tilted and I’d somehow stayed upright by accident. Elliot kept talking, words careful and halting. “I haven’t really told anyone. I don’t even know if there’s anything to tell, exactly. It’s just been getting harder to pretend I’m fine with… all of it.” He made a vague motion at himself, as if his body were a question mark he’d rather not gesture at directly. “The way people see me. The way I’m supposed to be seen.” I stayed very still. I wanted to interrupt and say you don’t have to be anything for anyone here. I wanted to tell him he could try on whatever he wanted in my presence. I wanted to ask a hundred questions and wrap him in all the certainty I owned. Instead I said, “Thank you for telling me.” He finally turned toward me. The expression on his face was so naked it almost hurt to look at. “That’s it?” “No.” My voice came out firmer than I intended, which was probably comforting in exactly the wrong way. I softened it. “I mean, that’s the beginning of it. You don’t have to explain it all to me right now.” His shoulders dropped a fraction, like he’d been bracing for impact and found none. “I thought,” he said, looking at the floor, “maybe you’d think I was being dramatic.” I almost laughed, but the sound died before it left me. “Elliot, you are the least dramatic person in this department.” That earned me a real smile. Small, quick, and devastating. Then I said the thing I’d been holding back because it mattered and because I didn’t want to make it about me, but it was about me too, in the strange way desire always is. “For what it’s worth,” I said, “you don’t have to look any one way for me to be interested.” His eyes snapped up. The room went very quiet. I felt heat crawl up my neck. So much for subtle. “I didn’t mean—” I started, then stopped, because I did mean it, and now that it was in the air I couldn’t pretend otherwise. Elliot’s mouth parted slightly. He looked more startled by my confession than by his own. “You’re… interested?” he asked. The directness of it hit me hard enough to make me almost laugh again. “Yes, Elliot. I have eyes.” He blinked, then actually smiled, but it was shy and uncertain, and that made my chest ache in a completely unfamiliar way. “I thought you just liked my jackets,” he said. “I do like your jackets.” “You’ve complimented them a lot.” “Because they’re excellent jackets.” He let out a breathy laugh, and then, because the universe apparently enjoys humiliating me when I’m vulnerable, I reached for the drying rack behind me and brushed against a stack of photo paper I’d forgotten was sitting too close to the open tray. The top sheets flashed under the safelight as I jostled them, and my stomach dropped. “Shit.” Elliot was already reaching, but it was too late. The exposed edges had caught enough stray light to ruin the whole stack. We both stared at the paper as if our disappointment might somehow reverse physics. “Well,” I said weakly, “that’s unusable.” He made a tiny sound that might have been a laugh or a groan. “I think we just sacrificed the project to the darkroom gods.” “Great. Excellent. Very on brand for us.” He looked at me then, properly looked, and something in his face shifted from nervousness to something warmer, braver. “We can print more tomorrow.” “Yeah.” Neither of us moved. The darkroom felt smaller now, more intimate, like the red light had turned into a held breath. I could see the pulse jumping in Elliot’s throat. He could probably see mine too, which was annoying. I leaned back against the counter, trying to reclaim my usual authority and discovering it had turned into something quieter. Less command, more invitation. “So,” I said carefully, “what do you want me to call this? Your questioning. Your stuff.” He considered that. I watched the thought cross his face, watched him test the idea like it might bruise. “Maybe just… not pretending,” he said finally. “For now.” Something tender opened in me so fast it nearly made me dizzy. “Okay,” I said. “Then we don’t pretend.” He nodded once, like he was trusting me with something fragile and real. I wanted to kiss him. The urge was immediate and sharp, but it wasn’t the same kind of wanting I usually felt—the kind that pushed, claimed, moved forward because it knew where it was going. This felt slower. More dangerous. Like if I moved wrong, I’d shatter the moment. So I didn’t move. Instead I asked, “Would it be okay if I asked you something?” He studied me. “Okay.” “What name do you feel like when you’re not performing?” His breath caught, and his eyes went glossy in the red light. He looked away, then back at me, like he was gathering himself from pieces. “Elliot,” he said quietly. “Still Elliot. I think. But maybe… not exactly the same way.” I nodded. “That makes sense.” And because I could see he needed it, because I needed it too, I let my voice go soft without losing its steadiness. “You don’t have to have it all figured out tonight.” He smiled, this time with real relief in it. “Good, because I definitely don’t.” The space between us changed after that. Not less charged—more. As if the honesty had tuned something in the room, made the silence hum with possibility instead of fear. He stepped closer to the counter, close enough that I could see the faint freckles across his nose, the careful line of his mouth. “You know,” he said, almost shyly, “you’re different than I expected.” I arched a brow. “That sounds dangerous.” “It’s a compliment.” “I know.” He looked down, then back up. “You’re intense. But not in a way that feels like… pressure.” That landed somewhere deep. I swallowed. “I can do pressure,” I said. “I just don’t want to use it on you.” His expression went soft in a way that made my whole body feel too awake. “I like that.” I let myself smile. “Good.” The darkroom was still a darkroom. We still had work to finish, ruined paper to replace, a final project to salvage. But something had shifted under the red light, something unnamed and alive. For the first time all semester, I wasn’t trying to figure out how to get Elliot to fit into one of my usual categories. I was just watching him become more himself, and realizing—terrified, thrilled—that whatever I was feeling had room to change too. Maybe that was what chemistry really was. Not certainty. Not conquest. Just two people standing in the dark, discovering what else they could be.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's perspective as requested, maintaining her assertive personality while showing how it's complicated by her attraction to Elliot and desire to respect his vulnerability. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, with the red light creating an intimate, confessional atmosphere that enhances the emotional revelations. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's voice feels authentic - confident yet uncertain when faced with unfamiliar emotional territory. Her internal struggle between her usual take-charge approach and wanting to give Elliot agency is well-rendered through both her thoughts and actions. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite being seen through Dani's perspective, with his tentative self-exploration feeling genuine rather than performative. The prose quality is high, with evocative imagery that serves the story rather than overwhelming it. Phrases like "looked like a memory that hadn't happened yet" and "how can someone look that sharp and that gentle at the same time" capture both the atmosphere and Dani's perspective effectively. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development, particularly in the vulnerable conversation about gender questioning. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension through small moments and physical proximity before the emotional revelation. The accidental exposure of the photo paper provides a nice moment of levity that doesn't undercut the emotional weight. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful without being unrealistically resolved, acknowledging that both characters are in a process of discovery. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain phrases occasionally veer toward the overly literary ("made my chest ache in a completely unfamiliar way"), and some internal monologue could be more subtle. The piece also relies heavily on internal narration, though this is somewhat justified by the first-person perspective and the characters' cautious approach to each other. The story successfully avoids common pitfalls around LGBTQ+ themes, treating Elliot's questioning with respect and avoiding both fetishization and oversimplification. The attraction between the characters feels organic and the complexity of Dani's feelings about gender and attraction is handled with nuance. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the time the bells near the Trevi Fountain began their lazy afternoon chime, Ben was standing at the curb with a guidebook open in both hands and the expression of a man who had already made several mistakes before noon. He was trying to read a map, trying to avoid a cluster of tourists, and trying not to feel foolish in a city that seemed determined to make him feel it anyway. “Scusi—” he began, just as a blur of black motorcycle and red scarf cut across his line of sight. He stepped back too late. The bike lurched, a wheel skidding against the stone. Ben made a sound that was half gasp, half apology, and the driver—helmet under one arm, dark hair escaping in impatient waves—threw a foot down and caught herself with the kind of infuriating elegance that made near-disasters look like choreography. “Ma sei matto?” she snapped. “Are you trying to die in front of the fountain?” Ben blinked. “I—I’m terribly sorry. I didn’t see—” “Clearly.” “I was just—” “Reading,” she said, glancing at the guidebook as if it had personally offended her. “In the middle of the street?” “Technically, I’m on the pavement.” “Ah, yes, technically. Rome loves technically dead people.” He flushed. “I’m Ben.” She stared at him, then at the fountain, then back at him. “Alessandra.” He tried the name carefully. “Ales-san-dra.” She grinned, quick and dangerous. “Not bad for a tourist.” “I’m not a tourist,” he said at once, then realized the absurdity of it. “Well. Not exactly. I’m here on sabbatical.” “Worse.” He laughed despite himself. It surprised him enough that he stopped. Alessandra noticed, her expression brightening with mischief. “You’re one of those,” she said. “Professor. Museum. Shoes too clean.” “I beg your pardon.” “British.” “Yes, but—” “Very bad at crossing streets.” He looked down, helplessly amused. “That may be true.” She tucked her helmet under her arm and leaned against the bike, which he now saw bore a colorful scratch along the side—an ugly, rainbow-slick scrape that caught the sun in little wounded flashes. “Who did that?” he asked before he could stop himself. Alessandra followed his gaze. “A wall. A bus. A very dramatic pigeon. Pick one.” “I’m sorry.” “Non ti preoccupare,” she said, then, with a flick of her wrist, shoved the guidebook shut and tapped it against his chest. “Come on.” “Come on?” “Since you almost got yourself flattened, I’m giving you a tour.” He stared. “You can’t be serious.” She mounted the bike with the easy confidence of someone who considered gravity a suggestion. “Are you always this slow, or only when frightened?” “I’m not frightened.” “Good. Then get on.” He looked around as if someone—anyone—might rescue him from this astonishing stranger. No one did. Tourists drifted around them in glittering confusion. A woman fed pigeons. Rome continued to be Rome. Ben swallowed. “I don’t think this is entirely sanctioned.” Alessandra’s smile turned sly. “That’s why it will be memorable.” --- They started in Trastevere, where she threaded the motorcycle through narrow streets that made Ben grip her waist with both hands and then apologize into the back of her jacket for gripping her waist with both hands. “Stop apologizing,” she called over her shoulder. “I’m English. It’s reflexive.” “Then break the reflex.” At a tiny bakery, she killed the engine and left him standing beside the bike while she went inside. He heard her speaking rapidly in Italian, heard a burst of laughter, then saw her emerge with two warm suppli wrapped in paper and an expression of pure triumph. “How did you do that?” he asked. “I smiled.” “That is not enough to procure food.” “In Rome, sometimes it is.” She handed him one. Their fingers brushed. The contact was small, but Ben felt it with embarrassing clarity. He bit into the suppli and, despite himself, made a low sound of approval. Alessandra’s eyes narrowed. “See? You trust me now.” “I trust the rice ball.” “Same thing.” They ate on a stone step in the shade, and for a few minutes she was almost still. Almost. Her knee bounced. Her gaze kept roaming the street, catching details, exits, possibilities. Ben noticed then that she moved like someone perpetually halfway into trouble and wholly unrepentant about it. “What do you do, when you aren’t nearly killing academics?” he asked. “Courier.” “Motorcycle courier?” “Do I look like I deliver flowers on a bicycle?” “No, I suppose not.” She looked at him sideways. “And you? Apart from reading guidebooks in traffic.” “Medieval literature.” “That sounds made up.” “It isn’t.” “It sounds like a punishment.” Ben smiled into the paper. “Sometimes it is.” “That’s sad.” “You say that as if you don’t enjoy making people miserable.” She looked delighted by the accusation. “I do enjoy many things.” “Like stealing?” The word slipped out before he could think better of it. Alessandra froze only for a fraction of a second—too quick for anyone else to notice, but Ben did. Then she laughed, a bright dismissal. “Maybe.” He should have been alarmed. Instead, he was more interested than he ought to have been. --- By late afternoon she had taken him to the top of Janiculum Hill, where Rome lay spread beneath them like a secret she was willing to share only in pieces. The city glowed gold and pink in the lowering sun. Church domes caught the light. Traffic hummed below like distant water. Ben stood beside her, out of breath from the climb. “You planned this,” he said. “I’m always planning things.” “This is not how a normal day in Rome proceeds.” Alessandra sat on the low wall, swinging one boot. “Normal is boring.” “You are going to be arrested one day.” “Only if I’m unlucky.” He looked at her, at the wind moving through her hair, at the scrape on her motorcycle below, at the careless ease with which she occupied the world as if it were hers by right. “You’re impossible.” “Yes,” she said. “And yet you followed me.” “I appear to have made a series of questionable decisions.” “Did you regret any of them?” Ben opened his mouth, then closed it again. The truthful answer made his throat feel oddly tight. She watched him too closely. “Ben,” she said, softer now, “you can say it.” He looked away at the city. “No.” Her smile was immediate, almost tender. “Coward.” “Probably.” “Good.” That made him turn. “Good?” “Men who aren’t cowards are usually boring.” He laughed, and she laughed with him, and the sound of it loosened something in the air between them. --- At sunset she dragged him into a tiny bar near Campo de’ Fiori where the owner kissed her cheek and called her “delinquente” with fond exasperation. Ben was given an espresso he had no right to drink at such an hour and a glass of something amber that smelled like oranges and fire. Alessandra slipped a small silver lighter from the bar counter and tucked it into her pocket with all the innocence of a saint. Ben saw it. He said nothing. Instead, he watched her lean one elbow on the bar, speaking in rapid Italian to the bartender, her face animated, alive in a way that made the rest of the room seem blurred around the edges. She was laughing when she noticed him watching. “What?” she said. “You look…” He searched for the word and failed. “Uncontainable.” She seemed pleased by that, though she covered it with a scoff. “That sounds like a problem.” “For whom?” “For anyone standing too close.” He considered that, then found himself smiling at the rim of his glass. “I’m beginning to think that may include me.” “Maybe,” she said. The ambiguity of it—her tone, her eyes, the almost-smirk at the corner of her mouth—made his pulse jump in a way he didn’t entirely like and definitely didn’t understand. Outside, the sky was bruising into purple. She finished her drink, stood, and motioned him toward the door. “One last place.” “Should I be worried?” “Always.” --- They ended up at the mouth of a quiet alley where an old arcade of ivy-climbed stone opened onto a view of the river. Alessandra killed the engine and swung off the bike. Ben dismounted more carefully, trying not to look as shaken as he felt. She removed her helmet at last, and there was something almost intimate in seeing her hair fall free, slightly wild from the ride, her face unguarded in the darkening light. For a moment neither of them spoke. Then Ben cleared his throat. “Thank you.” “For terrifying you?” “For today.” She tilted her head. “You liked it.” “I did,” he admitted. “Even the illegal parts?” He gave her a helpless look. “Especially those, perhaps.” That made her smile in a way he hadn’t seen before—less teasing, more private. She reached into her jacket, and for one alarming second he thought she might produce another stolen object, some new proof of her rule-breaking genius. Instead, she took out a folded paper receipt and pressed it into his hand. “What’s this?” “Your next reason to come back.” He unfolded it. On the back, in sharp black ink, she had written an address and a time. “What is this place?” “Tomorrow,” she said. “If you want to see Rome properly.” “You mean illegally.” “I mean my Rome.” He looked up. “And if I don’t come?” Her eyes held his, bright and unreadable. “Then I’ll assume you’re even more boring than I thought.” Ben laughed quietly. “You’re impossible.” “Yes,” she said again, and this time the word sounded like an invitation. A car passed at the end of the alley, its headlights briefly washing over them, and in that moving light Ben realized how close he was standing to her, how little space there had been between humor and something else all day. “Alessandra,” he said, and his voice was gentler than he intended. She waited. He wanted to say many things. That he had been lonely in Rome before she nearly ran him down. That she had made the city feel suddenly dangerous and alive. That he was not accustomed to wanting to be shocked, or chased, or laughed at by a woman who might or might not be a thief. Instead he said, “I don’t usually do this.” Her mouth quirked. “No?” “No.” “Pity.” She stepped back first, but only by inches. Then, with a final glint of mischief, she touched two fingers to the guidebook tucked under his arm and said, “Read less. Live more, Professor.” And then she was on the motorcycle, the engine catching in a low, hungry growl. The colorful scratch on the side flashed once in the dark as she pulled away. Ben stood alone in the alley long after the sound of her bike had faded, receipt in hand, heart behaving like an idiot. Rome, all at once, felt less like a city and more like the beginning of a dare.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates two distinct characters - Alessandra, the fiery Roman motorcycle courier with kleptomaniac tendencies, and Ben, the polite British academic - and develops their chemistry through a series of interconnected scenes across a single day in Rome. The characterization is particularly well-handled. Alessandra emerges as genuinely complex - dangerous yet charming, mischievous yet not malicious. Her kleptomania is woven in subtly (the lighter theft) rather than being heavy-handedly announced. Ben's transformation from flustered academic to willing participant in minor rebellion feels organic and well-paced. The cultural clash between them creates natural tension and humor. The dialogue sparkles with authenticity. The exchanges feel natural while revealing character - Alessandra's quick wit ("Rome loves technically dead people") contrasts effectively with Ben's more formal speech patterns. The banter has genuine chemistry without feeling forced or overly cute. The prose style is confident and polished. Descriptions are vivid without being overwrought ("Rome lay spread beneath them like a secret she was willing to share only in pieces"). The pacing moves well between action sequences and quieter character moments. The author shows restraint, allowing tension to build without rushing to resolution. The Roman setting is well-integrated, feeling authentic rather than touristy. The locations serve the story rather than existing merely as backdrop. The motorcycle journey structure provides natural momentum while allowing for character development. The ending strikes an appropriate note - romantic tension without premature resolution, leaving Ben (and readers) wanting more while providing satisfying closure to the day's events. The final image of Ben alone with the receipt effectively captures his transformation and sets up future possibilities. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly too polished for the raw energy of the characters, and some descriptions that, while beautiful, occasionally slow the pace slightly. However, these are minor quibbles with what is overall a very strong piece of contemporary romance writing. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Last Outpost hums the way a sleeping beast might hum if it had been built out of brushed steel, recycled air, and bad decisions. I’m behind the bar polishing a glass that’s already clean enough to see your future in when the first shift crowd starts thinning out and the second shift crowd starts pretending they didn’t just clock in. The place is cozy in the way only a station can be cozy: low amber lights, the soft clatter of tools in pockets, the warm stink of broth and spice and wet boots, and the steady thrum of the docking ring outside reminding everybody that if they get too attached, gravity and schedule can still take it all away. I like it here. I mean, I like it most days. “Net!” calls Daro from the back table, where he’s holding court with three ore-haulers and a synthetic card deck that’s definitely marked. “You seeing this? Your girl’s cheating again.” “I’m not your girl,” I call back. “Everyone is somebody’s girl,” Daro says, grinning around the rim of his glass. “That’s how romance works.” “That’s how bad poetry works,” I say. The miners laugh. One of them, a broad-shouldered woman with copper dust still in the seams of her hands, lifts her mug in my direction. “Keep the banter going, bartender. It makes the rations taste better.” “I can’t take credit for the rations,” I say. “That’s all Miri.” From the kitchen hatch, Miri yells, “You’re welcome, and tell them I’m not putting more pepper in the stew just because your customers have no taste.” A few groans rise from the room, affectionate and well-practiced. I’m reaching for a fresh towel when the front door slides open with a sigh and a rush of corridor air. The newcomer pauses just inside like they’ve walked through the wrong side of a dream. Frazzled is the first thing I think. Second is: trouble. They’re lean, all sharp angles under a travel cloak that’s seen better centuries. Tentacled dreads hang from their scalp in a wild halo, most of them twisted with tiny metal beads and bits of fiber that look like they were cut off a jacket in a hurry. One sleeve is scorched. Their left hand—three fingers, two thumbs—won’t stop twitching. They scan the room once, fast, then come straight to my bar. “Vega whisky, neat,” they say, voice low and rough. “Better make that a double.” There are a dozen ways to answer that. I settle on, “You’re speaking my language.” I pull down the bottle. “Name?” “Does it matter?” “Usually. I like knowing who I’m pouring for.” A beat. Their eyes flick to the far end of the bar, then back to me. “Sera.” “Just Sera?” “Sera is fine.” “That’s what people say when they’re trying not to tell me anything.” “Then I’m doing a good job.” I snort and pour. The whisky goes into the glass like dark amber fire. I add another measure without asking and slide it over. Sera takes the drink with both hands, as if it might bolt, and downs half of it immediately. Their shoulders do not relax. If anything, they get more tense. I lean an elbow on the bar. “You look like you either outran debt collectors, a spouse, or the law.” “Could be all three.” “Lucky me.” “Depends who’s asking.” “I’m Nets,” I say. “Bartender. Amateur therapist. Professional witness.” That gets the tiniest shift at the corners of their mouth. “Then witness this: I need a quiet corner and maybe a ship that doesn’t ask questions.” “I’m fresh out of ships.” “Pity.” I glance at the corridor cameras out of habit. Old habit. Bad one. My skin does the familiar prickling thing it always does when somebody mentions needing privacy in a place like this. “You in trouble?” I ask. Sera laughs once, but there’s no humor in it. “Yes.” “Law-enforcement trouble, union trouble, family trouble?” “Transport trouble.” That, more than the scorched sleeve, gets my attention. I set down the towel. “Explain.” Before they can answer, Daro’s voice floats over again. “Net! Your dealer just folded.” “I don’t have a dealer.” “Your dealer of cards, you menace.” “Then say that.” Sera’s gaze flicks toward the card table. “Your patrons always like this?” “Only when they’re winning. Which is never.” Another sound cuts through the room then—sharp, bright, and ugly. A glass breaks somewhere in the background, probably near the far booths. The whole bar flinches the way a body does when it hears something it doesn’t like. Heads turn. Someone swears. A chair scrapes. I look over and see a shard-strewn floor near the maintenance pillar, a scatter of clear glitter under the lights. Miri pokes her head out of the kitchen hatch. “Who did that?” “Not me!” comes two voices at once. “I’ll find the culprit later,” she says, already tired. “If anyone bleeds on my floor, I’m charging extra.” The room settles back down, but only a little. Sera has gone very still. I lower my voice. “That mean something to you?” “It might,” they say. “That’s not reassuring.” “No,” Sera says, staring hard at the broken glass, “it isn’t.” I follow their gaze and only then notice the two strangers who just entered behind the initial commotion. One is a broad-shouldered human in a refinery jacket with a stitched patch over one eye. The other is a tall, smooth-faced company type in slate gray, too clean for the Outpost and too careful in how they’re looking around. Neither comes to the bar. Both are watching Sera. I keep my face neutral. “Friends of yours?” “Sadly,” Sera murmurs, and drains the rest of the whisky. The company type speaks first, voice carrying in that polished way that tries to sound calm and turns into threat anyway. “Sera Kett. You’re out of options. Come quietly and we can pretend this remains a civil matter.” The refinery man folds his arms. “Not a civil matter for long if you make us chase you.” A murmur spreads through the room. Daro sits back in his chair and stops smiling. The miners exchange looks that say they don’t know the story, but they know enough to hate the interruption. I keep my hands on the bar. “Outpost policy says no weapons drawn, no threats over drinks, and no snatching someone from my stools without a very good reason.” The company type glances at me like I’m a stain that learned to talk. “This is a customs enforcement matter.” “Then customs can wait until the tab’s paid,” I say. The refinery man takes a step forward. “Lady, step aside.” “Lady,” I echo. “Cute. No.” Sera finally looks at me directly. Their eyes are a pale, stormy gray, exhausted to the edge of breaking. “Don’t get involved.” I bark out a humorless laugh. “You’re sitting at my bar with two people stalking you from the door. I’m involved by proximity alone.” “Net,” Daro calls softly, “you sure about this one?” “No,” I say. “But I’m sure about not letting uniforms ruin my evening.” The company type’s jaw tightens. “Last warning.” I pick up the towel again, folding it slowly as if I have all the time in the universe. “Here’s mine: if you want someone, show me paperwork, and if you’ve got paperwork, show me a warrant signed by someone with better manners than you.” The refinery man’s hand drifts toward his belt. Not a full draw. Not yet. Sera swears under their breath. Then, very quietly, so only I can hear, they say, “If they take me, they’ll take the drive too.” I look at them. “What drive?” But Sera’s already pushed back from the stool, one hand sliding inside the torn lining of their cloak. The company type says, “Don’t.” And in the sudden silence, under the warm hum of the Outpost and the lingering scent of spilled whisky, I realize that whatever Sera brought into my bar tonight isn’t just trouble. It’s valuable. Possibly illegal. And very likely about to make my cozy little station far less cozy.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi setting with approximately 60% dialogue in first-person present tense. The author establishes The Last Outpost as a lived-in space station bar with effective world-building through sensory details and casual interactions. The writing voice is consistent throughout, maintaining Nets' sardonic but protective bartender persona. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - character development, world-building, and plot advancement. Conversations flow organically, from the playful banter with Daro to the tense exchange with the antagonists. The author shows strong command of subtext, particularly in Sera's evasive responses and the escalating tension. Character work is solid across the board. Nets emerges as a compelling protagonist with hints of a mysterious past, displaying both street-smart wariness and protective instincts. Sera is effectively portrayed as desperate and cornered, while the antagonists feel appropriately threatening without being cartoonish. Even minor characters like Daro and Miri have distinct voices. The pacing builds tension effectively, starting with comfortable bar atmosphere and gradually introducing the central conflict. The broken glass serves as an effective transition point, and the revelation about "the drive" provides a strong hook for future chapters. The author wisely avoids resolving the conflict while establishing clear stakes. Prose quality is generally strong, with vivid imagery like "The Last Outpost hums the way a sleeping beast might hum" and effective sensory details throughout. The writing occasionally borders on being overly descriptive but generally maintains good balance. Some minor weaknesses include a few moments where the dialogue exposition feels slightly forced, and certain descriptive passages could be tightened. The setup, while effective, follows somewhat predictable patterns for the genre. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise well-executed piece that successfully establishes an engaging scenario with clear forward momentum. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The saxophone hit me first. Not physically, obviously, unless you count sound as a kind of weather, which in New Orleans it probably is. It slid around the corner and over the street like warm syrup, all sad and shiny, the kind of tune that makes you feel like you’ve already ruined your own life and are now walking toward the final scene of it. I stopped on the sidewalk with my backpack digging into both shoulders and just listened. Around me, the city was doing a million things at once. A woman in sequins yelled into a phone in French or maybe just French-adjacent, a couple tourists in matching bucket hats were arguing with a map, a guy on a bike nearly clipped a trash can and didn’t even look apologetic. Somewhere behind me, a streetcar clanged like it was personally offended by civilization. New Orleans looked like somebody had taken a normal city, shaken it up with glitter and rainwater and old ghosts, and then forgotten to put the lid back on. I should have been scared. I was scared. That was the thing. I was also weirdly, stupidly alive. Three days ago my biggest problem was pretending I didn’t hear my mom and dad whispering about me after I came out. Two days ago my biggest problem was the look on my dad’s face like I had walked into the house holding a live snake. Last night my biggest problem had become: how do I get on a ride going south when you do not, under any circumstances, have a plan past that. Answer: you lie. A lot. To strangers. To yourself. To the universe. And then you get dumped in downtown New Orleans at sunrise like bad luggage. I adjusted the strap of my bag and started walking again because standing still felt like the same thing as getting caught. The heat clung to me even though it was barely morning. My shirt was already sticking to my back. I had one bottle of water, nineteen dollars in crumpled bills, my phone with 8% battery, and the kind of adrenaline that makes everything seem both hilarious and doomed. I checked the map app for maybe the thirtieth time since I got here, even though the little blue dot kept wobbling like it was drunk too. I needed somewhere to sit down. Somewhere not full of cops. Somewhere not full of people who could tell I was a runaway before I even opened my mouth. “Hey.” I flinched so hard I nearly yanked my own backpack off. The guy standing by the curb had a skateboard under one arm and a paper cup in his other hand. He looked about my age, maybe a little older, all sharp elbows and thrift-store confidence. His hair was dark and messy in a way that probably took effort. He had a small silver ring in one eyebrow and a faded bruise on his jaw. “Sorry,” he said, lifting the cup a little. “You dropped this.” I stared at him. Then at the ground. Nothing there. He grinned. “Kidding. You looked like you needed a reason to stop.” “Do I know you?” “Nah.” He took a sip from the cup. “I’m Jules.” I did the stupid thing where I hesitated too long, because every part of me had become a giant exposed nerve over the last two days. “Taylor,” I said finally. “Taylor,” he repeated like he was testing it. “You got a place to be?” “Somewhere with air conditioning and no questions.” “Ambitious.” “I’m serious.” “So am I.” He nodded down the street. “You look roasted. There’s a place on Royal where they don’t throw people out unless they’re bleeding or loudly insane.” “Great. That narrows it down.” He snorted, and it was such a real laugh that I hated how much it loosened something in my chest. I followed him because I’m apparently the kind of idiot who trusts a stranger with a skateboard and a bruise. Or maybe I was just too tired to keep being afraid every second. We walked under balconies draped with ironwork and hanging plants and strings of tiny flags. Music leaked from open doors. People were already drinking outside bars even though it wasn’t even noon. A woman in a purple dress and giant sunglasses passed us and said, “Baby, you look like you fought God and lost.” “Accurate,” I muttered. Jules glanced at me. “You from around here?” “No.” “Vacation?” I let out a short laugh that sounded wrong even to me. “Sure.” He didn’t push. That should have been a relief. Instead it made my stomach twist, because people don’t ask questions when they already know the answer is going to hurt. We cut through a narrow side street where the buildings leaned close and the shade felt like mercy. A line of broken windows reflected the sky in pieces. Somewhere nearby, somebody was frying something that smelled like heaven and regret. “Why are you helping me?” I asked before I could stop myself. Jules kept walking. “Maybe I’m not.” I waited. He glanced over. “Maybe I’m just bored.” “Cool. So I’m your entertainment.” “Don’t make it weird.” “You’re the one leading me into an alley.” “It’s not an alley. It’s a shortcut.” He shrugged. “Also, if I was gonna rob you, I’d have picked a better backpack.” I almost smiled. Almost. Then I noticed the way he kept looking over his shoulder. “Are you being followed?” He answered too quickly. “No.” That was a yes. My pulse kicked up. “By who?” “Doesn’t matter.” “It matters to me.” He stopped walking and looked at me fully then, his face gone careful in an instant. “Taylor, if I wanted to scare you off, I’d have said ‘federal agents’ or ‘my ex.’” I stared at him. “And you still didn’t answer,” I said. His mouth tightened. “I said it doesn’t matter.” Okay. Fine. Great. Stranger with secrets. Obviously. Of course. My life had become an episode of a show I wouldn’t choose to watch. We reached a little courtyard with a fountain that didn’t work and a bench under a tree dropping stuff on us every few seconds. Jules dropped onto the bench like he’d been carrying the whole city in his spine. I stayed standing for a second, then sat at the far end, ready to bolt. He noticed. “You always sit like you’re about to run?” “Only when I’m wise.” He looked at my bag. “You ran from somewhere.” I said nothing. He raised both hands. “Okay, okay. That’s fair.” The silence stretched, but it wasn’t empty. The saxophone from somewhere nearby came back in, soft and low and aching, and suddenly I wanted to cry for no reason I could explain. Or for all of them. For my mom’s face when I told her. For the box of clothes I left behind. For the version of myself who thought maybe being honest would make things better. Instead I stared at my hands. Jules said, quieter, “You hungry?” I laughed once, sharp and ugly. “Are you kidding? I’m everything hungry.” He nodded like that made perfect sense and dug in his pocket, pulling out a crushed granola bar and half a pack of crackers. “Not much, but—” “You carrying emergency snacks on purpose?” “New Orleans is a place with a lot of opinions,” he said. “And a lot of times you need crackers.” I took them before I could overthink it. The crackers were stale. The granola bar tasted like cardboard and sugar. It was still the best thing I’d eaten in twenty-four hours. “Thank you,” I said, mouth full. “Don’t thank me yet.” “Why?” He leaned back, looking at the fountain like it might start working out of spite. “Because you’re either gonna hate this city by tonight, or you’re gonna want to stay.” My stomach dropped a little. “Stay where?” Jules looked at me then, and for a second the mask slipped. He looked younger than before. Tired, too. Like somebody who had already been through a whole separate disaster and had just packed it away for later. “I know a place,” he said. “Not official. Not exactly safe. But safe-ish.” “That is not a thing.” “It is if you’re broke enough.” I should have said no. I should have gotten up and kept moving and trusted nobody and stuck to the plan, whatever the plan was now. But my phone battery was dying. My money was almost gone. I had no idea where I was, and the city was too huge and hot and alive to be fake. And Jules had the look of somebody who understood what it meant to be one bad decision away from the street. “Why are you helping me?” I asked again, softer this time. He looked at me for a long second. Then he said, “Because somebody helped me once.” And because I was tired, and lonely, and stupid, and seventeen in all the ways that matter, I believed him. At least enough to follow him. So I stood up, slung my bag back on, and let the city swallow us both as the saxophone kept playing somewhere behind us, sad and golden and impossible. For the first time since I left Tennessee, I didn’t know if I was running toward danger or away from it. Maybe both. Maybe that was the first real honest thing I’d done in days.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen runaway's first day in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a blend of vulnerability, defensive humor, and raw honesty that feels genuine for a 16-year-old in crisis. The prose style is notably accomplished, with vivid sensory details that bring New Orleans to life without overwhelming the narrative. Descriptions like "sound as a kind of weather" and the city looking like it was "shaken up with glitter and rainwater and old ghosts" are evocative without being purple. The author maintains consistent first-person voice throughout, mixing present and past tense naturally as requested. Character development is nuanced, particularly in Taylor's internal contradictions - simultaneously scared and alive, defensive yet desperate for connection. Jules emerges as more than a plot device, with his own hints of backstory and secrets that feel organic rather than manufactured. The dialogue feels authentic to teenage speech patterns while avoiding clichés. The emotional complexity is well-handled, showing Taylor's conflicted feelings about family, identity, and survival without melodrama. The piece avoids easy answers or unearned optimism, ending on an appropriately uncertain note that matches the character's situation. Structurally, the narrative moves at an appropriate pace, building tension through small revelations and Taylor's growing awareness of potential dangers. The saxophone motif provides effective atmospheric continuity. Minor weaknesses include some slightly overwrought metaphors ("giant exposed nerve") and occasional telling rather than showing in emotional moments. The piece could have pushed deeper into Taylor's specific fears about being trans and vulnerable, though this may be appropriate restraint for the character's defensive nature. The writing demonstrates sophisticated understanding of voice, pacing, and emotional truth while maintaining readability and engagement throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 74.3
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chewy had been found in a charity shop three centuries after the end of the world, which was a complicated achievement for a stuffed koala with a battery box in his back and a voice chip that sounded as though it had been edited by a committee. He sat on the edge of a bunk in the lean-to shelter Midas had built from roofing iron, pallet wood, and the sort of optimism that usually got people buried under collapsed masonry. “Let’s review,” said Midas, holding a chipped cup of something tea-adjacent. “We go out at first light. We find water. We find anything edible that has not, in the intervening years, become a philosophy. We do not become eaten by anything with too many legs. We return before dusk. Simple.” Chewy’s plastic eyes glinted in the gloom. One ear was chewed off, probably by a dog, though in the bad old days of the Collapse even the dogs had been making choices. “Simple,” he said in a voice like a toy sold to children and repurposed by history. “You are aware, my dear Midas, that your plans tend to possess the structural integrity of wet paper?” “I resent that.” “You resent everything. It is one of your more durable hobbies.” Midas sighed. “We need to check the eastern runnels. There might still be a spring beyond the old tramway. And the ridge beyond that—” “The ridge beyond that contains,” said Chewy, “the sort of unkind weather that separates the merely foolish from the late foolish.” Midas ignored this, which was his second-favourite survival tactic after panicking. “And if the rumours are right, there’s some kind of refuge out in the wilds. Safe place. People. Walls. A community.” “At which point,” said Chewy, “they will either welcome us, enslave us, or explain that ‘community’ is a very flexible term in hard times.” “Your faith in humanity is inspiring.” “It is a burden I bear with dignity.” They set out with two water skins, a canvas sack, a crowbar, and one tin of apricots so old that the label had become a thesis on entropy. The world beyond their camp was a patchwork of broken things: half-sunk roads, fields gone feral, pylons standing like the ribs of giant, disappointed animals. The wind moved through the ruins and made a sound like somebody whispering receipts. Midas walked with the determined caution of a man expecting the ground to object. Chewy rode in a sling across his chest, head bobbing with each step, all soft fur and silent menace. By noon they had found not water, not refuge, and not even the faint satisfaction of being ignored by predators. They had found a town. “Ah,” said Chewy. “Civilisation. What remains of it, anyway. Which is mostly shopping trolleys and regret.” The town had once been called Bellweather, according to a cracked sign still hanging on one hinge. Now it was mostly lanes of wind-bent corrugated iron and walls with the plaster peeled off to reveal old adverts for things no one could use, such as broadband and confidence. Midas, peering around a collapsed corner, frowned. “Graffiti.” “Remarkable,” said Chewy. “Someone has left a wall in the wild with writing on it. Human ingenuity knows no shame.” The message had been painted in black, the letters tall and jagged: **THE GREEN HOLLOW TAKES IN THE LOST. FOLLOW THE STARS WITH NO DAWN.** Beneath it, in a different hand: **DON’T.** Midas felt, as he often did in the presence of information, that this was either an excellent sign or the beginning of a bad one. “Hidden refuge,” he said. “See?” Chewy’s button eyes narrowed, which was impressive, given they were button eyes. “Also see: trap. Ambush. Cult. Or someone’s idea of a joke with an unusually strong sense of timing.” “We follow it.” “We do not.” “We absolutely do.” Chewy gave him a long, considering look. “You’re going to do the thing where your curiosity outruns your common sense and both are overtaken by disaster.” “That’s unfair.” “You’ve got a reputation.” Midas tucked the sack tighter under his arm and started after the arrow-shaped smear of paint that led out of town. Chewy made a noise that might once have been a sigh and was now a resigned mechanical whir. The path took them into the hills, where the grass grew in stubborn clumps around the skeletons of old wind turbines. The sky thickened. The light changed. A crow watched them from a dead branch with the hard, knowing stare of a professional witness. Then the first trouble arrived wearing boots. The men stepped out of the scrub with scavenger masks and lengths of pipe. There were three of them, and they had the compact, practical look of people who had discovered that morality was a luxury item. “Hand over the bag,” said the tallest. Midas instinctively clutched it. “No.” This was, as responses went, brave in the way a candle was brave before a gale. The tallest man looked at Chewy. “What’s that?” Chewy activated his speaker with a chirp and said, in a cheerful market-assistant voice, “I am a proprietary comfort companion. Please do not damage me. I contain delicate stuffing and judgments.” The scavengers blinked. Midas blinked too, because this was exactly the sort of thing Chewy did when things were about to become worse. The smallest scavenger took a step closer. The ground under him gave a soft, treacherous crack. A moment later he vanished into a camouflaged pit trap full of old bicycle frames, singing kettle fragments, and what sounded like an offended goat. The other two stared. Midas stared. Chewy stared with great purpose at a point just over their shoulders. “Run,” he said. They ran. Unfortunately, the hill they ran down ended at the edge of a ravine crossed by a suspension bridge that had mostly ceased to suspend. Midas skidded to a halt, arms pinwheeling. Chewy swung in the sling. “Do you know,” said Chewy, “I am beginning to detect a pattern.” Behind them came the scavengers, now motivated by irritation and the desire not to explain the goat noise to their friends. Midas looked at the bridge. He looked at the ravine. He looked at the scavengers. He looked at the sky, as if it might produce paperwork. Then he saw, painted on the far side of the gorge, another symbol: a green circle with a hollow centre, the same as at the graffitied wall. “Well,” he said. “That looks promising.” Chewy’s tone suggested he was less convinced. “It also looks distant.” They crossed because the alternatives were either the scavengers or gravity, and gravity had a known administrative style. Halfway over, the bridge began to sing in the wrong key. Midas did not enjoy this. Chewy did not enjoy this either, but he had the benefit of being made of polyester and bitterness. When the planks began to shear away behind them, Midas lunged, missed, and ended up hanging by one arm over a drop that was, in technical terms, unhelpful. Chewy, being a stuffed koala with a survival instinct that had long since departed and left a forwarding address, twisted in the sling and snapped one paw forward. A little hidden claw—added, apparently, by some long-ago manufacturer who believed in impossible things—caught on a rope strand. They did not so much cross as are forced into the vicinity of the other side by a series of unavoidable disasters. On the far bank, exhausted and bruised, Midas collapsed beside a marker stone half covered in moss. Chewy, swung loose at last, landed in a puddle and declared, “If I am ever required to travel again, I should like to do so as someone else.” A shape emerged from the trees. Midas reached for the crowbar. Then stopped. The shape was a person. Several people, actually, from the way the others appeared behind her among the ferns. All were armed. All looked clean in the way of people who had a functioning water source and the alarming confidence that comes with it. “You followed the message,” said the woman. Midas, who had run out of dignity some miles back, said, “Yes?” She glanced at the ravine, the broken bridge, the scattered scavengers, and the general condition of Midas. “That was not the intended route.” Chewy shook water from one ear. “We noticed.” “Nevertheless,” the woman said, “you’re alive. That’s more than can be said for the last four who came looking for the Hollow.” Midas sat up slowly. “You mean there really is a refuge?” The woman smiled, though not entirely kindly. “There is. We call it the Green Hollow because it sounds kinder than ‘the place with walls and rules and not enough blankets.’” “And the message?” “Warning. Invitation. Test. Depends who’s asking.” Chewy looked at Midas with the expression of a toy that had seen too much and was still under warranty. “I told you,” he said. “You told me everything.” “Yes. And on balance, I was correct.” They were escorted through a stand of trees, down into a sheltered valley where the air changed at once, becoming damper, greener, alive in a way the dead world outside was not. There were gardens patched from salvage, rain barrels, watchfires, and houses made from the bones of old things. Not paradise, then. But somewhere with opinions about the weather. At the gate, Midas allowed himself one tiny breath of relief. Then he realized the water skin had split on the bridge, the apricots were gone, his shirt had torn, and one boot had been filled with mud, gravel, and the remains of an old electrical component. Chewy’s fur was caked in river silt. One of his eyes was hanging by a thread of stitching. His speaker crackled and produced, with perfect timing, a cheerful recorded phrase from the Before Times: “Have a nice day!” Midas laughed. It came out sounding near enough to a sob that no one in the Hollow was rude enough to mention it. The woman at the gate took one look at them and said, “You can stay. If you work.” Midas looked at Chewy. Chewy looked at Midas. “Work,” said Chewy. “Excellent. Nothing says safety like compulsory labour.” “Shut up,” said Midas, but not unkindly. And so they entered the Green Hollow, not as heroes, not as chosen survivors, but as two very battered fools who had followed a warning, survived the consequences, and arrived at refuge by a route that suggested the world itself had been making it up as it went along. Which, to be fair, it had.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, philosophical asides, and ability to find humor in dire circumstances. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both bleak and absurd, with excellent character dynamics between the pragmatic stuffed koala Chewy and the hapless protagonist Midas. The dialogue is particularly strong, with Chewy's sardonic observations and Midas's defensive responses creating genuine comedic moments. Lines like "Your faith in humanity is inspiring" / "It is a burden I bear with dignity" perfectly capture Pratchett's rhythm and wit. The voice remains consistently wry throughout, with clever turns of phrase like "something tea-adjacent" and "the sort of optimism that usually got people buried under collapsed masonry." The plot structure follows the requested format well - deliberation, mission gone sideways, return worse for wear. The escalating disasters (scavengers, pit trap, bridge collapse) feel organic rather than forced, and each complication builds naturally from the characters' choices. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The ending feels somewhat rushed and convenient - the Green Hollow appears exactly when needed, and the acceptance into the community comes too easily after the harrowing journey. While the characters arrive "worse for wear" physically, there's little emotional cost or growth. The story also occasionally veers into overly clever territory, with some descriptions feeling more concerned with wit than clarity. The imagery is generally effective, particularly the post-apocalyptic landscape descriptions, though some metaphors feel strained. The pacing works well overall, though the middle section with the scavengers and bridge sequence could be tighter. The piece succeeds in creating an engaging world and memorable characters while maintaining the requested tone throughout most of its length. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 17 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 9 Unearned Transformations: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
