+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 88.4
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The first thing they taught her on Daran was not the names of things but the distance between them. Distance between ship and dock, between dock and security cordon, between the bright white ribs of the colonial Hall and the older stone beneath it, between what was said and what was meant. The lesson had come wrapped in protocol, in courtesy, in the soft hands of aides who had never once raised their voices. It was all very civilized. It was all, she suspected, intended to keep her from noticing the knife hidden in the sleeve. She noticed it anyway. Envoy Mara Venn had been on the world of Nemea Three for six days before she slipped out alone. The official program had been full: welcome liturgy, inspection of waterworks, a luncheon with the Council of Settlements, a memorial ceremony for the First Landing, and endless hours standing beneath hanging banners while ministers and delegates exchanged remarks in the polished cadences of mutual dependence. The tension, every briefing had said, lay in the differences. Human lineages had diverged here. Some of the colony-born were small-boned and broad-eyed, adapted to thinner air in the upland basins; others were taller, more heat-tolerant, with skin darkened almost copper by generations under stronger suns. There were families whose children matured early, and others whose bodies lengthened the years of childhood; there were districts where scent carried more meaning than speech, and districts where speech had become clipped, musical, almost secretive. The briefings called these “subspecifications,” as if a name could make a living thing manageable. Mara had listened, taken notes, asked careful questions. Then, after the third banquet, she had discovered how easily a political system could hide in plain sight if one kept only to the polished rooms. So on the seventh morning, when the assigned escort was delayed by a mechanical fault and the local liaison was occupied in the atrium with some urgent matter of flags, she took the side stair down from the guest quarter and went out through a service gate into the city. No one stopped her. That was the first surprise. The second was the street itself. Daran’s capital, Aster, had been described to her as a civic center, a planned administrative zone, a place of clean lines and controlled movement. It was that, but only at its top layer. Beyond the government quarter the stone buildings crowded close, shoulder to shoulder, as if protecting one another from the wind. Their walls were pale basalt and dark granite, fitted without visible mortar. Every lintel and alley arch was carved with glyphs: a coiling serpent with a woman’s face; a horse leaping through a ring of stars; a vessel under a sun split into three rays. Old Earth myths, or rather their descendants, traced and re-traced by hands that had never seen Earth. Mara paused before a market awning where dried fruit hung in amber strings. A woman behind the counter looked up. Her pupils were slightly elongated, her cheekbones high and severe. “You are offworld,” the woman said. “I am,” Mara answered. The woman weighed this. “Then you should not wander without a guide.” “I’m told that often.” A faint smile. “Do you buy, or only observe?” Mara bought a handful of dark berries, tart and cold from an underground cache. She ate them while walking, and the market opened around her in layers. There were vendors of fiber cloth, of cultivated fish, of tools made from white ceramic and bone. There were children running messages between stalls, and old men seated in sun pockets discussing the weather with the gravity of diplomats. There were places where people touched foreheads in greeting, and places where no one touched at all. In one lane she saw a public queue not for food or water but for listening: a narrow, domed booth where a clerk stood taking testimony from citizens on a slate of luminous glass. Not once did she see the simple hierarchy her briefings had implied. Not governor and governed. Not upland and lowland. Not one faction pressing another into obedience. The city was full of bargains, of inherited duties, of temporary authority. A brewer’s apprentice argued with a transport foreman. Two women in work boots negotiated the transfer of a room in a co-op block with the same intensity Mara had seen ministers bring to border disputes. Everywhere, the city hummed with overlapping loyalties. Near noon she came to a square where a stone fountain stood dry under a statue of a woman with one hand raised and the other empty. At the base of the pedestal, in a carving half worn away, was a ship and, above it, a spiral that might once have been a galaxy, or a serpent swallowing itself. A boy of perhaps ten stood there with his hands in his pockets. He was smaller than most children she had seen, with wide-set eyes and a ridge of dark hair that lay flat against his skull. “You’re looking at that wrong,” he said. “Am I?” “It’s not a goddess. It’s a captain.” Mara crouched a little, so as not to tower over him. “How can you tell?” He pointed to the feet of the statue. “Boots.” She laughed before she could stop herself. “Then what’s the empty hand for?” “The map,” he said. “She lost the map and found the world anyway.” That, she thought, was a very human thing to carve into stone and call a myth. She would have stayed longer, but the day had shifted around her. The light sharpened. Somewhere a siren sounded and was cut off. Her wrist comm vibrated once: a polite summons with the unmistakable hardness of a command. By the time she returned to the guest compound, her aides were pale with relief. Her chief of staff, Leon Sorel, met her in the vestibule with his mouth compressed into a line. “Where,” he said, in the tone of a man trying not to use the word *disaster*, “have you been?” “Walking.” “In the city.” “Yes.” “Alone.” “Yes.” He shut his eyes for an instant, as if prayer might be useful. “Command has already asked whether you’ve been compromised.” “I’ve been hungry,” Mara said. “Mostly.” That earned her a look of deep offense. “You are not being funny.” “I’m trying not to be frightened.” He softened a fraction at that. “You’ve violated the movement protocol. The compound security logs show you left the authorized route. We are expected to maintain a visible distance from the local population unless escorted.” “Expected by whom?” “Command. And the Minister’s office. And every faction in the city that will interpret your behavior as either insult or intelligence gathering.” “Were I intelligence gathering,” Mara said, “I’d have taken notes.” “You are impossible.” “Yes,” she said, and smiled despite herself. “That has been established.” Command summoned her within the hour. Not by holo, which would have been courteous, but by a living courier whose face bore the expression of one asked to deliver bad weather. Mara stood at attention in the briefing chamber while Colonel Rhyse, the mission commander, listened to her account in silence. When she finished, he said, “You were told not to do that.” “I was told not to do many things.” “Envoy.” She met his gaze. “Colonel.” He sighed, and the sigh was more dangerous than anger. “You’ve put us in a position. They observed you. Not all of them, but enough. They will now assume either that you are careless or that you mean to learn something without asking. Both interpretations are inconvenient.” “Which is the true one?” He studied her, perhaps deciding whether she was insolent or simply young. “The second. And because you’ve already demonstrated poor discipline, you’re to make yourself useful. This afternoon you will call on Minister Halden, Science Portfolio.” Mara blinked. “He’s not a scientist.” “No,” Rhyse said dryly. “He is a minister.” “Then why—” “Because he controls the research bureaus, the environmental allocations, and half the hydroponic licenses on the planet. He also thinks he’s clever. Therefore he will wish to test you. Therefore you will let him.” Mara inclined her head. “And if I test him?” “Try not to be obvious.” Minister Halden received her in a room whose walls were carved stone, not paneling, and whose glyphs were so old they had become part of the architecture. A woman with a fishing spear. A man with a wolf’s head and a crown. A bridge over an abyss. Halden himself was broad-shouldered, silver-haired, and handsome in the way of men who had long ago learned to stand still until others became uneasy. He rose with perfect courtesy and offered tea. “I’ve read your profile,” he said, after the formalities. “Young. Offworld-trained. Idealistic, I gather. Dangerous combination.” “Profiles are often wrong,” Mara said. “Usually on purpose.” He smiled, as if pleased she understood the game. They spoke for half an hour about air recycling, crop failure, migration pressure, the upland clinics, the distribution of school quotas. Halden let her ask questions, then answered by circling away from them. She let him think he was guiding the conversation. Occasionally, very occasionally, she put a small stone in his path. At last he leaned back. “You went into the market this morning.” It was not a question. “Yes.” “Without escort.” “Yes.” “That was foolish.” “Yes.” He waited. “That is all?” “No.” “No?” “It was also educational.” He folded his hands. “And what did you learn, Envoy?” Mara considered the glyphs on the wall: the captain, the wolf, the bridge. Then she looked at him. “That your people do not live as your reports describe,” she said. “That the differences among lineages have not broken the city. They have made it more complicated. You govern a negotiated world, not a divided one.” Halden’s expression did not change, but something in the room tightened. “And,” she continued, “the Ministry of Science is not where science lives. It is where science is translated into power.” For the first time, he laughed. A brief sound. Genuine, perhaps, or close enough to it. “Good,” he said. “You may yet survive this posting.” Mara set down her cup. “Was that the test?” “No.” His eyes were bright and unreadable now. “That was the greeting.” Outside, the stone city was sinking into evening. Its carved myths glowed red-gold in the low light, and the old captain on her pedestal held her empty hand toward the darkening streets as if still asking for the map, or offering one. Mara thought of Command, of the market woman, of the boy who had seen a ship in a statue. She thought of a colony that had become a world by refusing to remain simple. Then she looked back at the minister and understood, with a clarity that chilled her, that the real negotiations had not yet begun.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Ursula K. Le Guin's literary style, successfully capturing her characteristic blend of anthropological observation, political complexity, and understated prose. The author has created a compelling narrative that fulfills the prompt's requirements while maintaining literary quality throughout. The adherence to instructions is excellent. The piece follows a young diplomatic envoy who breaks protocol to explore the city, encounters ordinary citizens and markets that contradict official briefings, faces reprimand from Command, and engages in a challenging conversation with the Minister of Science. The 1000-word length is respected, and the Le Guin style is convincingly emulated. The characterization is particularly strong. Mara Venn emerges as a believable protagonist - curious, intelligent, and willing to take calculated risks. Her actions feel authentic to someone in her position who recognizes the limitations of official channels. The supporting characters, from the market woman to Minister Halden, are efficiently but effectively sketched with distinct voices and motivations. The prose style successfully captures Le Guin's voice - measured, observational, with a focus on cultural and political nuance rather than action. The opening line ("The first thing they taught her on Daran was not the names of things but the distance between them") immediately establishes this tone and demonstrates sophisticated understanding of Le Guin's philosophical approach to science fiction. The world-building is particularly impressive, creating a believable human colony where divergent evolution has created complexity rather than simple division. The description of the city, its markets, and social structures feels authentic and lived-in. The carved glyphs, the dry fountain with its statue, and the various human adaptations all contribute to a rich sense of place. The dialogue is natural and serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing plot, and exploring themes. The conversation between Mara and Minister Halden is particularly well-crafted, showing two intelligent people testing each other while revealing the political complexities of the situation. The piece maintains excellent pacing, moving smoothly from Mara's unauthorized exploration through her confrontation with Command to the final scene with Halden. Each section builds naturally on the previous one, creating a coherent narrative arc. The imagery is vivid without being overwrought. Descriptions like "stone buildings crowded close, shoulder to shoulder, as if protecting one another from the wind" and the various carved glyphs create a strong sense of place without becoming purple prose. The ending strikes an appropriate note - neither artificially positive nor unnecessarily dark. Mara's realization that "the real negotiations had not yet begun" provides a satisfying conclusion while suggesting the complexity of what lies ahead. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly dense with description, and some transitions could be smoother. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 2.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 72.2
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The solar panel rig outside the lab had been built from a truck windshield, copper wire stripped out of dead walls, and three bicycle frames welded into a crude tilt mount. At noon it could coax enough current from the pale winter sun to run the PCR block for twelve minutes. At dusk, if the clouds were thin and the wind didn’t shake the contacts loose, it might manage eight. Tonight the voltage meter on the wall was doing its usual nervous dance, the needle quivering between 9.1 and 11.4 volts as the batteries bled through the inverter. Every flicker made the little incubator fan hiccup. Mara put two fingers to the side of the thermal cycler and felt the machine’s vibration change pitch as the power dipped again. “Come on,” she muttered. “Just one clean run.” The lab answered with the smell of hot dust and old plastic. Around her, the room looked like the skeleton of a university basement had been dragged through a riot and then repurposed by desperate people with no money and too much knowledge. There were laminar hood filters made from furnace fans and furnace filters. A pipette stand carved from an ammunition crate. Half the glassware had been replaced with cut-down IV bags and repurposed insulin syringes. The autoclave was an old pressure cooker with a bicycle brake clamped to the lid to keep the valve from singing too loud and attracting scavengers. On the far bench, a rack of seed vials sat under a strip of bluish salvage LEDs. Each vial held something the corporations had promised would be the future of agriculture and then accidentally engineered into a business model built on rot: crops whose modified traits degraded after one reproductive cycle, every generation requiring a fresh licensed seed pack, fresh patents, fresh dependence. That had seemed elegant when cities still had supermarkets and governments still had audit departments. Now the world was full of ash, fence-line communes, and people who could tell, by taste alone, whether a tomato had been born in a corporate greenhouse or in a stolen field. Mara lifted the latest sample toward the light. The DNA extraction was barely a teaspoon of cloudy liquid from a vine that had survived three weeks longer than it should have. The plant itself had come from the settlement’s south plot, a patch of starving soil where the old genetically enhanced staples had become a gamble. In the first generation they had yielded hard, clean, disease-resistant fruit. In the second, they had thinned out, gone brittle, lost their salt tolerance, and collapsed under mildew. The built-in obsolescence wasn’t a flaw anymore. It was the architecture of collapse. She set the vial down and glanced at the black-market slate beside the centrifuge. Contract open. No signature, only a time window, a payment amount, and a demand written in terse market cant: **DELIVER VIABLE SEED STOCK / FOUR VARIETIES / NO TRACE / NO PATENT-TAIL / BEFORE FULL MOON** Below it: a token address from the River District traders and a warning that any crop carrying corporate watermark sequences would be rejected on sight. Mara had seen people executed over less. The black market no longer trusted labels; it trusted labor. If you said a crop was clean, it had better germinate, breed true, and not poison the children. She leaned back in the lab chair, feeling the ache in her shoulders settle into the joints. Her left hand trembled faintly. Not from fear. From the older work. At the base of her thumb, a transparent patch of skin had become visible years ago after one of her own untested therapies—a gene-edited anti-inflammatory delivery system, homebrewed from scavenged viral vectors and optimism. The patch pulsed faintly when she got stressed, shifting blood flow and holding down the swelling in her wrists. It was not approved. It was not stable. It had, however, let her keep working after the collapse of the city clinics, and once, during the fever winter, it had reduced a septic infection long enough for the herbalists to save her arm. That was the bargain with rogue medicine now: a narrow escape from one disaster in exchange for a new kind of body. Some modifications hurt. Some didn’t. Some gave you advantages you hadn’t planned for. Mara touched the bridge of her nose. Her vision sharpened slightly as the retinal patch under her right eye adjusted to the dim lab light. A gift from a hacked agricultural biosensor, originally meant to detect fungal bloom in greenhouse rows. It had let her read faint electrophoresis bands in near darkness for the last six months. It also made bright sunlight feel like razors. In exchange for seeing too much, she had lost the luxury of comfortable daylight. A hard knock rattled the lab door. Mara went still. Another knock, then the scrape of a boot on concrete. Not the settlement guard rhythm. Too patient. Too controlled. She reached under the bench and put her hand on the taser coil made from a stun gun transformer and copper scavenged from an electric stove. “Who is it?” “Courier,” said a man’s voice through the steel. “River District. I’ve got the counter-offer.” She opened the door a hand’s width. A thin man stood in the hall wearing a rain cloak stitched from tarp fragments. His cheeks were hollow. His gloves were surgical, one finger stained with iodine. He held a sealed packet between thumb and forefinger like it might bite him. “You’re late,” Mara said. “Your settlement’s price moved in the last hour.” “That’s how extortion works.” His mouth twitched. “You’ve done business before.” “Enough to know better.” He produced the packet. “Open it.” Inside was a printed strip of thermal paper and a flash drive wrapped in waxed cloth. Mara skimmed the note. The River District wanted four crop lines fast: a drought bean, a cold-tolerant tuber, a fast-growing greens strain, and a wheat analogue with low nutrient overhead. In return they offered medical supplies, batteries, and a week’s access to their cold room. But there was a second clause. **PAYMENT CONDITIONAL ON BLACK-OUT PROOF.** **NO VIABLE CORPORATE SEQUENCES ON RECOVERED MATERIAL.** **YOU CAN’T BE CLEAN IF YOU’RE STILL USING THEIR GENOME.** Mara laughed once, without humor. “They want a miracle.” “They want ownership,” the courier said. “Not of the seed. Of the evidence.” “Why me?” “You have salvage access. You have a reputation for making dead things live.” “That’s a dangerous compliment.” “It’s the only kind that matters now.” She studied him. The man’s neck bore a faint lattice of scar tissue at the throat, the telltale mark of a gland patch grafted too shallow. Another rogue modification. Probably a clot regulator or synthetic saliva gland. People in the districts had become mosaics of whatever kept them alive: algae symbionts for oxygen debt, fungal immune edits for mold lungs, vascular heat exchangers in the cold zones. Most changes came with side effects. Reduced immune response. Strange food cravings. A tendency to dream in fragments of older genomes. “Who’s paying?” she asked. He shook his head. “You don’t want the answer.” “Then I probably do.” “The broker is called Glass Mercy.” Mara didn’t move. Everyone in the ruins knew the name. A corporate remnant, or perhaps a cartel pretending to be a corporation, depending on which rumor you believed. They bought stolen biology and sold back survival. They also had a habit of tracing everything. Their patents had outlived governments; their enforcement drones had not all been shot down. The courier saw her expression. “Now you understand why the money’s good.” Mara looked back at the thermal cycler, at the dim green status light blinking under unstable current. The machine was still mid-run, amplifying the target fragments from the first crop line. If the power dipped too hard, the sequence would fail, and she’d lose three hours of work. If the contract was real, she could buy replacement primers, fresh filters, and enough electrolyte packs to keep the settlement medic stocked through winter. She could also make enemies she couldn’t outrun. “Why tell me?” she asked. The courier shrugged. “Because the contract says the process has to be witnessed. They want someone to certify that what you make doesn’t carry the old poison. I’m the witness.” “That’s not enough.” He reached into his cloak and drew out a syringe capped in red wax. “Then take this.” She frowned. “Modified hematopoietic stimulant,” he said. “Black-market grade. It’ll keep your marrow from crashing if your edits start fighting your baseline cells.” Mara stared at it. “You know what this does to the liver?” “I know what happens if you don’t take support before a long edit session.” She hated that he was right. Her last round of self-medication had left her with bone-deep fatigue and a week of bruising. The stimulant could help. It could also trigger the unwanted upside she’d learned to live with: faster clotting, sharper healing, a terrible appetite, and three hours of feverish focus in which her hands became steady enough to thread a pipette through a keyhole. She took the syringe. The courier exhaled through his teeth. “So that’s a yes?” Mara looked at the thermal cycler one more time. The solar panel rig outside flickered as a cloud crossed the sun; the lights dipped; the centrifuge gave a sad mechanical whine and then steadied. Her crop line was almost done. If it held, she could breed the first clean stock in a year without corporate watermark markers. If it didn’t, the settlement would be eating boiled bark and salt mushrooms by midwinter. “Tell Glass Mercy,” she said, “I’ll take the contract.” The courier nodded once, as if he’d expected nothing else. “Delivery at dawn. River-side gate.” He turned to go, then paused. “One more thing.” Mara waited. “Whatever you build,” he said quietly, “make sure it can reproduce twice.” Then he was gone, the hall swallowing his footsteps. Mara stared at the closed door, then down at the syringe in her palm. The label had been scratched off. Of course it had. She rolled the cap between her fingers, listening to the cycler’s faint mechanical ticks as the DNA strands cooled and rewrote themselves in the machine’s red-lit chamber. Outside, the solar panel rig stuttered and caught, stuttered and caught, like a heart deciding whether to continue. In the old world, corporations had sold obsolescence as innovation. They had turned the next generation into a subscription model. Now, in the ruins they left behind, Mara had one job left: to make life that didn’t expire on schedule. She inserted the syringe into her thigh and pushed the plunger. The cold hit first, then the heat. Her pulse jumped. The transparent patch on her hand brightened, soft and lunar under the skin. Her vision narrowed to the bench, the samples, the gel trays, the runes of data on the salvaged screen. Her fingers steadied. “Alright,” she whispered to the lab, to the crops, to the dead engineers whose mistakes she was still correcting. “Let’s make something that lasts.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence in the biopunk genre, successfully creating a vivid post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering has collapsed into scavenged survival. The author shows impressive attention to scientific detail and world-building, creating believable jury-rigged equipment and a plausible economic system built around genetic obsolescence. The prose is generally well-crafted with evocative imagery, particularly in descriptions of the makeshift laboratory and the physical modifications characters have undergone. The writing maintains a consistent dystopian tone throughout, and the technical language feels authentic without becoming overly dense. However, the piece suffers from several structural weaknesses. The plot is relatively straightforward - a simple contract negotiation that lacks dramatic tension or meaningful stakes beyond survival. The protagonist Mara, while competently drawn, remains somewhat flat; her motivations are clear but not particularly complex or surprising. The dialogue, while functional, lacks distinctive voice and tends toward exposition. The ending feels somewhat predictable and doesn't deliver on the promise of the black market contract setup. The injection scene provides a minor physical transformation but doesn't fundamentally change the character or situation in meaningful ways. The piece reads more like an extended setup than a complete chapter with its own arc. The world-building, while detailed, occasionally veers toward over-explanation, particularly in the technical descriptions of equipment and genetic modifications. Some passages feel more like technical manuals than narrative prose, though this may be intentional for the genre. The piece succeeds best in its atmospheric elements and scientific plausibility, but falls short in character development and plot momentum. It's competent genre fiction that establishes its world effectively but doesn't transcend its premise.
